name: INDUSTRIAL INSPECTION AND QUALITY CONTROL LAB

topics.title: Measurement of bore diameter using 2-ball method and 4-ball method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPEL507 - INDUSTRIAL INSPECTION AND QUALITY CONTROL LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of bore diameter using 2-ball method and 4-ball method # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Bore Diameter — Formal KTU Definition A **bore** is the internal cylindrical surface of a hole produced by drilling, boring, reaming, or honing. The **bore diameter** is the diameter of the smallest inscribed circle that can be fitted inside the cylindrical cavity at any cross-section. It is one of the most frequently inspected dimensions in the manufacture of cylinders, sleeves, bearing housings, valve bodies, and hydraulic components. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPEL507 — Module 2)** > Conventional bore-measuring instruments such as vernier **inside calipers** and **telescoping gauges** fail to deliver consistent accuracy below **25 mm** diameter. The **2-ball** and **4-ball methods** are *comparative (non-gauging) techniques* that overcome this limitation by using **precision steel balls** of certified diameter and a **micrometer** as the reference standard. ## 1.2 The 2-Ball Method — Intuitive Analogy Imagine you are trying to measure the inside width of a small **drinking glass (≈ 25 mm mouth)**. You cannot fit a ruler inside. However, if you drop two **identical marbles** into the glass and then span a caliper across the two marble tops, the gap between the marbles *amplifies* the bore width into a *measurable external dimension*. The bore diameter is recovered by **adding the marble diameter** back to the measured span. That, in essence, is the **2-ball method**. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-language definition (2-ball method):** > Two precision balls of equal known diameter `d` are placed inside the bore. The balls touch the bore wall on diametrically opposite sides. A micrometer measures the distance `M` between the highest points of the two balls. The bore diameter is calculated as **D = M + d**. ## 1.3 The 4-Ball Method — Intuitive Analogy The 2-ball method has one weakness: any **error in the ball diameter `d` flows directly into the result**. To remove this, we use the **same pair of balls again** — but this time inside a **calibrated setting ring** of known diameter `D_s`. The micrometer is read at both places. Because the *same balls* are used, the unknown `d` cancels out, leaving a much cleaner answer. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-language definition (4-ball method):** > Four precision balls of equal diameter are used. Two balls are inserted into the **unknown bore** (giving reading `M_b`) and the same size pair is inserted into a **setting ring of known diameter D_s** (giving reading `M_s`). The unknown bore diameter is recovered as **D = D_s + (M_b − M_s)**. The ball diameter `d` is **mathematically eliminated**, so its calibration error does not propagate into the result. ## 1.4 Why These Methods Are Used in the Lab > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Range:** 1 mm to 25 mm bore diameter. > - **Accuracy achievable:** ± 0.002 mm to ± 0.005 mm when calibrated balls and a calibrated setting ring are used. > - **Capital cost:** A few balls + a micrometer — far cheaper than a dedicated **bore gauge** or **air-gauge probe**. > - **Application:** Acceptance inspection of small precision bores in instrument bearings, nozzles, fuel-injector sleeves, gauges, etc. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step by Step ### 2.1.1 2-Ball Method 1. **Selection of balls:** Two precision steel balls of *equal certified diameter* `d` are chosen such that `d ≤ D/2`. (In practice, `d` is chosen close to `D/2` so that the balls seat firmly against the bore wall.) 2. **Placement:** The bore is held horizontal; the balls are gently introduced and roll to the **lowest point** of the bore so that both ball centers lie on a *horizontal diameter* of the bore. 3. **Seating:** Each ball touches the cylindrical wall and, by symmetry, the two centers lie on opposite ends of a **horizontal diameter** of the bore. 4. **Measurement:** A micrometer (preferably with **ball-tipped anvils**) is used to span the two highest points of the balls. The reading is `M`. 5. **Calculation:** Bore diameter `D = M + d`. ### 2.1.2 4-Ball Method 1. Use the **same pair of balls** (`d`) for both the unknown bore and the setting ring. 2. Place the two balls in the **unknown bore**, take reading `M_b`. 3. Place the same two balls in the **setting ring of known diameter D_s**, take reading `M_s`. 4. The ball diameter `d` is eliminated algebraically: - From the bore: `M_b = D − d` - From the setting ring: `M_s = D_s − d` - Subtract: `D = D_s + (M_b − M_s)`. > [!TIP] > **The 'Why' behind each step** > - *Why two balls and not one?* — Two contact points define a **chord** of the bore. A chord becomes a true **diameter** when the two contact points lie on opposite sides of the centre, which a single ball cannot guarantee. > - *Why use a setting ring in the 4-ball method?* — It converts the measurement from an *absolute* calculation (which depends on the *certified* ball size) into a *comparative* one (which only depends on the *stability* of the ball size between two readings). ## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry Segment | Typical Use | |---|---| | **Hydraulics & Pneumatics** | Inspection of cylinder bores of shock absorbers, valve spools | | **Automobile** | Fuel-injector sleeves, tappet bores, bearing housings | | **Instrumentation** | Jewelled bearings of watches, gauges, sensor housings | | **Defence** | Nozzle bores of rockets, gun barrels at sub-calibre stages | | **Tool Room** | Verification of small ring gauges and plug gauges | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Unknown bore diameter (2-ball) | D | $D = M + d$ | mm | | 2 | Ball diameter | d | Given/certified | mm | | 3 | Micrometer reading over balls (2-ball) | M | Measured | mm | | 4 | Unknown bore diameter (4-ball) | D | $D = D_s + (M_b - M_s)$ | mm | | 5 | Setting ring diameter (known) | $D_s$ | Calibrated master value | mm | | 6 | Reading in unknown bore (4-ball) | $M_b$ | Measured | mm | | 7 | Reading in setting ring (4-ball) | $M_s$ | Measured | mm | | 8 | Micrometer zero error | $Z$ | $M_{true} = M_{observed} - Z$ | mm | | 9 | Repeatability error | $R$ | $R = M_{max} - M_{min}$ | mm | | 10 | Mean reading | $\bar{M}$ | $\bar{M} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum M_i$ | mm | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign convention:** All readings are taken as **positive magnitudes**. The micrometer reading is the *distance over the balls* (i.e., from the top of one ball to the top of the other). Do **not** confuse this with the *gap* between the balls. ## 2.4 Comparison: 2-Ball vs 4-Ball Method | Feature | 2-Ball Method | 4-Ball Method | |---|---|---| | **Number of balls used** | 2 | 4 (same size, two pairs) | | **Reference required** | Certified ball diameter `d` | Calibrated setting ring $D_s$ | | **Computation** | Direct: $D = M + d$ | Comparative: $D = D_s + (M_b - M_s)$ | | **Effect of ball-diameter error** | Propagates fully to `D` | **Eliminated** (cancels by subtraction) | | **Number of measurements** | 1 (in the bore) | 2 (bore + setting ring) | | **Time required** | Less | Slightly more | | **Best accuracy** | ± 0.005 mm | ± 0.002 mm | | **KTU lab preference** | Demonstration of principle | **Preferred** for high-accuracy reporting | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Geometry of the 2-Ball Method Consider a bore of unknown diameter `D` with centre `O`. Let two precision balls, each of diameter `d` (radius `r = d/2`), be placed inside the bore such that they rest on the cylindrical wall. ``` TOP OF BORE ┌────────────────────────────────┐ ←─ work-piece top surface │ │ │ ●───────● ← ball tops │ │ ( ) ( ) │ ← ball 1, ball 2 │ • O • │ ← ball centres, O = bore centre │ │ └────────────────────────────────┘ ◄─── M ───► ``` ### 3.1.1 Assumptions - The bore is **non-tapered** (plain cylindrical). - The two balls are of **equal diameter** `d`. - The bore axis is **horizontal**, so both balls lie in the same horizontal plane. - The micrometer anvils contact the **highest point** of each ball. ### 3.1.2 Derivation of $D = M + d$ Let `O` be the centre of the bore (radius $R = D/2$). Let $C_1$ and $C_2$ be the centres of ball 1 and ball 2 respectively. **Step 1 — Locate each ball centre.** Because each ball touches the bore wall, the distance from each ball centre to the bore centre `O` is $R - r = (D - d)/2$. **Step 2 — Place the balls on a horizontal diameter.** The two balls rest on diametrically opposite sides of the bore wall. Hence the line $C_1C_2$ passes through `O`. **Step 3 — Compute the distance between ball centres.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_1C_2 \;&=\; OC_1 + OC_2 \\ C_1C_2 \;&=\; \frac{D-d}{2} + \frac{D-d}{2} \\ C_1C_2 \;&=\; D - d \end{aligned} $$ The line $C_1C_2$ is **horizontal**, because the bore axis is horizontal and the balls rest at the same gravitational potential (lowest point of the bore). **Step 4 — Locate the top of each ball.** The top of ball 1 is at a vertical distance `r = d/2` above $C_1$. The top of ball 2 is at a vertical distance `r = d/2` above $C_2$. Since $C_1$ and $C_2$ are at the same height, the two ball tops are also at the same height. **Step 5 — Identify what the micrometer measures.** The micrometer reads the **horizontal distance** between the two ball tops. Because both ball tops are at the same vertical level, this horizontal distance equals the horizontal distance between $C_1$ and $C_2$: $$ M \;=\; C_1C_2 \;=\; D - d $$ **Step 6 — Solve for D.** $$ \boxed{\,D \;=\; M + d\,} $$ --- ## 3.2 Geometry of the 4-Ball Method The 4-ball method repeats the 2-ball measurement, first in the **unknown bore** and then in a **calibrated setting ring** of known diameter $D_s$. The same pair of balls is used in both cases, so the unknown ball diameter `d` cancels. ### 3.2.1 Derivation of $D = D_s + (M_b - M_s)$ **Step 1 — Apply the 2-ball formula to the unknown bore.** $$ M_b \;=\; D - d \;\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;\; D = M_b + d $$ **Step 2 — Apply the 2-ball formula to the setting ring (which is a known cylindrical ring of diameter $D_s$).** $$ M_s \;=\; D_s - d \;\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;\; d = D_s - M_s $$ **Step 3 — Substitute `d` from Step 2 into Step 1.** $$ \begin{aligned} D \;&=\; M_b + d \\ D \;&=\; M_b + (D_s - M_s) \\ D \;&=\; D_s + M_b - M_s \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Final result.** $$ \boxed{\,D \;=\; D_s + (M_b - M_s)\,} $$ The ball diameter `d` **no longer appears** in the working formula. This is the *principal advantage* of the 4-ball method — the result is independent of the *absolute* value of `d` and depends only on the *stability* of `d` between the two readings. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-style Problem) > **Problem statement:** > The diameter of a small bore is measured by the 4-ball method. The micrometer reading across the balls placed inside the unknown bore is $M_b = 27.42$ mm. The same pair of balls is then placed in a calibrated setting ring of diameter $D_s = 25.00$ mm, and the reading is $M_s = 22.43$ mm. Calculate the unknown bore diameter `D` and the equivalent ball diameter `d` that was used. ### 3.3.1 Solution — Step by Step **Step 1 — Compute `d` from the setting-ring data.** $$ \begin{aligned} M_s \;&=\; D_s - d \\ 22.43 \;&=\; 25.00 - d \\ d \;&=\; 25.00 - 22.43 \\ d \;&=\; 2.57 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute `D` using the recovered `d`.** $$ \begin{aligned} D \;&=\; M_b + d \\ D \;&=\; 27.42 + 2.57 \\ D \;&=\; 29.99 \text{ mm} \;\;\approx\; 30.00 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Cross-check using the direct 4-ball formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} D \;&=\; D_s + (M_b - M_s) \\ D \;&=\; 25.00 + (27.42 - 22.43) \\ D \;&=\; 25.00 + 4.99 \\ D \;&=\; 29.99 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ Both methods agree — confirming the calculation. **Step 4 — Report the answer with units and significant figures.** $$ D \;\approx\; 30.00 \text{ mm},\;\;\; d = 2.57 \text{ mm} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Trick Question Variant** > A common variant asks: *“If the bore measurement $M_b$ is taken with a micrometer that has a zero error of +0.02 mm, what is the corrected bore diameter?”* > Always correct the micrometer reading **first**: $M_{b,corr} = M_{b,obs} - Z$, and **then** plug into the formula. --- ## 3.4 Propagation of Errors (Analytical Matrix) | Source of Error | 2-Ball Method | 4-Ball Method | |---|---|---| | Ball-diameter error $\Delta d$ | $D_{err} = \Delta d$ (propagates **fully**) | **Cancelled** by subtraction | | Micrometer error $\Delta M$ | $D_{err} = \Delta M$ | $D_{err} = \sqrt{(\Delta M_b)^2 + (\Delta M_s)^2}$ | | Setting-ring error $\Delta D_s$ | Not applicable | $D_{err} = \Delta D_s$ | | **Combined worst-case** | $\Delta D = \Delta M + \Delta d$ | $\Delta D = \Delta D_s + \sqrt{2}\;\Delta M$ | Because $\Delta d$ is **eliminated** in the 4-ball method, the total uncertainty is usually smaller — provided the setting ring is itself well calibrated. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — 2-Ball Method ```mermaid graph TD A[Bore of Unknown Diameter D] --> B[Insert Two Precision Balls of Diameter d] B --> C[Balls Seat on Opposite Walls of Bore] C --> D[Place Micrometer Across Top of Balls] D --> E[Record Micrometer Reading M] E --> F[Apply Formula D = M + d] F --> G[Report Bore Diameter D] style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — 4-Ball Method ```mermaid graph TD A[Unknown Bore of Diameter D] --> B[Insert Pair of Balls of Diameter d into Unknown Bore] B --> C[Measure Reading Mb Using Micrometer] C --> D[Insert Same Pair of Balls into Calibrated Setting Ring of Diameter Ds] D --> E[Measure Reading Ms Using Micrometer] E --> F[Apply Formula D = Ds + Mb minus Ms] F --> G[Report Bore Diameter D Independent of d] style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Decision Flow — Which Method to Use? ```mermaid graph TD Start([Start: Bore Diameter to Measure]) --> Q1{Is high
accuracy
required?} Q1 -- No --> TwoBall[Use 2-Ball Method
D = M + d] Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is calibrated
setting ring
available?} Q2 -- Yes --> FourBall[Use 4-Ball Method
D = Ds + Mb - Ms] Q2 -- No --> Cert[Use 2-Ball Method
with Certified Balls
D = M + d] TwoBall --> Result([Report D]) FourBall --> Result Cert --> Result style Start fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px style TwoBall fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px style FourBall fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px style Cert fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px style Result fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic (ASCII Representation) ### 4.4.1 2-Ball Method — Cross-Sectional View ``` WORK-PIECE TOP SURFACE ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐ │ ↑↑ ↑↑ │ ↑↑ = micrometer anvil contact │ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ │ ╱ ╲ = ball profile │ ( ● ) ( ● ) │ ● = ball centre │ ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ │ │ •──────────────────• ←── d ──→ │ d = ball diameter │ O ← bore centre │ │ │ │ ◄─────────── M ────────────► │ M = micrometer reading │ │ (top-to-top of balls) └──────────────────────────────────────┘ WORK-PIECE BOTTOM Geometry: M = D − d D = M + d ``` ### 4.4.2 4-Ball Method — Comparative Cross-Section ``` UNKNOWN BORE CALIBRATED SETTING RING ┌──────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ ↑↑ ↑↑ │ │ ↑↑ ↑↑ │ │ (●) (●) │ │ (●) (●) │ │ •───────• │ │ •───────• │ │ O │ │ O │ │ ◄─M_b─► │ │ ◄─M_s─► │ │ (unknown D) │ │ (known D_s) │ └──────────────────┘ └──────────────────┘ │ │ └────────────┬───────────────────────┘ ▼ D = D_s + (M_b − M_s) ``` > [!NOTE] > The ASCII diagrams above are *not* drawn to scale. They are intended only to label the relevant geometric quantities. In the lab record, students are expected to redraw them **to scale** with proper dimensioning. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **Q: State the principle of the 2-ball method for measuring the bore diameter of a small precision component. Mention the working formula.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The 2-ball method uses two precision steel balls of equal, certified diameter `d` placed inside the bore. The balls rest on diametrically opposite sides of the cylindrical wall. A micrometer measures the distance `M` between the highest points of the two balls. The bore diameter is calculated using the formula $D = M + d$. This method is preferred for bore diameters below 25 mm where conventional internal measuring instruments are inaccurate. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` **Q: What is the key advantage of the 4-ball method over the 2-ball method of bore measurement?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The 4-ball method uses the same pair of balls in both the unknown bore and a calibrated setting ring. The ball diameter `d` is mathematically eliminated, and the result depends only on the calibrated setting-ring diameter $D_s$ and the difference in micrometer readings. Consequently, any error in the certified ball diameter does **not** propagate into the final result, giving higher accuracy than the 2-ball method. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question B1 — **Choice A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **2-ball method** for measuring the bore diameter of a precision sleeve. Derive the working formula. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a 2-ball method experiment, two balls of diameter $d = 3.00$ mm are placed inside a bore. The micrometer reading over the balls is $M = 22.05$ mm. Calculate the bore diameter. If the same bore is re-measured with balls of diameter $d = 2.50$ mm, what is the new micrometer reading (assume the bore diameter is unchanged)? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] 1. **Sketch** (neat labelled cross-section showing bore, two balls, micrometer) — 2 marks 2. **Procedure** — 2 marks 3. **Derivation** of $D = M + d$ — 3 marks *Sketch description (2 marks):* Show the cross-section of a horizontal bore with two balls of diameter `d` touching the cylindrical wall on opposite sides. Indicate the ball centres lying on a horizontal diameter of the bore. The micrometer is shown above the work-piece, with its anvils contacting the highest points of the two balls. Label the micrometer reading `M` and the bore diameter `D`. *Procedure (2 marks):* - Step 1: Select two precision steel balls of equal certified diameter `d` (where $d \le D/2$). - Step 2: Hold the work-piece with the bore axis horizontal and insert the balls; they roll to the lowest point of the bore. - Step 3: Span the highest points of the two balls with a micrometer and record `M`. - Step 4: Repeat 3–4 times to obtain a mean reading. *Derivation (3 marks):* $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let the bore centre be } O \text{ and bore radius be } R = D/2.\\ \text{Ball radius } r = d/2.\\ \text{Distance of each ball centre from } O \text{ is } R - r = (D-d)/2.\\ \text{The two ball centres lie on a horizontal diameter of the bore.}\\ \text{Therefore, the distance between the ball centres:}\\ C_1C_2 \;&=\; (D-d)/2 + (D-d)/2 \;=\; D - d.\\ \text{Both ball tops are at the same height (r above the line of centres).}\\ \text{Hence, the micrometer reading } M = C_1C_2 = D - d.\\ \therefore \quad D \;&=\; M + d. \end{aligned} $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** $d = 3.00$ mm, $M = 22.05$ mm. **Step 1 — Bore diameter using 2-ball formula (1 mark):** $$ D = M + d = 22.05 + 3.00 = 25.05 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 2 — Bore diameter assumed unchanged (1 mark):** $D = 25.05$ mm. **Step 3 — New micrometer reading with $d = 2.50$ mm (5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{new} \;&=\; D - d_{new} \\ M_{new} \;&=\; 25.05 - 2.50 \\ M_{new} \;&=\; 22.55 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $D = 25.05$ mm, $M_{new} = 22.55$ mm. --- ### Question B1 — **Choice B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` **(a)** Explain the **4-ball method** of bore-diameter measurement with a neat diagram. Derive the working formula and state **two advantages** over the 2-ball method. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a 4-ball method experiment, the micrometer reading across the balls placed in the unknown bore is $M_b = 28.74$ mm. The same pair of balls gives a reading of $M_s = 23.76$ mm when placed in a calibrated setting ring of diameter $D_s = 25.00$ mm. Calculate **(i)** the ball diameter `d` and **(ii)** the unknown bore diameter `D`. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] 1. **Sketch and procedure** — 2 marks 2. **Derivation** of $D = D_s + (M_b - M_s)$ — 3 marks 3. **Two advantages** — 2 marks *Sketch and procedure (2 marks):* - Draw the unknown bore with two balls of diameter `d` and the calibrated setting ring (of known diameter $D_s$) with the same two balls. - Mark the two micrometer readings $M_b$ (in the unknown bore) and $M_s$ (in the setting ring). - Procedure: insert balls in bore, record $M_b$; insert the same balls in the setting ring, record $M_s$; compute $D$ from the formula. *Derivation (3 marks):* $$ \begin{aligned} \text{For the unknown bore: } M_b = D - d \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; D = M_b + d.\\ \text{For the setting ring: } M_s = D_s - d \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; d = D_s - M_s.\\ \text{Substituting:}\\ D \;&=\; M_b + (D_s - M_s)\\ D \;&=\; D_s + (M_b - M_s). \end{aligned} $$ *Two advantages (2 marks — 1 mark each):* - **Advantage 1:** The ball diameter `d` is **mathematically eliminated**, so any error in the certified value of `d` does not affect the final result. This improves accuracy. - **Advantage 2:** The method is essentially a **comparison technique** with a calibrated setting ring as the reference, so it is self-checking and provides traceability to a national standard. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** $M_b = 28.74$ mm, $M_s = 23.76$ mm, $D_s = 25.00$ mm. **(i) Ball diameter `d` (3 marks):** From $M_s = D_s - d$: $$ \begin{aligned} d \;&=\; D_s - M_s \\ d \;&=\; 25.00 - 23.76 \\ d \;&=\; 1.24 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Unknown bore diameter `D` (4 marks):** Using $D = D_s + (M_b - M_s)$: $$ \begin{aligned} D \;&=\; 25.00 + (28.74 - 23.76) \\ D \;&=\; 25.00 + 4.98 \\ D \;&=\; 29.98 \text{ mm} \;\;\approx\; 30.00 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Cross-check using $D = M_b + d$:** $$ D = 28.74 + 1.24 = 29.98 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark $$ **Final Answer:** $d = 1.24$ mm, $D = 29.98$ mm. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > - **Forgetting to add `d`:** Many students write $D = M - d$ instead of $D = M + d$. Remember, `M` is the **external** span across the balls; the **internal** bore is **larger** by the ball diameter. Always **add**. > - **Sign error in 4-ball method:** $D = D_s + (M_b - M_s)$ — note the **plus** between $D_s$ and the difference, not minus. $D_s$ is the reference (positive) and the difference can be positive or negative depending on whether the unknown bore is larger or smaller than the setting ring. > - **Mixing up the bore and the setting ring readings:** If a student accidentally swaps $M_b$ and $M_s$, the error is exactly $2(M_b - M_s)$, which is large. Always label clearly. > - **Ignoring zero error of the micrometer:** The micrometer's zero error $Z$ must be **subtracted** from the observed reading **before** plugging into the formula: $M_{true} = M_{obs} - Z$. > - **Reporting without units:** KTU examiners specifically deduct marks if the final answer does not carry the unit (mm). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (memorise for the lab exam viva and written test)** > > 1. **2-ball formula:** $D = M + d$. Always **add** the ball diameter. > 2. **4-ball formula:** $D = D_s + (M_b - M_s)$. Ball diameter cancels out. > 3. **Key assumption:** The bore is **plain (non-tapered) and horizontal** during measurement. > 4. **Ball selection rule:** Choose balls such that $d \le D/2$. If $d$ is too large, the balls will not enter the bore; if $d$ is too small, they may roll unpredictably. > 5. **Best use:** Bore diameters in the range **1 mm to 25 mm**. > 6. **Achievable accuracy:** ± 0.002 mm (4-ball) vs ± 0.005 mm (2-ball). > 7. **Equipment needed:** Precision steel balls, calibrated setting ring, micrometer with ball-tipped anvils (or flat anvils), clean cloth, magnifying glass. > 8. **Pre-experiment checks:** Clean the bore and balls; verify the micrometer zero; inspect balls for dents, rust, or geometry defects. > 9. **Number of readings:** Take a **minimum of three** repeated readings at the same location and report the mean; take readings at **two or more axial positions** along the bore to detect taper, bell-mouth, or barrelling. > 10. **Source-of-error categories:** Instrumental (micrometer, balls, setting ring), Environmental (temperature), Human (parallax, inconsistent ball placement), and Work-piece (surface finish, burrs, ovality). > 11. **Report format:** Title, aim, apparatus, theory (with formulae), procedure, observation table, sample calculation, result, sources of error, and precautions. > 12. **Common variants in viva:** "Why two balls instead of one?" (to define a *chord* of the bore), "Why use a setting ring?" (to cancel `d` and provide traceability), "What if the bore is tapered?" (2-ball formula fails; use the 2-ball *taper* method with a fixed separation).

topics.title: Measurement of gear tooth profile using gear tooth vernier
topics.content:

# Module Experiment 2 — Gear Tooth Vernier

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Gear Tooth Vernier (Gear Tooth Caliper)** is a precision linear measuring instrument specifically designed to measure the **chordal thickness** of gear teeth at the pitch circle and the **chordal addendum** (distance from the tooth tip to the chord). It employs the **principle of the vernier scale** and uses **two perpendicular vernier systems** — one vertical (height) and one horizontal (thickness) — to enable direct readout of tooth geometry on involute spur and helical gears in a single setup.

The instrument is governed by **IS 4074 / ISO 1328** standards for gear measurement accuracy classes and is the most common shop-floor tool for **qualitative inspection of gear tooth wear, profile deviation, and thickness variation** without removing the gear from its assembly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of it like measuring the "belt" across a mountain peak.**
> Imagine each gear tooth as a small mountain. A gear tooth vernier does NOT measure the curved surface length around the mountain (that would be the circular tooth thickness measured along the pitch circle). Instead, it lays a **straight imaginary "belt"** (chord) across the tooth at the pitch circle level and measures two things:
> 1. **How wide is the belt?** → chordal thickness (W)
> 2. **How high is the mountain peak above the belt?** → chordal addendum (hₐ)
>
> By comparing these measured values against the theoretically calculated values, an inspector instantly knows whether the tooth has worn down, grown, or been manufactured incorrectly — just as you could tell if a mountain peak had eroded by comparing it to its original height.

### 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Notation |
|---|---|
| Standard pressure angle (α) | **20°** (most common), 14.5°, 25° |
| Circular tooth thickness at PC | **t = πm / 2** (for standard gears) |
| Standard addendum | **m** (where m = module in mm) |
| Least Count (typical) | **0.02 mm** (50 vernier div on 49 mm) |
| Instrument standard | **IS 4074**, conforms to AGMA 2000 |
| Measurement principle | **Mechanical / Direct linear** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** In KTU lab exams, the examiner almost always assumes a **standard 20° full-depth involute spur gear** unless stated otherwise. Memorize the simplified chordal formulas for 20°: **W ≈ 1.4760 m** and **hₐ ≈ 1.2687 m**.

### 1.4 Why Use Chordal Thickness Instead of Circular Thickness?

| Aspect | Circular Thickness (t) | Chordal Thickness (W) |
|---|---|---|
| Measured along | Arc of pitch circle | Straight line between flanks |
| Direct measurement? | **Not possible** with simple caliper | **Easily measurable** with caliper jaws |
| Practical use | Theoretical only | **Shop-floor inspection** |
| Tangential errors amplified? | Yes (involute curvature) | No (straight-line geometry) |

This is precisely why the gear tooth vernier uses chordal dimensions — they are **linear and directly accessible**, while circular dimensions require complex setups like floating-carriage measuring machines.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Construction of a Standard Gear Tooth

Before deriving chordal formulas, let's anchor the geometry:

- **Module (m):** m = D / Z, where D = pitch circle diameter, Z = number of teeth.
- **Pitch circle (PC):** Reference circle of diameter D = m·Z where tooth thickness equals space width (each = πm/2 for standard gears).
- **Addendum circle:** Circle of diameter D + 2m (tooth tips lie on this).
- **Dedendum circle:** Circle of diameter D − 2.25m (root clearance).
- **Pressure angle (α):** Angle between line of action and common tangent at pitch point. For standard gears: **α = 20°**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Chordal Dimensioning

The chordal thickness is measured across the tooth, but the chord must pass through the **mid-point of the circular tooth thickness arc at the pitch circle**, NOT the tooth tip. The vertical scale measures from the **top of the tooth down to the pitch chord**.

**Logical steps for inspector:**
1. Identify module (m) and pressure angle (α) of the gear.
2. Compute theoretical **chordal thickness W**.
3. Compute theoretical **chordal addendum hₐ**.
4. Preset vertical scale to **hₐ**.
5. Preset horizontal scale to **W**.
6. Place vernier on tooth — if it fits perfectly with flanks touching jaws and tip touching the vertical jaw, the gear is geometrically accurate.
7. Read **deviation (ΔW, Δhₐ)** to quantify error.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-ready** for both theory and numerical problems. Do NOT confuse chordal thickness (chord) with circular thickness (arc).

| S.No | Parameter | Formula | Numerical Coeff. for α = 20° | Units |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Circular tooth thickness | $t = \dfrac{\pi \cdot m}{2}$ | 1.5708 m | mm |
| 2 | Chordal tooth thickness | $W = t \cdot \cos(\alpha) = \dfrac{\pi m}{2}\cos(\alpha)$ | **1.4760 m** | mm |
| 3 | Half-angle subtended at PC | $\beta = \dfrac{\pi}{2Z}$ | depends on Z | rad |
| 4 | Chordal addendum | $h_a = m + \dfrac{W}{2}\tan(\alpha)$ | **1.2687 m** | mm |
| 5 | Tooth thickness over pins (odd Z) | $M = \dfrac{\pi \cdot m \cdot Z \cdot \cos(\alpha)}{2} + d_p \cdot \left[1 + \dfrac{Z(1-\cos\alpha)}{\pi}\right]$ | — | mm |
| 6 | Least Count | $\text{LC} = \dfrac{\text{MSD} - \text{VSD}}{\text{No. of VSD}}$ | 0.02 mm (typical) | mm |
| 7 | Tooth thickness error | $\Delta W = W_{\text{measured}} - W_{\text{theoretical}}$ | — | mm |
| 8 | Profile deviation allowance | per **ISO 1328 / AGMA 2000** | — | μm |

### 2.4 Pressure Angle Comparison Table

| Pressure Angle α | cos(α) | sin(α) | tan(α) | W (= πm/2 · cos α) | hₐ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 14.5° | 0.96815 | 0.25038 | 0.25862 | **1.5208 m** | 1.1977 m |
| **20° (Standard)** | **0.93969** | **0.34202** | **0.36397** | **1.4760 m** | **1.2687 m** |
| 25° | 0.90631 | 0.42262 | 0.46631 | 1.4236 m | 1.3324 m |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Gearbox Manufacturing:** Every automobile gearbox manufacturer (Tata, Maruti, Bosch) inspects gears with this instrument at **100% inspection** on the production line.
- **Maintenance:** Aircraft, locomotive, and wind-turbine gearboxes are checked periodically for tooth wear — chordal thickness reduction is a direct indicator of remaining service life.
- **Quality Acceptance:** In IS 4074 and AGMA standards, tooth thickness deviation determines the **quality grade** of the gear.
- **Reverse Engineering:** Used to estimate wear in field-failed gears to compute the **original tooth thickness** for failure analysis.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Chordal Tooth Thickness (W)

### Step 1 — Start with Circular Tooth Thickness

For a standard full-depth involute spur gear, the pitch circle is the circle where **tooth thickness equals space width**, and together they make up the full circumference:

$$
\pi D = Z \cdot t \implies t = \dfrac{\pi D}{Z}
$$

Substituting D = mZ:

$$
t = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (mZ)}{Z} = \pi m / 2
$$

### Step 2 — Geometric Projection onto Chord

Consider the tooth at the pitch circle. The circular thickness arc subtends a **half-angle** at the gear centre:

$$
\beta = \dfrac{t/2}{D/2} = \dfrac{t}{D} = \dfrac{\pi}{2Z}
$$

Now, the chord connecting the two endpoints of this arc is the **chordal thickness W**. By the geometry of a circle, if a chord of length W subtends an angle 2β at the centre, then:

$$
W = 2 \cdot (D/2) \cdot \sin(\beta) = D \sin(\beta)
$$

But since β is small (typically 1°–10° for real gears), we can apply the small-angle equivalence and pressure-angle correction:

$$
\sin(\beta) \approx \beta \cdot \cos(\alpha)
$$

Where the factor **cos(α)** arises because the involute profile's normal at the pitch circle is inclined at pressure angle α to the radial line, so the chord perpendicular distance is reduced by cos(α).

### Step 3 — Final Closed-Form Expression

$$
W = \dfrac{\pi D}{Z} \cdot \cos(\alpha) = \dfrac{\pi (mZ)}{Z} \cdot \cos(\alpha) = \dfrac{\pi m}{2} \cos(\alpha)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{W = \dfrac{\pi m}{2} \cos(\alpha)}
$$

For **α = 20°**, substituting numerical values:

$$
W = \dfrac{3.1416 \times m}{2} \times 0.9397 = 1.5708 \times 0.9397 \times m = 1.4760 \, m
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Chordal Addendum (hₐ)

### Step 1 — Identify the Two Component Distances

The chordal addendum is measured from the **top of the tooth** (on the addendum circle) down to the **chord line** at the pitch circle. This distance has two additive components:

- **Component A:** Addendum height from pitch circle to tooth tip = **m**
- **Component B:** Vertical drop from pitch circle midpoint down to the chord = depends on geometry

### Step 2 — Derive Component B Geometrically

At the midpoint of the chord, the chord lies at a vertical distance **e** below the pitch circle (since the involute flanks are bowed outward). For a chord subtending half-angle β at the centre:

$$
e = \dfrac{D}{2} \cdot (1 - \cos\beta)
$$

But the chord is measured perpendicular to the radial line of the **flank normal**, which introduces the pressure angle α. The component perpendicular to the chord (i.e., the vertical drop along the centre line) is:

$$
e = \dfrac{W}{2} \tan(\alpha)
$$

### Step 3 — Assemble the Full Expression

$$
h_a = m + \dfrac{W}{2} \tan(\alpha)
$$

Substituting $W = \dfrac{\pi m}{2} \cos(\alpha)$:

$$
h_a = m + \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\pi m}{2} \cos(\alpha) \cdot \tan(\alpha) = m + \dfrac{\pi m}{4} \sin(\alpha)
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Value for α = 20°

$$
h_a = m + \dfrac{3.1416 \times m}{4} \times 0.3420
$$

$$
h_a = m + 0.7854 \times 0.3420 \times m = m + 0.2687 \cdot m = 1.2687 \, m
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{h_a = 1.2687 \, m \text{ (for } \alpha = 20°\text{)}}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU style):** A standard 20° full-depth spur gear has module m = 4 mm and Z = 24 teeth. Calculate (a) the chordal tooth thickness, (b) the chordal addendum. If a gear tooth vernier gives a measured chordal thickness of 5.88 mm on one tooth, determine the tooth thickness error and the percentage error.

### Part (a): Chordal Tooth Thickness

$$
W = 1.4760 \times m = 1.4760 \times 4 = 5.904 \, \text{mm}
$$

### Part (b): Chordal Addendum

$$
h_a = 1.2687 \times m = 1.2687 \times 4 = 5.0748 \approx 5.075 \, \text{mm}
$$

### Part (c): Tooth Thickness Error

$$
\Delta W = W_{\text{measured}} - W_{\text{theoretical}} = 5.88 - 5.904 = -0.024 \, \text{mm}
$$

### Part (d): Percentage Error

$$
\% \text{Error} = \dfrac{\Delta W}{W_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{-0.024}{5.904} \times 100 = -0.4066 \, \%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates the tooth is **under-thick** (worn or undersized). For an ISO Grade 8 gear with m = 4, allowable tooth thickness deviation is typically ±25 to ±40 μm, so −24 μm is **within tolerance** but at the upper edge of caution.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity to Input Parameters

| Parameter Variation | Effect on W | Effect on hₐ | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Module (m) by 10% | ↑ W by 10% | ↑ hₐ by 10% | Coarser teeth are easier to inspect |
| ↑ Pressure angle (α) | ↓ W slightly | ↑ hₐ significantly | 25° gears are "fatter" at the tip |
| ↑ Number of teeth (Z) | No effect (W depends only on m, α) | No effect | Z affects chord curvature, not W |
| ↓ Tooth thickness due to wear | ↓ W directly | ↑ hₐ (since material lost from tip) | Both readings shift together — useful as wear indicator |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of a Gear Tooth Vernier

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the functional architecture of the instrument, illustrating how the operator interacts with the gear and the dual-scale measurement system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Operator"] -->|"Positions jaws on tooth flank"| node2["Vertical Vernier Assembly"]
    node1 -->|"Positions jaws on tooth tip"| node3["Horizontal Vernier Assembly"]
    node2 -->|"Reads h_a in mm"| node4["Main Scale 0 to 25 mm"]
    node2 -->|"Vernier 50 div"| node5["LC = 0.02 mm"]
    node3 -->|"Reads W in mm"| node6["Main Scale 0 to 30 mm"]
    node3 -->|"Vernier 50 div"| node7["LC = 0.02 mm"]
    node4 --> node8["Chordal Addendum Reading"]
    node5 --> node8
    node6 --> node9["Chordal Thickness Reading"]
    node7 --> node9
    node8 --> node10["Compare with Theoretical h_a = 1.2687 m"]
    node9 --> node11["Compare with Theoretical W = 1.4760 m"]
    node10 --> node12["Quality Decision Accept or Reject"]
    node11 --> node12
    node2 --> node2a["Jaws Contact Flank Profile"]
    node3 --> node3a["Jaws Contact Tooth Tip"]
    node2a --> node2
    node3a --> node3
```

## 4.2 Measurement Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Start: Identify gear specs m, Z, alpha"] --> stepB["Compute W and h_a"]
    stepB --> stepC["Preset vertical scale to h_a"]
    stepC --> stepD["Preset horizontal scale to W"]
    stepD --> stepE["Place vernier on tooth"]
    stepE --> stepF{"Vertical jaw contacts tip?"}
    stepF -->|"No"| stepG["Adjust and recheck"]
    stepF -->|"Yes"| stepH{"Horizontal jaws touch flanks?"}
    stepH -->|"No"| stepI["Tooth profile error detected"]
    stepH -->|"Yes"| stepJ["Read W and h_a directly"]
    stepG --> stepE
    stepI --> stepK["Record error, mark tooth"]
    stepJ --> stepL{"Within ISO tolerance?"}
    stepL -->|"Yes"| stepM["Accept tooth"]
    stepL -->|"No"| stepN["Reject tooth"]
```

## 4.3 Geometric Schematic — Chordal vs Circular Thickness

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    g1["Gear Centre O"] -->|"Radius D/2"| pc["Pitch Circle PC"]
    pc -->|"Tooth thickness arc t"| arc["Circular Thickness Arc"]
    pc -->|"Chord across flanks"| ch["Chordal Thickness W"]
    ch -->|"Below tip by h_a"| tip["Tooth Tip Addendum Circle"]
    arc -->|"Subtends angle pi/Z at O"| ang["Half-angle beta = pi/2Z"]
    ch -->|"Inclined at angle alpha to radial line"| ang
    tip -->|"Addendum m above PC"| pc
    ch -->|"Measured by horizontal jaws"| jaws["Gear Tooth Vernier Jaws"]
    tip -->|"Touched by vertical jaw"| jaws
    jaws -->|"W < W_theory means tooth worn"| result["Error / Wear Indicator"]
```

## 4.4 Functional Description of Parts

| Component | Function | Schematic Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal sliding jaw** | Measures W across tooth flanks | Node 3a in architecture diagram |
| **Vertical sliding jaw** | Measures hₐ from tooth tip to chord | Node 2a in architecture diagram |
| **Main scale (vertical)** | Linear scale 0–25 mm, 1 mm divisions | Node 4 in architecture diagram |
| **Main scale (horizontal)** | Linear scale 0–30 mm, 1 mm divisions | Node 6 in architecture diagram |
| **Vernier plate (vertical)** | 50 div over 49 mm → **0.02 mm LC** | Node 5 in architecture diagram |
| **Vernier plate (horizontal)** | 50 div over 49 mm → **0.02 mm LC** | Node 7 in architecture diagram |
| **Locking screw** | Freezes the jaws at preset W, hₐ values | Step D in procedure flowchart |
| **Datum face** | Sits flat on the gear end-face for repeatable positioning | Implicit in step E |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define chordal thickness and chordal addendum in the context of gear inspection. Why are chordal measurements preferred over circular measurements for shop-floor inspection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Chordal thickness (W):** It is the straight-line distance measured across a gear tooth at the pitch circle, between the two points where the involute flanks intersect the pitch circle. Mathematically, $W = \dfrac{\pi m}{2} \cos(\alpha)$.
>
> **Chordal addendum (hₐ):** It is the perpendicular distance from the tip of the tooth (addendum circle) to the chordal thickness line. Mathematically, $h_a = m + \dfrac{W}{2} \tan(\alpha) = m + \dfrac{\pi m}{4} \sin(\alpha)$.
>
> **Why chordal is preferred:** Circular tooth thickness is measured along an arc on the pitch circle, which is geometrically inaccessible to a simple linear caliper (the curve cannot be touched by straight jaws without error). The chordal thickness is a straight-line dimension, directly measurable with two flat-jaw contacts, making it ideal for **quick, repeatable shop-floor inspection** using a gear tooth vernier without expensive floating-carriage equipment.

*(1 mark for each definition, 1 mark for the reason — award ½ mark for partial correctness.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**For a standard 20° full-depth spur gear with module m = 3 mm, calculate the theoretical chordal tooth thickness and chordal addendum.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: m = 3 mm, α = 20°, standard full-depth gear.

Step 1 — Chordal thickness:

$$
W = \dfrac{\pi m}{2} \cos(20°) = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 3}{2} \times 0.9397 = 4.7124 \times 0.9397 \approx 4.4282 \, \text{mm}
$$

Step 2 — Chordal addendum:

$$
h_a = m + \dfrac{W}{2}\tan(20°) = 3 + \dfrac{4.4282}{2} \times 0.3640 = 3 + 2.2141 \times 0.3640 \approx 3.806 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- W ≈ **4.428 mm**
- hₐ ≈ **3.806 mm**

*(1 mark for correct formula, 1 mark for W calculation, 1 mark for hₐ calculation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a gear tooth vernier**. Explain how the **least count** is determined. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A standard 20° full-depth spur gear has module m = 5 mm and 30 teeth. Calculate:
   - (i) The pitch circle diameter
   - (ii) The chordal tooth thickness
   - (iii) The chordal addendum
   
   If the measured chordal thickness on a worn tooth is 7.35 mm, find the **percentage wear** and comment on whether the gear is acceptable as per ISO 1328 Grade 9 (allowable tooth thickness deviation = ±40 μm for m = 5). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Least Count

**Construction (3 marks):**

A gear tooth vernier consists of:
- An **L-shaped frame** with a fixed reference face (datum face) that rests on the gear's end-face.
- A **horizontal sliding jaw** that moves along the main horizontal scale to clamp across the tooth flanks.
- A **vertical sliding jaw** that moves along the vertical scale to touch the tooth tip.
- **Two vernier plates** — one for each axis — each engraved with 50 divisions over 49 mm, providing an LC of 0.02 mm.
- A **locking screw** to freeze the jaws at preset values.
- The jaws have **flat, lapped contact surfaces** that seat onto the involute flanks near the pitch circle.

**Working (2 marks):**

1. The gear's module m and pressure angle α are read from the drawing.
2. Theoretical chordal thickness W and chordal addendum hₐ are calculated.
3. The vertical scale is preset to hₐ by sliding the vertical jaw.
4. The horizontal scale is preset to W by sliding the horizontal jaw.
5. The instrument is mounted on the tooth; if it fits with all three contact points (tip + two flanks) simultaneously, the tooth is to specification. If not, the deviation is read directly.

**Least Count Determination (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Least Count} = \dfrac{\text{1 Main Scale Division (MSD)} - \text{1 Vernier Scale Division (VSD)}}{1}
$$

With 50 vernier divisions over 49 mm:
- 1 VSD = 49 / 50 = 0.98 mm
- 1 MSD = 1.00 mm
- LC = 1.00 − 0.98 = **0.02 mm**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

Given: m = 5 mm, Z = 30, α = 20°.

**(i) Pitch circle diameter:**

$$
D = m \cdot Z = 5 \times 30 = 150 \, \text{mm}
$$

**(ii) Chordal tooth thickness:**

$$
W = \dfrac{\pi \times 5}{2} \times \cos(20°) = 7.854 \times 0.9397 = 7.381 \, \text{mm}
$$

**(iii) Chordal addendum:**

$$
h_a = 1.2687 \times 5 = 6.3435 \approx 6.344 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Percentage wear:**

$$
\Delta W = W_{\text{worn}} - W_{\text{theoretical}} = 7.35 - 7.381 = -0.031 \, \text{mm} = -31 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

$$
\% \text{ wear} = \dfrac{0.031}{7.381} \times 100 = 0.420 \, \%
$$

**Acceptability (ISO 1328 Grade 9, m = 5):**

Allowable deviation = ±40 μm. Measured deviation = −31 μm → **|ΔW| < 40 μm**, so the gear is **ACCEPTABLE** for Grade 9.

> **Alternative consideration:** If the manufacturer specified Grade 8 (allowable ±25 μm), the gear would be **REJECTED** as the wear exceeds tolerance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expressions for **chordal tooth thickness (W)** and **chordal addendum (hₐ)** for a standard involute spur gear. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A standard 20° involute spur gear has module m = 2.5 mm and Z = 40 teeth. Calculate:
   - (i) Circular tooth thickness at pitch circle
   - (ii) Chordal tooth thickness
   - (iii) Tooth space width at addendum circle
   
   Explain **three sources of error** in gear tooth vernier measurements and how they affect the accuracy. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of W and hₐ

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Gear is **standard full-depth involute spur gear**.
2. Pitch circle is the reference circle where tooth thickness = space width = πm/2.
3. Pressure angle α = 20° (or as specified).
4. Tooth profile is theoretically perfect involute.

**Derivation of W (3 marks):**

The circular tooth thickness at the pitch circle is:

$$
t = \dfrac{\pi D}{Z} = \dfrac{\pi (mZ)}{Z} = \dfrac{\pi m}{2}
$$

This arc subtends a half-angle β = π / (2Z) at the gear centre. The involute flank normal at the pitch circle is inclined at α to the radial line. Hence, the chord connecting the two flank points at the pitch circle (perpendicular to the flanks) has length:

$$
W = t \cdot \cos(\alpha) = \dfrac{\pi m}{2} \cos(\alpha)
$$

**Derivation of hₐ (3 marks):**

The chordal addendum has two components:
- Addendum from PC to tip = m
- Vertical drop from PC midpoint to the chord line = (W/2) · tan(α)

Therefore:

$$
h_a = m + \dfrac{W}{2}\tan(\alpha) = m + \dfrac{\pi m}{4}\sin(\alpha)
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

Given: m = 2.5 mm, Z = 40, α = 20°.

**(i) Circular tooth thickness at PC:**

$$
t = \dfrac{\pi m}{2} = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 2.5}{2} = 3.927 \, \text{mm}
$$

**(ii) Chordal tooth thickness:**

$$
W = \dfrac{\pi m}{2} \cos(20°) = 3.927 \times 0.9397 = 3.690 \, \text{mm}
$$

**(iii) Tooth space width at addendum circle (using involute equation):**

The tooth thickness at the addendum circle (at radius rₐ = m + m/2 × 2 = 1.5m):

$$
t_a = r_a \left(\dfrac{t}{D/2} + 2(\text{inv}\,\alpha_a - \text{inv}\,\alpha)\right)
$$

Where inv α = tan α − α (in radians).

For m = 2.5, Z = 40, D = 100, rₐ = (1.5 × 2.5) + (2.5/2 × 40)/2... let me simplify: rₐ = (D/2) + m = 50 + 2.5 = 52.5 mm. The base circle radius r_b = (D/2) cos α = 50 × 0.9397 = 46.985 mm.

Pressure angle at addendum circle:

$$
\cos\alpha_a = \dfrac{r_b}{r_a} = \dfrac{46.985}{52.5} = 0.8949 \implies \alpha_a = 26.51°
$$

Angular tooth thickness at addendum circle:

$$
\phi_a = \dfrac{t}{D/2} + 2(\text{inv}\,\alpha_a - \text{inv}\,\alpha)
$$

where inv α = tan 20° − (π/9) = 0.3640 − 0.3491 = 0.01490 rad
and inv αₐ = tan 26.51° − (26.51 × π/180) = 0.4989 − 0.4627 = 0.0362 rad

$$
\phi_a = \dfrac{3.927}{50} + 2(0.0362 - 0.01490) = 0.07854 + 0.0426 = 0.12114 \, \text{rad}
$$

Tooth space width at addendum circle:

$$
s_a = \pi - \phi_a = 3.1416 - 0.12114 = 3.0205 \, \text{rad} \quad \text{(in terms of arc on unit circle)}
$$

In linear measure (multiply by rₐ):

$$
s_a^{\text{linear}} = r_a \cdot s_a = 52.5 \times 3.0205 = 158.6 \, \text{mm}
$$

> **Note to student:** For KTU lab exam, part (iii) is rarely asked in full involute detail — typically only W and hₐ are required. If the question asks for chordal measurements at the addendum circle, apply the same formula with rₐ instead of D/2.

**Three Sources of Error (1 + 1 + 1 marks):**

1. **Pitch Error:** Due to indexing error in the gear cutting machine, individual tooth spacings vary. This causes the same tooth to read differently when measured at different angular positions. Mitigation: Measure each tooth and average.
2. **Profile Error:** If the tooth is not a perfect involute, the jaws contact the flank at a different radius than the pitch circle, giving an erroneous W reading. This is detected by **trace diagrams** from gear testers.
3. **Instrument Calibration Drift / Operator Error:** Vernier jaws may not be perfectly perpendicular, or the locking screw may slip. Mitigation: Calibrate on a **setting standard** (master gear or slip gauge block) before each inspection session, and use a **light finger pressure** on the locking screw.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by cos(α)** — Students write W = πm/2 directly, which is the CIRCULAR thickness, not chordal. This error alone costs **2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Wrong chordal addendum formula** — Some students write hₐ = m + W (incorrect). Correct form is hₐ = m + (W/2)·tan(α) or equivalently m + (πm/4)·sin(α). Mixing these up costs **1–2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Using degrees vs. radians in involute function** — When calculating inv α = tan α − α, the angle α MUST be in radians. A common error is to subtract 20 instead of 0.3491 rad. This silently corrupts the calculation.
>
> 4. **Sign convention in error reporting** — Always specify whether the measured value is **less than** (worn tooth, negative error) or **greater than** (oversized, positive error) the theoretical value. Just writing "0.024 mm error" without sign loses ½ mark.
>
> 5. **Confusing pressure angle values** — Standard SI/involute gears use **20°**, NOT 14.5° (which is the older British standard) or 25° (high contact ratio). Misreading α = 14.5° when 20° is given wastes a full calculation.
>
> 6. **Unit mismatch in percentage wear** — When computing %, divide by the theoretical (nominal) value, not the measured value. Some students mistakenly divide by the worn value, which inflates the percentage and fails the ISO acceptance check.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gear Tooth Vernier**

- ☐ **Definition:** Gear tooth vernier measures **chordal thickness (W)** and **chordal addendum (hₐ)** of gear teeth.
- ☐ **Two Perpendicular Jaws:** Horizontal for W, vertical for hₐ; both have vernier scales.
- ☐ **LC Formula:** LC = 1 MSD − 1 VSD = (typical) **0.02 mm** for 50/49 vernier.
- ☐ **Core Formula 1:** $W = \dfrac{\pi m}{2}\cos(\alpha)$ — chordal tooth thickness.
- ☐ **Core Formula 2:** $h_a = m + \dfrac{W}{2}\tan(\alpha) = m + \dfrac{\pi m}{4}\sin(\alpha)$ — chordal addendum.
- ☐ **For α = 20°:** W ≈ **1.4760 m**, hₐ ≈ **1.2687 m** — **memorize these coefficients**.
- ☐ **Procedure:** Calculate → preset → place on tooth → read W and hₐ → compare with theory.
- ☐ **Error Reporting:** Always report **sign** (positive = oversized, negative = worn).
- ☐ **Standard Pressure Angle:** **20°** unless otherwise specified (default for SI gears).
- ☐ **Quality Standard:** **ISO 1328 / IS 4074 / AGMA 2000** defines allowable tooth thickness deviation.
- ☐ **Limitation:** Measures only **one tooth at a time**; cannot detect pitch error or profile error in one reading.
- ☐ **Difference from Gear Tooth Micrometer:** Vernier is manual read-out; micrometer uses a thimble for finer reading (typically 0.01 mm LC) — but vernier is more common on KTU lab floors.
- ☐ **Master Setting:** Always set the vernier on a **master gear** or **slip gauge** before inspection.
- ☐ **Error Sources to Memorize:** (i) Pitch error, (ii) Profile error, (iii) Instrument calibration, (iv) Operator inconsistency.

---

**End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Gear Tooth Vernier (PCPEL507 / KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of straightness and flatness using Autocollimator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL507 - INDUSTRIAL INSPECTION AND QUALITY CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of straightness and flatness using Autocollimator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — STRAIGHTNESS & FLATNESS VIA AUTOCOLLIMATOR

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Autocollimator:** A precision optical/electronic instrument used to measure **small angular displacements** of a reflecting surface with very high accuracy (typically **0.1 arc-seconds to 1 arc-second**). It is the standard non-contact inspection tool for evaluating **straightness** of a line and **flatness** of a surface in a metrology laboratory.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Industrial Inspection & Quality Control Lab, the autocollimator is the primary instrument used to verify the **geometric form errors** of high-precision components such as machine tool guideways, surface plates, and optical flats.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Laser Level on Steroids"

Imagine you are trying to check if a long dining table is perfectly flat. A carpenter would use a **spirit level** (bubble level). Now imagine you need to check flatness to within the thickness of a sheet of paper (a few microns of deviation over a 1-meter length). A spirit level is useless at that precision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** An autocollimator is essentially a *super-accurate, non-contact, light-based spirit level*. Instead of a bubble, it projects a fine cross-hair/reticle through a telescope, and an internal beam-splitter reflects the image back through the same telescope onto a graduated scale. If the reflecting surface (mirror) tilts even slightly, the return image shifts on the scale — and that shift is directly proportional to the **angle of tilt**.

| Quantity | Typical Value (Industrial Lab) |
|---|---|
| Angular resolution | **0.1″ to 1″ (arc-seconds)** |
| Working distance | **0.5 m to 10 m** |
| Beam diameter | **≈ 8–25 mm** |
| Measurement range | **± 10′ (minutes of arc) typically** |
| 1 arc-second | **4.848 × 10⁻⁶ rad** |

### 1.3 What are Straightness and Flatness?

- **Straightness** — A line/edge is straight if all its points lie on a single straight line. Measured as the *maximum deviation* (in µm) of the actual line from the reference straight line over the measured length.
- **Flatness** — A surface is flat if all its points lie on a single plane. Measured as the *maximum separation* (in µm) between two parallel planes that just enclose the actual surface.

Both are **form tolerances** in **GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing)** as per **ISO 1101**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of an Autocollimator

The word **"autocollimator"** comes from *auto* (self) + *collimator* (device that produces parallel rays). The instrument uses the same objective lens to **project** a collimated (parallel) beam onto a reflector *and* to **receive** the reflected beam back through itself.

### 2.2 The Optical Path — Step-by-Step Logic

1. A **light source** (LED or incandescent) illuminates a **graduated reticle (scale)** placed at the focal point of the objective lens.
2. The objective lens **collimates** the light into a parallel beam that travels to the **target mirror / reflector**.
3. The mirror reflects the beam back along the *same path* (collinearly).
4. The returned beam passes through the objective lens and is **focused** to form an image of the reticle in the focal plane of the eyepiece (or on a photodetector, in electronic units).
5. If the mirror is **perpendicular** to the optical axis → the returning image coincides exactly with the original reticle (zero reading).
6. If the mirror is **tilted by an angle θ** → the returning beam deviates by an angle **2θ**, producing a lateral image shift `d` on the focal plane.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "factor of 2" rule:** Because reflection obeys the law of reflection (angle of incidence = angle of reflection), a mirror tilt of `θ` produces a beam deviation of `2θ`. This is the most important rule to remember for KTU problems.

### 2.3 Derivation of the Master Equation

Consider the objective lens of focal length `f`. A ray from the reticle emerges parallel to the axis. After reflection from a mirror tilted by `θ`, the ray returns at an angle `2θ` to the axis. This ray is focused by the same objective onto the focal plane, where it forms an image at a lateral displacement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(2\theta) &= \frac{d}{f} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For very small angles (which is the regime of an autocollimator, since θ is typically a few arc-minutes), we use the small-angle approximation **tan(x) ≈ x (in radians)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\theta &\approx \frac{d}{f} \\
\theta &\approx \frac{d}{2f} \quad \text{(radians)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In **arc-seconds**, the conversion factor is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ rad} &= \frac{180 \times 60 \times 60}{\pi} \text{ arc-seconds} = 206265 \text{″} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

So the final working formula in arc-seconds is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta \text{ (arc-seconds)} &= \frac{d \times 206265}{2f} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, the instrument scale is *graduated directly* in arc-seconds or arc-minutes, eliminating the need to compute `d` and `f` manually.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Beam deviation | $\Delta = 2\theta$ | rad or ″ | Law of reflection |
| 2 | Angular tilt of mirror | $\theta = d / (2f)$ | rad | Small-angle approx. |
| 3 | Angular tilt in arc-seconds | $\theta = (d \times 206265) / (2f)$ | ″ | Use when scale is in mm |
| 4 | Linear height difference | $h = L \times \tan(\theta) \approx L \times \theta$ | mm | For straightness over length L |
| 5 | Linear height in µm | $h_{\mu m} = L_{mm} \times \theta_{rad} \times 1000$ | µm | 1 mm = 1000 µm |
| 6 | Straightness error | $E_{str} = h_{max} - h_{min}$ | µm | Max − Min of corrected profile |
| 7 | Flatness error (3-point method) | $E_{flat} = h_{max} - h_{min}$ | µm | 3 legs of surface plate |
| 8 | Flatness error (diagonal method) | $\text{4 readings: 2 diagonals crossed}$ | µm | More accurate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always convert units consistently** before substitution. A common KTU mistake is mixing mm and µm, or radians and arc-seconds. The board examiner deducts marks for unit inconsistency.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

Autocollimators are used in:

- **Calibration of machine tool straightness** (lathe bed, milling machine column).
- **Flatness verification of surface plates** and **granite masters** in QC labs.
- **Alignment of precision instruments** (theodolites, telescopes, optical benches).
- **Squareness checking** (with a pentaprism or right-angle mirror).
- **Angular indexing of dividing heads** in gear manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Apparatus Required (for the KTU Lab Record)

1. **Autocollimator** (with illuminated cross-wire reticle and graduated eyepiece scale).
2. **Surface plate / precision straightedge** (the test piece).
3. **Movable reflector carriage** (with a small plane mirror mounted on a kinematic base).
4. **Step blocks / precision gauge blocks** (to step the carriage).
5. **Stable base** (anti-vibration table).
6. **Reading lamp / micrometer eyepiece** (for the optical type).

## 3.2 Experimental Procedure — Straightness Measurement

### Method A: Direct Reading on Graduated Scale (Optical Autocollimator)

1. Mount the autocollimator on a rigid stand. **Level it** with the integral bubble level.
2. Place the **plane mirror** on the test straightedge at one end (datum position).
3. Adjust the autocollimator's eyepiece until the **graduated scale is sharp**.
4. Tilt the autocollimator slightly to bring the **cross-hair image** to the centre of the scale → set **zero reading**.
5. Slide the mirror along the straightedge in **equal increments** (say 50 mm). At each position, read the angular deflection in arc-seconds or arc-minutes from the scale.
6. Tabulate: **Position (mm) | Scale reading (′ or ″)**.
7. Convert angular readings to **linear deviations** using:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_i = L_i \times \tan(\theta_i) \approx L_i \times \theta_i \text{ (rad)} 
\end{aligned}
$$

8. Plot a **straightness profile** (deviation vs. position) and find the **maximum peak-to-valley** value.

### 3.3 Sample Worked Numerical — Straightness

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A straightedge of length **500 mm** is checked with an optical autocollimator (focal length $f = 200$ mm). The mirror is shifted in 50 mm steps. The image displacement readings `d` on the focal-plane scale are:

| Position (mm) | 0 | 50 | 100 | 150 | 200 | 250 | 300 | 350 | 400 | 450 | 500 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| d (mm) | 0.00 | 0.04 | 0.07 | 0.09 | 0.10 | 0.11 | 0.10 | 0.08 | 0.05 | 0.03 | 0.00 |

**Step 1 — Compute angle at each position:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_i \text{ (rad)} &= \frac{d_i}{2f} = \frac{d_i}{2 \times 200} = \frac{d_i}{400}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $d_3 = 0.07$ mm (at 100 mm position):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_3 &= \frac{0.07}{400} = 1.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

In arc-seconds:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_3 \text{ (″)} &= 1.75 \times 10^{-4} \times 206265 \approx 36.1 \text{″}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to linear height deviation at each station:**

Since the mirror is at distance $L_i$ from the datum, the **height difference** between the actual surface and a reference line is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_i = L_i \times \theta_i \quad (\text{mm})
\end{aligned}
$$

For $L_3 = 100$ mm, $d_3 = 0.07$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_3 &= 100 \times \frac{0.07}{400} = \frac{7}{400} = 0.0175 \text{ mm} = 17.5 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Full calculation table:**

| Position $L_i$ (mm) | $d_i$ (mm) | $\theta_i = d_i / 400$ (×10⁻⁴ rad) | $h_i = L_i \theta_i$ (mm) | $h_i$ (µm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.0000 | 0.00 |
| 50 | 0.04 | 0.10 | 0.0050 | 5.00 |
| 100 | 0.07 | 0.175 | 0.0175 | 17.50 |
| 150 | 0.09 | 0.225 | 0.0338 | 33.75 |
| 200 | 0.10 | 0.25 | 0.0500 | 50.00 |
| 250 | 0.11 | 0.275 | 0.0688 | 68.75 |
| 300 | 0.10 | 0.25 | 0.0750 | 75.00 |
| 350 | 0.08 | 0.20 | 0.0700 | 70.00 |
| 400 | 0.05 | 0.125 | 0.0500 | 50.00 |
| 450 | 0.03 | 0.075 | 0.0338 | 33.75 |
| 500 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.0000 | 0.00 |

**Step 4 — Compute straightness error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{str} &= h_{max} - h_{min} = 75.00 - 0.00 = 75.00 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on reference line:** The "Min" and "Max" are taken *after* rotating the profile about its endpoints (or fitting a least-squares line) so the endpoints touch zero. Here, the endpoints are already 0, so no rotation is needed. In a real lab, this is the most common source of error.

## 3.4 Experimental Procedure — Flatness Measurement

A surface plate's flatness is checked by the **3-Point or Diagonal Method** using the autocollimator + a movable mirror.

### 3.4.1 Three-Point Method

1. Place the surface plate on three calibrated support legs (at three corners A, B, C).
2. Mount the autocollimator at one corner. Place the mirror at the **other two corners (B and C)** sequentially. Read the angles.
3. The **angular differences** give the tilts. Convert each tilt to a height difference between the legs.
4. **Flatness error** = sum of the maximum positive and negative deviations when the surface is referenced to the **best-fit plane** through the three legs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **best-fit plane** is the plane that **minimises the sum of squared deviations** from all measured points (least-squares plane). A common KTU pitfall: students use the *horizontal plane* as reference — this gives an **inclination error** in addition to flatness error, which is **wrong** per ISO 1101.

### 3.4.2 Diagonal Method (Preferred)

1. Divide the surface into a grid of $m \times n$ points.
2. At each grid point, read the autocollimator angle with the mirror.
3. Compute heights $h_{i,j} = L \times \theta_{i,j}$.
4. Fit a plane through the **four corner points** by averaging the two diagonal slopes.
5. Subtract the fitted plane from the measured heights.
6. **Flatness error** = (max of corrected heights) − (min of corrected heights).

### 3.5 Sample Numerical — Flatness

**Problem:** A 400 × 400 mm surface plate is measured at a 3 × 3 grid using an autocollimator. Mirror is at 200 mm from the autocollimator base. The scale readings (in arc-seconds) are:

| Row\Col | C1 | C2 | C3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | 0 | +3 | 0 |
| R2 | +4 | +6 | +2 |
| R3 | +2 | +5 | +1 |

**Step 1 — Convert arc-seconds to radians:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta \text{ (rad)} = \frac{\theta \text{ (″)}}{206265}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the centre point (R2,C2) = 6″:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{6}{206265} = 2.909 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to height at each grid (using L = 200 mm reference distance):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h = L \times \theta = 200 \times \frac{\theta_{\text{arcsec}}}{206265}
\end{aligned}
$$

For centre: $h = 200 \times 2.909 \times 10^{-5} = 5.818 \times 10^{-3}$ mm = **5.82 µm**.

Full grid in µm:

| Row\Col | C1 | C2 | C3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | 0.00 | 2.91 | 0.00 |
| R2 | 3.88 | 5.82 | 1.94 |
| R3 | 1.94 | 4.85 | 0.97 |

**Step 3 — Apply the diagonal-method best-fit plane correction:**

The four corners are (0, 0, 0), (0, 2.91, 0), (1.94, 0, 0.97), (3.88, 1.94, 1.94). Fitting a plane by least-squares gives correction coefficients (typical board answer: subtract the average slope). The corrected values yield:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{flat} = h_{max,corrected} - h_{min,corrected}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a clean KTU answer, the flatness error from this data ≈ **5.82 µm** (centre relative to best-fit plane).

## 3.6 Sources of Error (Examiner's Hot List)

1. **Autocollimator not levelled** → introduces a constant angular offset.
2. **Mirror not in contact** with the surface (debris, dust) → height error.
3. **Air turbulence** along the optical path → image wander (use an air shield/tube).
4. **Temperature gradients** → refractive index changes; allow 30 min warm-up.
5. **Parallax in the eyepiece** → incorrect scale reading.
6. **Mirror flatness itself** → should be $\lambda/10$ or better.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source LED] --> B[Illuminated Reticle Scale]
    B --> C[Beam Splitter]
    C --> D[Objective Lens focal length f]
    D --> E[Parallel Collimated Beam]
    E --> F[Target Mirror on Test Surface]
    F --> G[Reflected Beam at 2 theta deviation]
    G --> D
    D --> H[Focused Image on Focal Plane]
    H --> I[Eyepiece with Crosswire or CCD Detector]
    I --> J[Scale Reading arc seconds or arc minutes]
    J --> K[Computation of theta and linear deviation h]
    K --> L[Straightness or Flatness Profile Plot]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow of a Complete Inspection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1 Level the Autocollimator using bubble] --> stepB[Step 2 Mount the Test Piece on Surface Plate]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Place the Movable Mirror at Position 1]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Set Zero by Aligning Crosswire]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Shift Mirror to Next Grid Point]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 Record the Angular Reading]
    stepF --> stepG{Is this the Last Point?}
    stepG -- No --> stepE
    stepG -- Yes --> stepH[Step 7 Convert Angles to Linear Heights h]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 8 Apply Best Fit Reference Line or Plane]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 9 Compute Peak to Valley Error]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step 10 Compare with ISO 1101 Tolerance Grade]
```

## 4.3 Optical Schematic (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Illuminant] --> ret[Graduated Reticle at Focal Plane]
    ret --> obj[Objective Lens]
    obj --> beam[Parallel Beam in Air]
    beam --> mir[Flat Mirror on Test Surface]
    mir --> beam
    beam --> obj
    obj --> ret
    ret --> eye[Eyepiece with Crosshair]
    eye --> eye2[Observer Eye or CCD]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic above is the **block-level functional architecture** of a typical optical autocollimator. The same architecture is used in **electronic/digital autocollimators**, with the only difference being that the eyepiece is replaced by a **linear CCD/CMOS photodetector array** and the readings are displayed digitally.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] 
**Define an autocollimator. State any two of its industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**
An autocollimator is a precision optical/electronic instrument that uses a collimated light beam and a reflector to measure small angular displacements of a reflecting surface, typically with a resolution of 0.1″ to 1″.
*(1 mark)*

It works on the principle that a tilt `θ` of the reflecting mirror produces a deviation of `2θ` in the reflected beam, which is read on a graduated scale at the focal plane of the objective lens.
*(1 mark)*

**Two industrial applications:**
1. Checking straightness of machine tool guideways and surface plates.
2. Squareness/parallelism measurement of precision components in metrology labs.
*(1 mark)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] 
**Why is the deviation of the reflected beam in an autocollimator equal to 2θ and not θ, where θ is the mirror tilt?**

**Model Answer:**
When a light ray strikes a plane mirror, the **law of reflection** states that the *angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection*, both measured from the normal to the mirror surface.
*(1 mark)*

If the mirror is tilted by an angle `θ` from a reference position, the normal also tilts by `θ`. The incoming parallel beam now makes an angle `θ` with the *new* normal, and is reflected symmetrically at `θ` on the other side. The **total angular change** between the original beam direction and the reflected beam direction is therefore `θ + θ = 2θ`.
*(2 marks)*

This is why the autocollimator master equation is `θ_mirror = d / (2f)` and not `d / f`.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle** of an optical autocollimator. Derive the relation between the angular tilt of the mirror and the displacement of the image on the focal plane. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (block diagram already shown in Section 4.1):** The autocollimator consists of a light source, an illuminated graduated reticle, a beam splitter, an objective lens of focal length `f`, and an eyepiece with a cross-hair. *(2 marks)*

**Working Principle:** Light from the source illuminates the reticle at the focal plane. The objective lens collimates the diverging rays into a parallel beam that strikes the external plane mirror. The reflected beam returns through the same objective lens and is focused onto the reticle plane, where the observer sees the reticle image superimposed on the original scale. *(2 marks)*

**Derivation:** Let the mirror be tilted by an angle `θ` from the perpendicular. By the law of reflection, the returned beam deviates by `2θ` from the axis. This deviated beam is focused at a lateral distance `d` from the optical axis on the focal plane of the objective lens. From the geometry of the focused ray:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(2\theta) &= \frac{d}{f} \\
2\theta &\approx \frac{d}{f} \quad \text{(small angle)} \\
\theta &= \frac{d}{2f} \text{ radians}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks — for the explicit derivation with explanation)*

---

**(b)** A straightedge of length **600 mm** is tested with an autocollimator (focal length $f = 250$ mm) at 6 equal stations. The image displacements are 0, 0.06, 0.10, 0.12, 0.08, 0 mm. Calculate the **straightness error** of the edge in µm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Stations and lengths** (6 stations → 5 intervals, each 600/5 = 120 mm):

| Station | Position $L_i$ (mm) | $d_i$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 120 | 0.06 |
| 3 | 240 | 0.10 |
| 4 | 360 | 0.12 |
| 5 | 480 | 0.08 |
| 6 | 600 | 0.00 |

**Step 2 — Compute angle at each station:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_i = \frac{d_i}{2f} = \frac{d_i}{500} \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute linear deviation $h_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_i = L_i \times \theta_i = \frac{L_i \times d_i}{500} \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Station | $L_i$ (mm) | $d_i$ (mm) | $h_i = L_i d_i / 500$ (mm) | $h_i$ (µm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0.00 | 0.0000 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 120 | 0.06 | 0.0144 | 14.40 |
| 3 | 240 | 0.10 | 0.0480 | 48.00 |
| 4 | 360 | 0.12 | 0.0864 | 86.40 |
| 5 | 480 | 0.08 | 0.0768 | 76.80 |
| 6 | 600 | 0.00 | 0.0000 | 0.00 |

**Step 4 — Apply the rotation correction** (subtract the linear trend that joins the endpoints to enforce the reference-line convention).

Endpoints are both zero, so no rotation needed (the reference line is the straight line joining the endpoints).

**Step 5 — Compute straightness error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{str} &= h_{max} - h_{min} = 86.40 - 0.00 = 86.40 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Marks: 1 for table setup, 2 for formula application, 2 for numerical substitution, 1 for final answer, 1 for unit correctness.)*

**Final Answer:** Straightness error ≈ **86.4 µm** over 600 mm.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Alternative]

**(a)** Describe with a diagram the **method of measuring flatness of a surface plate** using an autocollimator and a movable mirror. What is the role of the **best-fit plane**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram:** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes. *(1 mark)*

**Procedure (Diagonal Method):**
1. The surface plate is placed on a stable support, level with the autocollimator axis.
2. The surface is divided into a uniform grid (e.g., $5 \times 5$ or $3 \times 3$).
3. A small, high-quality plane mirror is placed at each grid point in turn, with its back flat against the surface.
4. At each point, the autocollimator reading (in arc-seconds) is recorded. *(2 marks)*
5. The angle is converted to a height: $h_{i,j} = L \times \theta_{i,j}$, where $L$ is the perpendicular distance from the autocollimator base reference plane to the mirror.
6. A plane is fitted through the four corner points (or via least-squares through all points).
7. The flatness error is the maximum peak-to-valley deviation of the corrected heights. *(2 marks)*

**Role of the Best-Fit Plane (per ISO 1101):**
The best-fit plane is the *unique plane that minimises the sum of squared perpendicular distances* from all measured points. It is the correct reference for flatness because the surface plate is not physically constrained to be horizontal — it is only required to be *flat*. Using a horizontal (gravity) reference would incorrectly add the **inclination** of the entire plate to the true flatness error. *(2 marks)*

---

**(b)** The flatness of a 500 mm × 500 mm surface plate is measured using an autocollimator with the mirror at 250 mm from the instrument axis. The arc-second readings at 9 grid points are:

| | C1 | C2 | C3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | 0 | 5 | 8 |
| R2 | 4 | 9 | 12 |
| R3 | 6 | 10 | 14 |

Calculate the flatness error in µm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Convert each reading to height in µm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{i,j} \text{ (µm)} = L \times \theta_{i,j} \text{ (rad)} \times 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

with $L = 250$ mm and the radian value = arc-seconds / 206265.

So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h \text{ (µm)} = 250 \times \frac{\theta_{\text{arcsec}}}{206265} \times 1000 = \frac{250 \times 1000}{206265} \times \theta_{\text{arcsec}} = 1.2123 \times \theta_{\text{arcsec}}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Point | θ (″) | h (µm) |
|---|---|---|
| (R1,C1) | 0 | 0.00 |
| (R1,C2) | 5 | 6.06 |
| (R1,C3) | 8 | 9.70 |
| (R2,C1) | 4 | 4.85 |
| (R2,C2) | 9 | 10.91 |
| (R2,C3) | 12 | 14.55 |
| (R3,C1) | 6 | 7.27 |
| (R3,C2) | 10 | 12.12 |
| (R3,C3) | 14 | 16.97 |

**Step 2 — Best-fit plane through the four corners:**

Corners: (0, 6.06, 9.70), (6.06, 12.12, 16.97). Slope in R-direction ≈ (7.27 − 0) / 2 grid-units = 3.635 per row. Slope in C-direction ≈ (9.70 − 0) / 2 grid-units = 4.85 per col. The plane equation in grid units $(r, c)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{plane}(r, c) = 3.635 \cdot r + 4.85 \cdot c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Corrected heights** (measured − plane):

| Point | Measured | Plane | Corrected |
|---|---|---|---|
| (1,1) | 0.00 | 8.485 | −8.49 |
| (1,2) | 6.06 | 13.335 | −7.28 |
