| (1,3) | 9.70 | 18.185 | −8.49 |
| (2,1) | 4.85 | 12.12 | −7.27 |
| (2,2) | 10.91 | 16.97 | −6.06 |
| (2,3) | 14.55 | 21.82 | −7.27 |
| (3,1) | 7.27 | 15.755 | −8.49 |
| (3,2) | 12.12 | 20.605 | −8.49 |
| (3,3) | 16.97 | 25.455 | −8.49 |

**Step 4 — Flatness error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{flat} = h_{max,corrected} - h_{min,corrected} = (-6.06) - (-8.49) = 2.43 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Marks: 1 for setup, 2 for conversion, 2 for best-fit plane, 1 for subtraction, 1 for final result with unit.)*

**Final Answer:** Flatness error ≈ **2.43 µm**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** — writing $\theta = d/f$ instead of $d/(2f)$ → loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Using degrees or arc-minutes without converting** to arc-seconds or radians → loses 1–2 marks for unit inconsistency.
> 3. **Reporting straightness/flatness without units (µm)** → loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Failing to apply a reference line/plane correction** (using raw measurements as the error) → loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing straightness (1D, single line) with flatness (2D, surface)** in the procedure → loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Not mentioning the warm-up period and air turbulence shielding** in the procedure → loses 1 mark in viva.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- An **autocollimator** is a non-contact optical instrument for **small angular measurements** with sub-arc-second resolution.
- The working principle relies on the **law of reflection** — a mirror tilt of `θ` produces a **beam deviation of `2θ`**.
- Master equation: $\theta = d / (2f)$ (radians), or $\theta_{\text{arcsec}} = (d \times 206265) / (2f)$.
- **Straightness** is a 1-D form error (line); **flatness** is a 2-D form error (surface).
- **Procedure essentials:** level the instrument → set zero at datum → take readings at uniform grid points → convert angles to heights using $h = L \theta$.
- **Straightness error** = max(heights) − min(heights) after applying the **reference-line rotation correction**.
- **Flatness error** = max(heights) − min(heights) after subtracting the **best-fit plane** (least-squares plane through all measured points).
- **Reference standards:** ISO 1101 (Geometric Tolerancing), ASME Y14.5 (GD&T).
- **Key sources of error:** instrument not levelled, air turbulence, thermal gradients, mirror flatness, parallax in eyepiece.
- **Conversion constant to remember:** 1 radian = **206 265 arc-seconds** = 180/π degrees.
- **Common KTU mistakes to avoid:** missing the factor of 2, unit mixing, no reference correction, no units in the final answer.
- **Industrial applications:** machine tool alignment, surface plate certification, theodolite calibration, squareness with pentaprism, parallelism of precision slides.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of angle using sine bar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL507 - INDUSTRIAL INSPECTION AND QUALITY CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of angle using sine bar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sine Bar (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **sine bar** is a precision inspection instrument used for accurately measuring angles and for accurately locating workpieces at a given angle during machining, grinding, or inspection. It derives its name from the trigonometric principle of the **sine function**, which is used to determine the angle from a measured linear height.

A sine bar consists of a hardened, precision-ground steel bar with two identical cylindrical rollers (also called *plug gauges* or *pins*) of equal diameter mounted at a fixed, accurately known distance apart. The bar is placed on a flat datum surface (surface plate) and one roller is raised using a stack of **slip gauges** to incline the bar at the desired angle.

**Standard Centre Distance (L):** Typically manufactured in lengths of **100 mm, 200 mm, 300 mm**, and **500 mm** as per IS : 5359 / ISO 6120.

**Standard Roller Diameter (d):** Usually **20 mm** (sometimes 25 mm).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Seesaw of Precision"

Imagine a perfectly rigid, beautifully polished wooden plank lying flat on a kitchen counter. Now imagine you wedge a thick book under one end of the plank. The plank tilts up, forming a small ramp. The **height of the book**, the **length of the plank between two pivot points**, and the **tilt angle** of the plank are locked together by a beautiful trigonometric relationship — the *sine rule*.

A sine bar is exactly this — but engineered with laboratory-grade perfection:

- The **plank** = the hardened steel sine bar.
- The **two pivot points** = the two cylindrical rollers of *exactly* equal diameter.
- The **book** = a stack of precision slip gauges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Operating Principle:**
> $$\sin \theta = \frac{\text{Height of slip gauges }(h)}{\text{Distance between roller centres }(L)}$$

If you know any two of these quantities (height $h$, distance $L$, angle $\theta$), the third can be determined — this is the *single source of truth* behind all sine-bar calculations.

### 1.3 Classification of Sine Bars (KTU 2024 Module Coverage)

| Type | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Sine Bar** | Two cylindrical rollers of equal diameter fixed at known centre distance | Measuring angles up to **45°** with high accuracy |
| **Compound Sine Bar** | Combination of two simple sine bars hinged together | Measuring compound angles and large angles (>45°) |
| **Sine Centre** | Sine bar integrated with a precision chuck or fixture | Holding and locating cylindrical/conical workpieces |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Constructional Breakdown

A sine bar is not just a "bar" — every component has a precise purpose:

1. **Body (Bar):** Made of high-grade hardened steel (often **chromium steel** or **carbide-tipped** for wear resistance). The top and bottom surfaces are precision-lapped and **perfectly parallel** to each other.
2. **Two Cylinders/Rollers:** Machined from hardened steel, **perfectly cylindrical**, with their axes **parallel** to the bar and to each other. Their centres are at an accurately maintained distance **L**.
3. **Mounting/End Bosses:** Provide rigid support for the rollers and sometimes include tapped holes for attachment to fixtures.
4. **Working Surface:** The top of the bar is flat and parallel to the line connecting the lower tangent points of the two rollers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two Rollers of *Equal* Diameter?**
> If the rollers were of unequal diameter, the geometric *effective pivot points* would shift, making the trigonometric relationship invalid. Equal-diameter rollers guarantee that the effective pivot heights are identical, so the bar truly tilts about a horizontal line.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Sine-Bar Principle

Let us trace **why** the formula $\sin\theta = h/L$ works:

- The sine bar rests on a flat surface plate on its **two rollers**.
- When we insert a slip-gauge stack of height **h** under **one** roller, that roller is lifted by exactly **h**.
- The other roller remains in contact with the surface plate.
- Because the bar is **rigid** and the rollers are **equal diameter**, the bar tilts as a single rigid body about the line passing through the lower tangent of the *stationary* roller.
- The geometry forms a **right-angled triangle**:
  - **Hypotenuse** = distance between roller centres = $L$
  - **Opposite side** = height of slip gauges = $h$
  - **Angle of inclination** = $\theta$
- Therefore, by definition of sine:
$$\sin \theta = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{h}{L}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Angle from known height | $\sin \theta = \dfrac{h}{L}$ | Dimensionless | $h$ = slip-gauge stack height, $L$ = roller centre distance |
| 2 | Height for desired angle | $h = L \cdot \sin \theta$ | mm | Used to *set* an angle for inspection/machining |
| 3 | Angle in degrees | $\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{L}\right)$ | degrees (°) | Used to *measure* an unknown angle |
| 4 | Angle in radians | $\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{L}\right)$ | radians (rad) | Same equation; convert by $\times \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ for degrees |
| 5 | Maximum measurable angle | $\theta_{\max} = 90°$ | degrees | Theoretically possible when $h = L$ (impractical) |
| 6 | Practical upper angle | $\theta_{\text{practical}} \leq 45°$ | degrees | Above 45°, slip-gauge stack becomes unstable |
| 7 | Effective roller height | $h_{\text{eff}} = h + (d/2) - (d/2) = h$ | mm | Equal-diameter rollers cancel out — *no correction needed* |
| 8 | Sensitivity (small change in angle) | $\Delta\theta \approx \dfrac{\Delta h}{L \cdot \cos\theta}$ | radians | Smaller $L$ ⇒ higher angular sensitivity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility — Where Sine Bars Are Used:**
> - Inspection of **angle gauges, templates, and bevels**.
> - Setting up **tapered turning** on a lathe (set compound slide to angle).
> - **Tool grinder** angle setting (clearance, rake angles).
> - Verifying **dovetail slides and angular surfaces**.
> - Locating a workpiece at an angle during **milling, drilling, or EDM**.

### 2.4 Procedure for Measuring an Unknown Angle (Standard KTU Lab Protocol)

1. Clean the surface plate, sine bar, rollers, and the workpiece.
2. Place the sine bar on the surface plate with both rollers resting on it.
3. Position the workpiece on top of the sine bar.
4. Stack slip gauges to a *trial* height $h_1$ under one roller.
5. Use a **dial test indicator (DTI)** on the workpiece surface.
6. Adjust the slip-gauge height $h$ until the DTI reads the **same value** at both ends of the workpiece surface → confirming the surface is **parallel to the surface plate**.
7. Calculate the angle using $\theta = \sin^{-1}(h/L)$.
8. Verify by repeating with the sine bar reversed (180°) — this corrects for any **wobble or taper**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Sine-Bar Equation

We begin by establishing the geometric situation. Consider a sine bar of centre distance **L** placed on a flat surface plate. Let one roller rest directly on the surface plate (height = 0) and the other roller be raised by a stack of slip gauges of total height **h**.

The line connecting the two roller centres, when projected, forms the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose:

- **Vertical rise** = $h$ (the slip-gauge stack height).
- **Horizontal run** = $L$ (the centre-to-centre distance, since the surface plate is horizontal).
- **Angle** between hypotenuse and horizontal = $\theta$.

By the **definition of the sine function in a right triangle**:

$$
\sin \theta \;=\; \frac{\text{Opposite side}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} \;=\; \frac{h}{L}
$$

**Step 1 — Inverting to solve for h:**
Rearranging the above to express the height in terms of the angle:

$$
h \;=\; L \cdot \sin \theta
$$

This is the *design equation* used when an inspector wants to **set** an angle.

**Step 2 — Inverting to solve for θ:**
Taking the inverse sine (arcsin) of both sides:

$$
\theta \;=\; \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{L}\right)
$$

This is the *measurement equation* used when an inspector wants to **find** an unknown angle.

**Step 3 — Conversion between degrees and radians:**
Since scientific calculators and many KTU problems use both:

$$
\theta_{\text{deg}} \;=\; \theta_{\text{rad}} \times \frac{180°}{\pi} \qquad \text{or} \qquad \theta_{\text{rad}} \;=\; \theta_{\text{deg}} \times \frac{\pi}{180°}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Setting Up an Angle (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A sine bar of centre distance **200 mm** is to be used to set an angle of **18° 30'**. Calculate the height of the slip gauges required.

**Given:**
- $L = 200 \text{ mm}$
- $\theta = 18°\,30' = 18.5°$

**Step 1 — Convert angle to decimal degrees:**
$$
\theta = 18° + \frac{30}{60}° = 18.5°
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the height formula:**
$$
h = L \cdot \sin \theta = 200 \cdot \sin(18.5°)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate sine:**
$$
\sin(18.5°) = 0.31730
$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**
$$
h = 200 \times 0.31730 = 63.461 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Build slip-gauge stack:**
Using a standard slip-gauge set, build a stack totalling **63.461 mm** (e.g., 50 + 10 + 2 + 1 + 0.461 ≈ using 1.001 + 1.000 blocks, etc., or 50 + 10 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 0.461).

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always write slip-gauge stack composition in *descending* order from largest block. Examiners award a step-mark for this habit.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Measuring an Unknown Angle

> **Problem:** The slip-gauge stack used under one roller of a **300 mm** sine bar measures **87.250 mm**. Determine the inclined angle.

**Given:**
- $L = 300 \text{ mm}$
- $h = 87.250 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Apply the angle formula:**
$$
\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{L}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{87.250}{300}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio:**
$$
\frac{87.250}{300} = 0.29083
$$

**Step 3 — Take inverse sine:**
$$
\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.29083) = 16.91° \approx 16°\,54'\,\text{approx.}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\theta \approx 16°\,54'$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity & Error Behaviour

| Variable Effect | Change | Effect on $\theta$ | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase $h$ (slip height) | $h \uparrow$ | $\theta \uparrow$ | Larger $\sin \theta$ ⇒ larger angle |
| Increase $L$ (centre distance) | $L \uparrow$ | $\theta \downarrow$ | Ratio $h/L$ decreases |
| Equal roller diameters required | $d_1 = d_2$ | Geometry stays valid | Cancels vertical offset |
| Wear on rollers | Effective $d$ varies | Introduces **systematic error** | Pivot line shifts |
| Surface plate not flat | Local tilt | Adds **random error** | Reference plane is wrong |
| Slip gauges not wrung clean | Stack shorter | $\theta$ under-reads | Foreign particle reduces height |
| Bar not parallel to top surface | Tilted axis | Error in $L$ effective | Roller axes not collinear |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sine Bar Measurement System

This diagram shows the functional flow of the sine-bar measurement process — what each block contributes and how the inputs are transformed into the measured angle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Surface Plate - Reference Datum] --> B[Sine Bar Body - Hardened Steel]
    C[Slip Gauge Stack - Height h] --> B
    D[Two Equal Diameter Rollers - Distance L] --> B
    B --> E[Inclined Reference Plane - Angle Theta]
    E --> F[Workpiece Mounted on Sine Bar]
    F --> G[Dial Test Indicator DTI]
    G --> H{Surface Parallel Check}
    H --> I[Reading Matches - Adjust h]
    H --> J[Reading Mismatches - Re-stack Slip Gauges]
    I --> K[Apply Formula sin Theta = h over L]
    J --> I
    K --> L[Compute Theta via arcsin]
    L --> M[Final Measured Angle - Output]
    N[Repeat with Sine Bar Reversed 180 degrees] --> M
```

### 4.2 Geometric Schematic — Right-Triangle Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Roller 1 - On Surface Plate - Height 0] --> P2[Roller 2 - Lifted by h mm]
    P2 --> P3[Slip Gauge Stack - Height h]
    P3 --> P4[Surface Plate - Horizontal Reference]
    P4 --> P1
    P1 -.Hypotenuse L.-> P2
    P1 -.Vertical h.-> P3
    P2 -.Angle Theta.-> P4
    P5[Right Triangle with Opposite h Hypotenuse L] -.Governs.-> P1
```

### 4.3 Component-Level Architecture of a Sine Bar

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SB[Sine Bar Assembly] --> BD[Bar Body - Hardened Steel]
    SB --> R1[Roller 1 - Equal Diameter d]
    SB --> R2[Roller 2 - Equal Diameter d]
    SB --> WS[Working Surface - Lap Finished]
    SB --> EB[End Bosses - Roller Mounts]
    BD --> WS
    R1 --> EB
    R2 --> EB
    BD -. Centre Distance L .-> R1
    BD -. Centre Distance L .-> R2
    WS -. Parallelism Less than 0.001 mm .-> BD
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
*Define a sine bar. State the principle on which it works.*

**Model Answer:**

A sine bar is a precision instrument used for the accurate measurement of angles and for setting workpieces at a required angle during inspection or machining. It consists of a hardened, ground steel bar with two cylindrical rollers of *equal diameter* mounted at a precise, known centre distance.

**Principle:** It works on the **sine principle** of trigonometry. When a slip-gauge stack of height $h$ is placed under one roller of a sine bar of centre distance $L$, the bar is inclined at an angle $\theta$ given by:

$$
\sin \theta = \frac{h}{L}
$$

*(3 marks – 1 for definition, 1 for components, 1 for principle + formula)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Why are the two rollers of a sine bar made of equal diameter? What is the role of slip gauges in sine-bar measurement?*

**Model Answer:**

The two rollers are made of **equal diameter** so that the effective pivot heights are identical. This ensures that when one roller is raised by a slip-gauge stack of height $h$, the bar tilts as a rigid body and the vertical displacement of the lifted roller is *exactly* $h$. If the diameters differed, the geometry would introduce a constant error.

**Role of slip gauges:** Slip gauges provide the *precise vertical height* $h$ under one roller. Since their accuracy is traceable to national standards (typically ±0.5 µm for grade-0 blocks), they are the *reference input* that converts a trigonometric quantity (angle) into a precisely measurable linear quantity (height). *(3 marks – 1 each for the three key points)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a sine bar. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sine bar of centre distance **250 mm** is used to check an angle of **32° 45'**. Calculate:
  (i) The height of the slip gauges required.
  (ii) The error in angle if the slip-gauge stack is off by **+0.025 mm**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):** A sine bar consists of:
- A hardened steel **body** with lapped top and bottom surfaces that are mutually parallel.
- Two **cylindrical rollers** of equal diameter (typically 20 mm), with axes parallel to each other and to the bar's top surface.
- The rollers are fixed at a known centre distance $L$ (standard values: 100, 200, 300, 500 mm).
- The top surface is the working surface on which the workpiece rests.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
- The sine bar rests on a flat surface plate via its two rollers.
- A slip-gauge stack of height $h$ is placed under one roller.
- The bar tilts rigidly; the angle $\theta$ of inclination is given by $\sin \theta = h/L$.
- For **measuring an unknown angle**, $h$ is varied until the workpiece surface is parallel to the surface plate (verified by a dial indicator). The angle is then computed.
- For **setting a known angle**, $h$ is calculated from $h = L \sin \theta$ and the bar is set accordingly.

*Schematic:* (Refer to Section 4 diagrams for the examiner's reference.)

---

**Model Answer — (b):**

**Given:** $L = 250 \text{ mm},\quad \theta = 32°\,45' = 32.75°$

**(i) Height of slip gauges:** Apply the formula:

$$
h = L \sin \theta = 250 \times \sin(32.75°)
$$

$$
\sin(32.75°) = 0.54120
$$

$$
h = 250 \times 0.54120 = 135.30 \text{ mm}
$$

**Stack composition (sample):** $100 + 30 + 5 + 0.30 = 135.30$ mm

**(ii) Angular error due to slip-gauge error $\Delta h = +0.025$ mm:**

The differential relation obtained by differentiating $\sin\theta = h/L$ with respect to $h$ is:

$$
\cos\theta \cdot \Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta h}{L}
$$

Solving for $\Delta \theta$:

$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta h}{L \cdot \cos\theta} = \frac{0.025}{250 \times \cos(32.75°)}
$$

$$
\cos(32.75°) = 0.84089
$$

$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{0.025}{250 \times 0.84089} = 1.189 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}
$$

Convert to seconds: $1.189 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \times 3600 \approx 24.5''$

**Result:** $\Delta \theta \approx 24.5''$ (approx. **25 arc-seconds**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** forget to convert $\Delta\theta$ from radians to **arc-seconds** when reporting angular precision. Examiners specifically look for unit conversion.
> - Many students write $\sin\theta = h/L$ and *forget* the differential step for part (ii). Use $\cos\theta \cdot d\theta = dh/L$ explicitly.
> - Always state whether the **slip-gauge stack** is increasing or decreasing the angle. Here, $+0.025$ mm increases $h$ and hence *increases* the angle.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Internal Choice*

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for measuring an unknown angle of a workpiece using a sine bar. List any **four sources of error**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a sine bar with centre distance **200 mm**, calculate the slip-gauge height required to set the following angles, and determine which angle has the **highest angular sensitivity** (i.e., largest change in $\theta$ per mm change in $h$):
  (i) $\theta_1 = 5°$
  (ii) $\theta_2 = 25°$
  (iii) $\theta_3 = 45°$
  (iv) $\theta_4 = 60°$ **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — (a):**

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Clean the surface plate, sine bar, and rollers using a soft cloth.
2. Place the sine bar on the surface plate and rest the workpiece on its top.
3. Position a **dial test indicator (DTI)** on the workpiece's angled surface.
4. Insert slip gauges under one roller in **small increments**, sweeping the DTI across the workpiece surface each time.
5. Adjust until the DTI reads the **same value** at both ends of the angled surface (within ±0.001 mm) → confirms the workpiece surface is now parallel to the surface plate.
6. Compute $\theta = \sin^{-1}(h/L)$.
7. **Reverse the sine bar by 180°** and repeat; this eliminates any error due to the workpiece not being centred.

**Sources of Error (3 marks):**
1. Slip gauges not properly **wrung** together — stack length shorter than nominal.
2. **Rollers not of equal diameter** or worn — pivot line shifts.
3. Surface plate not perfectly **flat** — datum is tilted.
4. Sine bar **top surface** not parallel to the roller axis — introduces tilt error.
5. **Thermal expansion** of the bar/slip gauges — affects length $L$ and $h$.
6. **DTI calibration error** — false parallelism reading.

---

**Model Answer — (b):**

**Given:** $L = 200 \text{ mm}$

Using $h = L \sin \theta = 200 \sin \theta$:

| Angle | $\sin\theta$ | $h$ (mm) | Sensitivity $\dfrac{d\theta}{dh} = \dfrac{1}{L\cos\theta}$ (rad/mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5° | 0.08716 | **17.432** | $\dfrac{1}{200 \times 0.99619} = 5.020 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 25° | 0.42262 | **84.524** | $\dfrac{1}{200 \times 0.90631} = 5.518 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 45° | 0.70711 | **141.421** | $\dfrac{1}{200 \times 0.70711} = 7.071 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 60° | 0.86603 | **173.205** | $\dfrac{1}{200 \times 0.5} = 1.000 \times 10^{-2}$ |

**Highest sensitivity:** $\theta_4 = 60°$ gives $\dfrac{d\theta}{dh} = 0.01 \text{ rad/mm}$, which is the **largest**.

**Conclusion:** *Smaller angles have lower angular sensitivity* — a 1 mm error in slip-gauge height causes a much smaller angular error at 5° than at 60°.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Some students confuse *angle magnitude* with *sensitivity*. Smaller angles are *harder to detect changes in* — examiners explicitly test this concept.
> - Always show the **differential formula** for sensitivity derivation, not just the numeric answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Master formula:** $\sin\theta = h/L$ — non-negotiable, must be memorized.
- ✅ **For setting an angle:** $h = L\sin\theta$.
- ✅ **For measuring an angle:** $\theta = \sin^{-1}(h/L)$.
- ✅ **Roller diameters MUST be equal** — otherwise geometry fails.
- ✅ **Standard centre distances:** 100, 200, 300, 500 mm — choose the **longest** for highest accuracy (because $\Delta\theta \propto 1/L$).
- ✅ **Practical upper angle:** **45°** — above this, slip-gauge stack is unstable.
- ✅ **Always reverse the sine bar** (180° flip) to eliminate workpiece eccentricity.
- ✅ **Always wring slip gauges carefully** — they are the primary accuracy source.
- ✅ **Sensitivity increases with angle** (because $\cos\theta$ decreases); for very small angles (<5°), a sine bar is **less sensitive** — use a **clinometer or autocollimator** instead.
- ✅ **Top surface of bar must be parallel to roller axes** — checked during manufacture.
- ✅ **Differential relation for error analysis:** $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L\cos\theta}$ — convert radians to **arc-seconds** ($1 \text{ rad} = \frac{180 \times 3600}{\pi} \text{ arc-seconds}$).
- ✅ **Applications:** Taper turning setup, tool-grinder angles, dovetail inspection, angle gauge checking.
- ✅ **Compound sine bar** is used for **compound angles** (two angles in different planes) — remember it as an extension, not a separate instrument.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of screw thread and cutting tool angles using profile projector and tool makers microscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPEL507 - INDUSTRIAL INSPECTION AND QUALITY CONTROL LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of screw thread and cutting tool angles using profile projector and tool makers microscope # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Optical Metrology** is a branch of dimensional metrology in which the profile or magnified image of a component is projected onto a screen or observed through a microscope for non-contact, high-precision measurement of geometric features. The two principal optical instruments used in this experiment are: 1. **Profile Projector (Optical Comparator):** A non-contact optical measuring instrument that projects a precisely magnified silhouette of the workpiece onto a ground-glass viewing screen. The magnified image is compared against a master profile drawing or a coordinated measuring scale, enabling direct measurement of thread elements and tool angles. 2. **Tool Makers Microscope (TMM):** A specialized optical measuring instrument equipped with micrometric carriages, a protractor eyepiece, and illuminated cross-hairs designed specifically to measure the angles and dimensions of cutting tools, threaded components, and small precision parts. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** This experiment integrates two Course Outcomes — the application of optical projection principles (geometric magnification) and the interpretation of engineering drawings/specifications for thread and tool geometry. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Real-world Analogy — The Shadow Puppet Theatre:** Imagine sitting in a dark room with a single bright bulb behind you. When you raise your hand between the bulb and a white sheet on the wall, a giant, clearly visible shadow of your hand appears on the sheet. The *closer* your hand is to the light, the *bigger* the shadow on the wall. A **Profile Projector** works on exactly this principle — except instead of a hand, it is a tiny screw thread or cutting tool, and instead of a bulb, it uses a precisely collimated light source passing through precision lenses. A **Tool Makers Microscope** is more like a jeweller's loupe — a small, finely adjustable magnifying viewer with measurement scales etched into the lens, allowing the operator to see and measure the *exact same* minute features but in 3-D rather than as a shadow. - The Profile Projector gives a **2-D enlarged shadow** on a screen → best for thread profile outlines. - The Tool Makers Microscope gives a **3-D magnified view through eyepieces** → best for tool cutting edges and rake angles. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > - **Standard ISO Metric Thread Angle:** **60°** > - **Standard ACME Thread Angle:** **29°** > - **Standard Whitworth (BSW) Thread Angle:** **55°** > - **Standard Magnifications on Profile Projector:** **10×, 20×, 50×, 100×** > - **Standard Magnification on Tool Makers Microscope:** **30× (most common)** > - **Least Count of Micrometer Eyepiece of TMM:** **0.001 mm (1 µm)** typically, or **0.01 mm** for coarse instruments > - **Least Count of Protractor Eyepiece of TMM:** **1 minute of arc (1′ = 1/60°)** > - **Wavelength of green monochromatic light (mercury vapour lamp):** **546.1 nm** (often used for high-resolution optical projection) These values are **bold** because they are factual, exam-frequent, and must be memorized as KTU-ready constants. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Principles of Operation ### 2.1.1 Profile Projector — Optical Working Principle The profile projector converts a *real, illuminated object* into a *magnified, real image* on a screen. The light path is: 1. **Light Source** (tungsten-halogen or mercury vapour) → emits a diverging beam. 2. **Condenser Lens** → collimates/directs the beam uniformly onto the workpiece. 3. **Workpiece** → acts as an opaque obstacle; its profile becomes the "object." 4. **Objective Lens** → forms a real, inverted, magnified image of the workpiece profile. 5. **Projection Screen** (ground glass with cross-lines and chart holder) → displays the magnified image for direct comparison or measurement. The instrument is generally used in **episcopic illumination** (light reflected from the workpiece surface) for cutting tools, and in **diascopic illumination** (light transmitted through translucent workpieces) for thread profiles. ### 2.1.2 Tool Makers Microscope — Optical Working Principle The TMM uses a **compound microscope** with the following stages: 1. **Sub-stage Illuminator** → projects light upward through a glass scale (for linear measurements) or onto the cutting edge. 2. **Condenser + Objective Lens** → forms a magnified real image of the cutting edge or thread profile. 3. **Micrometer Eyepiece (Filar Micrometer)** → contains a movable cross-hair driven by a precisely calibrated drum, allowing measurement of small linear distances on the magnified image. 4. **Protractor Eyepiece (Goniometer Eyepiece)** → contains a rotatable reticle with an angular scale reading in **degrees and minutes**, used to measure angles directly (e.g., thread angle, rake angle). 5. **X-Y Micrometric Workstage** → the workpiece is mounted on a stage that can be translated in two perpendicular directions with micrometer screws for positioning and measuring large features. ## 2.2 Measurement Philosophy — Why Use Optical Methods? - **Non-contact:** The workpiece is not scratched or deformed; suitable for soft, polished, or hardened surfaces. - **Magnification:** Sub-millimetre features become visible and measurable. - **Comparison method:** The magnified profile can be superimposed on a master drawing (typically 10× or 50× the actual size) for go/no-go judgement. - **Versatility:** A single instrument can measure threads, gears, tools, press tools, punches, and small components. - **High repeatability:** Visual alignment and optical magnification reduce human parallax errors. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are verified from standard metrology references. Memorize these for KTU ESE.** | # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Linear Magnification of Projector | $M = \dfrac{h_i}{h_o} = \dfrac{v}{u} = \dfrac{D}{f_o} - 1$ | dimensionless | $D$ = projection distance, $f_o$ = focal length of objective | | 2 | Linear Magnification (approx., when $D \gg f_o$) | $M \approx \dfrac{D}{f_o}$ | dimensionless | Simplification valid for standard projectors | | 3 | True Dimension from Screen Measurement | $\text{True size} = \dfrac{\text{Screen measurement}}{M}$ | mm | Divide the observed value by magnification | | 4 | Thread Pitch (P) | $P = \dfrac{\text{Axial distance over n threads}}{n}$ | mm | For metric ISO, $P = 1/TPI$ for inch threads | | 5 | Thread Helix Angle ($\lambda$) | $\tan \lambda = \dfrac{P}{\pi d_2}$ | degrees | $d_2$ = pitch diameter | | 6 | Lead of Single-start Thread | $L = P$ | mm | For multi-start: $L = n \times P$ | | 7 | Effective/Pitch Diameter ($d_2$) | $d_2 = d - 0.6495 \cdot P$ (metric ISO) | mm | $d$ = major diameter | | 8 | Minor Diameter ($d_1$) | $d_1 = d - 1.0825 \cdot P$ (metric ISO) | mm | | | 9 | Depth of Thread ($h$) | $h = 0.6134 \cdot P$ (metric ISO) | mm | | | 10 | Rake Angle ($\gamma$) | Measured directly on protractor eyepiece | degrees | | | 11 | Clearance Angle ($\alpha$) | $\alpha = 90° - \gamma - \beta$ (often given) | degrees | $\beta$ = wedge angle | | 12 | Magnification of TMM | $M_{TMM} = 30\times$ (typical) | dimensionless | Eyepiece $\times$ Objective | | 13 | Least Count of Filar Micrometer | $LC = \dfrac{\text{Pitch of screw}}{\text{Number of drum divisions}}$ | mm | Typically 0.001 mm | | 14 | Angular Least Count (Protractor) | $1' = \dfrac{1}{60}°$ | degree/minute | $1° = 60'$ | > [!NOTE] > **Pitfall Avoidance Tip:** In the thread formulas above, the constants **0.6495, 1.0825, and 0.6134** are derived from the geometry of a 60° V-thread with flat crests and roots. They are valid **only** for standard ISO metric threads. Never apply them to ACME (29°) or Whitworth (55°) threads. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Industry:** 100% inspection of crankshafts, camshafts, gear teeth, and threaded fasteners before assembly. - **Tool Room:** Verification of newly ground cutting tools (drills, milling cutters, single-point tools) against design drawings. - **Aerospace:** Inspection of safety-critical threaded fasteners and turbine blade profiles where dimensional accuracy is life-critical. - **Mass Production:** Quick optical comparison of components against 10×/50× master overlays, replacing slow manual gauging. - **Reverse Engineering:** Determining the pitch and angle of unknown threads on legacy or worn components. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Magnification Formula of a Profile Projector The optical projection system of a profile projector is essentially a **single thin convex lens** (the objective) that forms a real, inverted, magnified image of a brightly illuminated object on a distant screen. Using the **thin lens equation**: $$ \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f_o} $$ Where: - $v$ = image distance (lens to screen) - $u$ = object distance (lens to workpiece, with sign convention: negative on object side) - $f_o$ = focal length of the objective lens The **linear magnification** is: $$ M = \frac{h_i}{h_o} = \frac{v}{u} $$ Now, applying the **Newtonian lens form** (or by algebraic manipulation from the lens equation), the magnification can be expressed purely in terms of image distance and focal length. For a projector where the object is placed *just beyond* the focal point (so that a real, magnified image is formed on a far screen), we have: $$ \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f_o} + \frac{1}{u} $$ For projection where the object is very close to the focal point ($u \approx -f_o$), the image distance $v$ becomes very large, and the magnification becomes: $$ M = \frac{v}{u} \approx -\frac{v}{f_o} $$ Since the magnitude is what matters in metrology, and using $D$ as the projection throw distance (lens-to-screen), we get the **standard profile projector formula**: $$ \boxed{\,M = \frac{D}{f_o}\,} $$ This shows that magnification is **inversely proportional to focal length** and **directly proportional to throw distance**. In practice, manufacturers offer swappable objective lenses (10×, 20×, 50×, 100×) by changing $f_o$, and the throw $D$ is fixed by the cabinet geometry. ## 3.2 Derivation of Effective Diameter for a 60° Metric ISO Thread Consider an ISO metric thread with major diameter $d$ and pitch $P$. By ISO standard, the thread has: - A symmetrical V-profile with included angle $\alpha = 60°$ - Flat crests and roots, each occupying **1/8 of the pitch** (i.e., crest truncation $H/8$ and root truncation $H/8$, where $H$ is the height of the fundamental triangle). The height of the **fundamental triangle** (sharp V before truncation) is: $$ H = \frac{P}{2 \tan(\alpha/2)} = \frac{P}{2 \tan 30°} = \frac{P}{2 \times 0.5774} = 0.8660 \cdot P $$ The **actual thread depth** $h$ is the height of the fundamental triangle minus the crest and root truncations: $$ h = H - \frac{H}{8} - \frac{H}{8} = \frac{3H}{4} = \frac{3 \times 0.8660 \cdot P}{4} = 0.6495 \cdot P $$ The **pitch diameter** $d_2$ is the major diameter minus the depth from the crest to the pitch line. The pitch line lies **midway** between the crest and the root, so the depth from crest to pitch line is $h/2$: $$ d_2 = d - \frac{h}{2} = d - \frac{0.6495 \cdot P}{2} = d - 0.6495 \cdot P \quad \text{(after correction)} $$ > [!NOTE] > The textbook value of $0.6495 P$ already includes this $\times 2$ factor (it is $2 \times 0.32475$), and the **minor diameter** is: > $$ > d_1 = d - 2h = d - 2 \times 0.6495 \cdot P > $$ > However, the standard ISO formula commonly cited in metrology textbooks is: > $$ > d_2 = d - 0.6495 \cdot P, \quad d_1 = d - 1.0825 \cdot P > $$ > (with $1.0825 = \frac{5}{4} \times 0.8660$, accounting for clearance at the root). ## 3.3 Measurement Procedure — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix ### Part A: Screw Thread Profile Measurement on a Profile Projector | Step | Action | Theoretical Basis | Observation/Reading | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Switch on the projector; allow 5–10 min warm-up. | Stabilises lamp output and lens temperature. | Lamp stable. | | 2 | Select appropriate magnification (usually 10× or 20× for thread). | $M = D / f_o$; choose based on screen size and required resolution. | Magnification set: e.g., 20×. | | 3 | Place the **10× (or 20×) master thread profile chart** on the screen. | Standard charts are 10× or 50× the actual size. | Chart aligned on screen. | | 4 | Mount the threaded specimen on the workstage between centres or on a V-block. | Ensures stable, repeatable positioning. | Specimen clamped. | | 5 | Adjust illumination: diascopic for translucent thread; episcopic for opaque. | Diascopic produces a sharp thread outline. | Image sharp on screen. | | 6 | Focus the objective lens; centre the thread image on the screen. | Reduces parallax and centring error. | Image centred and focused. | | 7 | **Measure major diameter ($d$):** Use the cross-hair lines on the screen to touch the crests of two opposite thread flanks. Multiply screen reading by the inverse of magnification: $d = \text{reading} / M$. | Optical magnification divides actual size. | $d$ recorded. | | 8 | **Measure minor diameter ($d_1$):** Shift cross-hair to the root of the thread profile. Read from screen scale. | | $d_1$ recorded. | | 9 | **Measure pitch ($P$):** Move the X-axis micrometer (or screen cross-hair) across $n$ consecutive thread spacings and divide. | $P = \dfrac{\Delta x_{\text{screen}}}{n \cdot M}$ | $P$ recorded. | | 10 | **Measure thread angle ($\alpha$):** Use the protractor screen overlay or rotate the protractor eyepiece (if fitted) to align with the two flanks. Read the included angle. | For ISO metric, expected value: **60°**. | $\alpha$ recorded. | | 11 | Repeat measurements 3 times; tabulate mean and standard deviation. | Statistical reduction of random error. | Mean and SD computed. | ### Part B: Cutting Tool Angle Measurement on a Tool Makers Microscope | Step | Action | Theoretical Basis | Observation/Reading | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Switch on TMM; allow warm-up. | Lamp and optical stability. | Lamp stable. | | 2 | Mount the cutting tool on a special tool holder or V-block on the X-Y stage. | Stable, repeatable orientation. | Tool mounted. | | 3 | Focus the microscope on the cutting edge using the focusing knob. | Clear, sharp image of cutting edge. | Edge in focus. | | 4 | **Measure Rake Angle ($\gamma$):** Align the cross-hair along the cutting edge. Rotate the protractor eyepiece to align the angular scale zero with the tool reference surface (shank). Read the angle the cross-hair makes with the tool face. | Rake angle is the angle between the rake face and the tool shank (reference). | $\gamma$ recorded in degrees and minutes. | | 5 | **Measure Clearance (Relief) Angle ($\alpha$):** With the protractor eyepiece, align the cross-hair along the flank (clearance face) of the tool. Read angle from the workpiece reference. | Clearance angle is between the flank and the workpiece. | $\alpha$ recorded. | | 6 | **Measure Cutting Edge Angle ($\lambda$ or $K_r$):** Project the cutting edge onto the tool base plane. Measure the angle between the cutting edge and the side of the tool shank. | Defined in the tool base (XY) plane. | $\lambda$ recorded. | | 7 | **Measure Nose Radius ($r$):** Use the filar micrometer eyepiece. Align the movable cross-hair with one end of the nose arc, then with the other. The drum reading gives the magnified chord length. Divide by magnification (typically 30×) to get true nose radius. | $r = \dfrac{\text{Drum reading}}{M_{TMM}}$ | $r$ recorded in mm. | | 8 | Repeat and tabulate; compare with design specifications. | Tool specification verification. | Final report compiled. | ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical) **Problem:** On a 20× profile projector, the magnified thread profile of an M20×2.5 ISO metric bolt shows a major-diameter screen span of **400 mm** and a minor-diameter span of **351.25 mm**. The screen reading for **5 consecutive pitches** is **125 mm**. Compute the true major diameter, true minor diameter, and the pitch. Compare with theoretical values. **Solution:** Magnification $M = 20$. True major diameter: $$ d = \frac{400 \text{ mm}}{20} = 20.00 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark $$ True minor diameter: $$ d_1 = \frac{351.25 \text{ mm}}{20} = 17.5625 \text{ mm} $$ Theoretical minor diameter for M20×2.5: $$ d_1 = d - 1.0825 \cdot P = 20 - 1.0825 \times 2.5 = 20 - 2.7063 = 17.2937 \text{ mm} $$ The observed value (17.5625) is slightly higher than the theoretical root, which is acceptable because the **truncation allowance** at the root (H/8) leaves a flat root — actual $d_1$ (root) is larger than the sharp V calculation predicts. True pitch: $$ P = \frac{125 \text{ mm}}{5 \times 20} = \frac{125}{100} = 1.25 \text{ mm} $$ The measured pitch of 1.25 mm does **not** match the bolt designation M20×2.5 (which should have $P = 2.5$ mm). This indicates that the **measured specimen is actually an M20×1.25 fine-pitch bolt**, not the standard 2.5 mm coarse pitch. The student has correctly identified a **discrepancy** through measurement. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of Profile Projector — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Light Source
Tungsten-Halogen or Mercury Vapour] --> B[Condenser Lens
Collimates Light] B --> C[Workpiece
Screw Thread or Tool Profile] C --> D[Objective Lens
Magnification 10x to 100x] D --> E[Mirror or Direct Path] E --> F[Projection Screen
Ground Glass with Cross-Hair and Chart Holder] F --> G[Operator Eye and Comparison Chart] F --> H[X-Y Workstage
Micrometric Movement] H --> C style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram of Tool Makers Microscope — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Sub-Stage Illuminator
Adjustable Lamp] --> B[Condenser Lens
Directs Light Upward] B --> C[Workpiece
Cutting Tool on V-Block or Holder] C --> D[Objective Lens
Magnification e.g. 3x or 5x] D --> E[Beam Splitter and Internal Prism System] E --> F1[Protractor Eyepiece
Goniometer Reticule 0 to 360 deg] E --> F2[Filar Micrometer Eyepiece
Movable Cross-Hair and Drum] F1 --> G[Operator Eye Angular Measurement] F2 --> H[Operator Eye Linear Measurement] C --> I[X-Y Micrometric Workstage
Linear Translation] I --> J[Micrometer Drum Readouts
X and Y axes] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Profile Projector vs. Tool Makers Microscope ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PROJ[Profile Projector] P1[2D Silhouette Projection] --> P2[Best for Thread Profile Outlines] P2 --> P3[Comparison Against 10x or 50x Master Chart] end subgraph TMM[Tool Makers Microscope] T1[3D Magnified Direct View] --> T2[Best for Cutting Tool Edge Geometry] T2 --> T3[Direct Angle Measurement via Protractor Eyepiece] end PROJ -.Alternative Instrument.-> TMM TMM -.Alternative Instrument.-> PROJ style PROJ fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#006666,stroke-width:2px style TMM fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Process Flow — Measurement Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Warm-up the Instrument] --> S2[Step 2: Select Magnification and Mount Specimen] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Adjust Illumination and Focus] S3 --> S4{Choose Measurement Type} S4 -->|Thread| S5[Measure Major, Minor, Pitch, Angle] S4 -->|Tool| S6[Measure Rake, Clearance, Cutting Edge, Nose Radius] S5 --> S7[Tabulate, Repeat 3 times, Compute Mean and SD] S6 --> S7 S7 --> S8[Compare with Theoretical or Specification Values] S8 --> S9[Conclude on Acceptance or Rejection] style S4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S8 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Profile Projector. State any two applications. (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** **Model Answer:** A profile projector (also called an optical comparator) is a non-contact measuring instrument that projects a precisely magnified silhouette of a workpiece onto a ground-glass screen for visual comparison and measurement. It uses a light source, condenser, objective lens, and a viewing screen. The magnification is determined by the focal length of the objective and the throw distance to the screen. **Two Applications:** 1. Measurement of screw thread elements (major diameter, minor diameter, pitch, thread angle) by comparison with a master profile drawing. 2. Inspection of complex profiles of cutting tools, press tools, gears, and stampings against enlarged master charts. --- ### Question 2: List the various angles measured on a single-point cutting tool using a Tool Makers Microscope. (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Model Answer:** The various cutting tool angles measured on a Tool Makers Microscope are: 1. **Rake angle ($\gamma$)** — angle between the rake face and the tool shank reference. 2. **Clearance angle / Relief angle ($\alpha$)** — angle between the flank and the workpiece surface. 3. **Cutting edge angle ($\lambda$ or $K_r$)** — angle between the cutting edge and the side of the tool shank (measured in the tool base plane). 4. **Side cutting edge angle and nose angle** (in some multi-angle tools). 5. **Nose radius ($r$)** — measured using the filar micrometer eyepiece. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a profile projector. State the formula for linear magnification and explain how it is achieved in practice. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the procedure to measure the major diameter, minor diameter, pitch, and thread angle of a metric ISO screw thread on a profile projector. Mention the standard chart used and the formula relating screen reading to actual size. **(7 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Working Principle of a Profile Projector (7 Marks):** A profile projector works on the principle of **shadow projection** (optical magnification). A bright, uniform light source emits light that is collimated by a **condenser lens** and passed through (diascopic) or reflected from (episcopic) the workpiece. The **objective lens** then forms a **real, inverted, magnified image** of the workpiece profile on a **ground-glass screen**. **Block diagram (as in Section 4.1):** Light Source → Condenser → Workpiece → Objective → Screen. **Magnification formula:** $$ M = \frac{D}{f_o} $$ where $D$ is the throw distance (lens-to-screen) and $f_o$ is the focal length of the objective lens. In practice, magnification is changed by **interchanging objective lenses** of different focal lengths (e.g., 10×, 20×, 50×, 100×). The throw $D$ is fixed by the cabinet. The true size of any feature is obtained as: $$ \text{True size} = \frac{\text{Screen measurement}}{M} $$ **(b) Procedure to Measure Thread Elements (7 Marks):** 1. **Mounting:** Place the threaded specimen on a V-block or between centres on the workstage. 2. **Chart Selection:** Use a **10× or 50× master thread profile chart** corresponding to the thread under test. Place the chart on the screen holder. 3. **Magnification:** Select magnification (usually 10× or 20×) and **align the projected image with the master chart** by translating the workstage. 4. **Major diameter ($d$):** Align the screen cross-hair with the crests of two opposite thread flanks. Record screen reading. Compute: $d = \text{reading} / M$. 5. **Minor diameter ($d_1$):** Move cross-hair to the root of the thread. Record and divide by $M$. 6. **Pitch ($P$):** Translate the workstage (or cross-hair) across $n$ thread spacings. Compute: $P = (\text{axial reading on screen}) / (n \times M)$. 7. **Thread angle ($\alpha$):** Use the **protractor screen overlay** (or protractor eyepiece in TMM) to measure the included angle between the two flanks. For ISO metric, expected value = **60°**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a Tool Makers Microscope with a neat sketch. State the function of the protractor eyepiece and the filar micrometer eyepiece. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the measurement procedure for the **rake angle, clearance angle, cutting edge angle, and nose radius** of a single-point cutting tool on a Tool Makers Microscope. **(7 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction and Working of a Tool Makers Microscope (7 Marks):** A Tool Makers Microscope is a compound microscope-based measuring instrument. Its main components are: 1. **Base** with a heavy, stable casting to damp vibrations. 2. **Sub-stage illuminator** — adjustable lamp providing uniform light. 3. **X-Y micrometric workstage** — translates the workpiece in two perpendicular directions; micrometers give readings typically to 0.01 mm or 0.001 mm. 4. **Objective lens** — typically 3× or 5×, providing the primary magnification. 5. **Beam-splitting prism system** — directs light to both the protractor and filar eyepieces. 6. **Protractor eyepiece (goniometer eyepiece)** — contains a rotatable reticle with an angular scale graduated in **degrees and minutes** (least count **1′**). Used for direct angle measurement. 7. **Filar micrometer eyepiece** — has a fixed cross-hair and a movable cross-hair driven by a precision screw. The drum gives linear readings to **0.001 mm** (least count). The microscope forms a magnified image of the cutting edge in the eyepiece plane, where the reticles allow precise alignment. **(b) Measurement Procedure (7 Marks):** 1. **Mount** the tool on a special tool holder ensuring the cutting edge is in the optical axis. 2. **Focus** the microscope on the cutting edge. 3. **Rake angle ($\gamma$):** Align the protractor reticle cross-hair with the rake face. Read the angle relative to the tool shank (reference) directly. 4. **Clearance angle ($\alpha$):** Align the cross-hair with the flank (clearance face). Read the angle. 5. **Cutting edge angle ($\lambda$):** Project the cutting edge onto the tool base plane (by rotating the tool). Measure the angle between the edge and the shank side. 6. **Nose radius ($r$):** Use the filar micrometer to measure the magnified chord length of the nose arc: $$ r = \frac{\text{Drum reading}}{M_{TMM}} $$ where $M_{TMM}$ is the total magnification (objective × eyepiece, typically 30×). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistake Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to divide by magnification.** A student records "0.6 mm" as the nose radius when the drum actually read 18 mm and the magnification was 30×. Always divide screen/drum readings by $M$ to get the true value. > 2. **Confusing filar micrometer least count with stage micrometer least count.** The filar micrometer is built into the eyepiece; the stage micrometer is on the worktable. > 3. **Mixing up thread angle conventions.** ISO = 60°, ACME = 29°, Whitworth = 55°. The question may show a Whitworth thread and you must NOT use the ISO formula $d_1 = d - 1.0825 P$. > 4. **Not mentioning warm-up time.** Examiners specifically award 0.5–1 mark for stating that the lamp should be warmed up for 5–10 minutes before measurement. > 5. **Using diasopic instead of episcopic illumination (or vice versa) for a given workpiece.** Diascopic (transmitted) is for thin/translucent parts or thread profile outlines; episcopic (reflected) is for opaque cutting tools. The examiner will look for this distinction. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE** - **Profile Projector** = 2-D shadow projection; uses a screen; best for thread outlines; magnification by interchangeable objectives (10×, 20×, 50×, 100×); standard charts are 10× or 50× the actual size. - **Tool Makers Microscope** = 3-D compound microscope; uses eyepieces; best for tool edge geometry; has protractor eyepiece (LC = 1 minute) and filar micrometer (LC = 0.001 mm). - **Magnification formula** (Profile Projector): $M = D / f_o$. - **True size from screen** = Screen reading ÷ $M$. - **Standard thread angles** — ISO: **60°**; ACME: **29°**; Whitworth: **55°**. - **Pitch formulas** for ISO metric — $d_2 = d - 0.6495 P$; $d_1 = d - 1.0825 P$; $h = 0.6134 P$. - **Tool angles to measure** — Rake ($\gamma$), Clearance ($\alpha$), Cutting edge angle ($\lambda$ or $K_r$), Nose radius ($r$). - **Nose radius on TMM** — $r = \text{Drum reading} / M_{TMM}$. - **Illumination types** — Diascopic (transmitted, for threads); Episcopic (reflected, for tools). - **Always warm up the lamp** for 5–10 minutes before measurement. - **Repeat measurements** at least three times and report the mean and standard deviation. - **Least counts to memorize** — Protractor eyepiece: 1 minute of arc; Filar micrometer: 0.001 mm (1 µm); Standard stage micrometer: 0.01 mm. - **Comparison method** is the heart of optical metrology — the magnified profile is overlaid on a master drawing. - **Linear magnification of TMM** is typically 30× (objective 3× × eyepiece 10×) but can vary. - **For ACME threads**, depth is half the pitch; for ISO, depth is $0.6134 P$. - **The thread helix angle** is given by $\tan \lambda = P / (\pi d_2)$ — useful for multi-start threads and lead measurements.

topics.title: Measurement of dimensional and geometrical features using CMM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL507 - INDUSTRIAL INSPECTION AND QUALITY CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of dimensional and geometrical features using CMM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM)** is a precision metrological instrument that measures the **geometry of physical objects** by sensing discrete points on the workpiece surface using a probe. The three-dimensional coordinates $(x, y, z)$ of these probed points are captured relative to a fixed reference frame, and dedicated software uses these point clouds to evaluate **dimensional** (size-related) and **geometrical** (form, orientation, location, run-out) features in accordance with **ISO 10360** performance standards and **ASME Y14.5 / ISO 1101** Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing (GD&T) specifications.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology for PCPEL507, a CMM is defined as a **three-axis displacement-measuring system** in which the probe tip position is resolved in **Cartesian coordinates** with traceability to national/international length standards, allowing substitution of conventional gauges (vernier, micrometer, dial indicators, height gauge) by a single programmable, multi-feature inspection station.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A CMM is *not* a comparator — it is an **absolute** measuring system because every probed point is referenced to a calibrated **datum system** (typically a precision granite table or kinematic nest) and not to a master part.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded in a room and asked to describe a sculpture. You are given a thin stick and told: *every time the stick touches the sculpture, note the (left-right, forward-back, up-down) position of your hand and write it down*. After 200 such touches, you can mathematically reconstruct the entire shape of the sculpture on a computer.

- The **room** = the calibrated CMM envelope.
- **The stick** = the probe (touch-trigger, scanning, or optical).
- **Your hand position** = the read-head outputs of the three linear scales (X, Y, Z).
- **The notebook + reconstruction software** = the CMM controller and metrology software (e.g., CALYPSO, PC-DMIS, QUINDOS, Metrosoft CM).

Now, instead of merely listing points, the software can:
1. **Fit** a line, plane, circle, cylinder, cone, or sphere through the points (least-squares or min-max / Chebyshev).
2. **Compare** the actual fitted feature against the **nominal CAD model** with applied GD&T tolerances.
3. Generate a colour-coded deviation report (green = in tolerance, red = out of tolerance).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CMMs dominate modern inspection:** A single CMM replaces **30–50 conventional gauges**, requires no re-fixturing between features, and produces a **digital inspection report** that is auditable, archivable, and directly comparable with the original CAD nominal — fulfilling the Industry 4.0 closed-loop inspection paradigm.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in CMM Inspection

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Value | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Length Unit | L | **millimetre (mm)** or micrometre (µm) | SI base unit |
| Linear Resolution | $r_L$ | **0.1 µm – 1 µm** | ISO 10360-2 |
| Volumetric Accuracy | $E_{0,MPE}$ | **1.9 + L/333 µm** (e.g., Zeiss Prismo) | ISO 10360-2 |
| Probe Accuracy | $P_{Form,MPE}$ | **1.5 – 2.5 µm** | ISO 10360-5 |
| Scanning Probe Point Rate | $f_p$ | **100 – 2000 points/sec** | Manufacturer datasheet |
| Working envelope | $X \times Y \times Z$ | **e.g., 700 × 1000 × 600 mm** | Site selection spec |
| Ambient temperature | $T$ | **(20 ± 0.5) °C** | ISO 1 / VDI/VDE 2617 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of a CMM

A CMM is composed of the following functional sub-systems, each of which contributes to the overall measurement chain:

1. **Machine Structure (Mechanical Frame)**
   - Provides the rigid reference skeleton.
   - Common configurations:
     - **Fixed Bridge** (best accuracy, used in labs).
     - **Moving Bridge** (most common, floor-mounted, table travels Y).
     - **Cantilever** (good X-axis access, used for large sheet-metal parts).
     - **Column / Horizontal Arm** (very large parts, e.g., car body-in-white).
     - **Gantry** (extra-large parts, e.g., aerospace wing panels).
   - Material: **granite base (low thermal expansion ≈ 5 × 10⁻⁶ /°C)**, aluminium/ceramic bridge (lightweight + high stiffness-to-mass ratio).
   - Guiding: **air bearings** (frictionless, no wear, no lubrication).

2. **Scale / Length-measuring System (Three Linear Axes)**
   - **Optical linear encoders** (Heidenhain, Renishaw) — resolution up to **0.01 µm**.
   - **Laser interferometers** — used for periodic volumetric calibration.
   - Operating principle: scale grating pitch × interpolation factor.

3. **Probe System (The "Sense" of the CMM)**
   - **Touch-Trigger Probing (TTP)** — discrete on/off contact, used for low-to-medium point density.
   - **Scanning Probing** — continuous analogue output, 1000+ points/s, used for free-form surfaces.
   - **Optical / Vision Probes** — non-contact, used for soft, fragile, or micro-features.
   - **Multi-Sensor Carriages** — combined TTP + laser + camera heads.

4. **Controller & Software**
   - Real-time servo control (DC or linear motors with PID loop).
   - CAD-based offline programming (DMIS, I++ DME compliant).
   - Feature extraction and GD&T evaluation algorithms (Gauss–Newton least-squares, L₁ norm, Chebyshev min-max).

5. **Fixturing & Datum Reference Frame (DRF)**
   - Modular fixtures, vacuum chucks, kinematic nests.
   - Establishes the **3-2-1 locating principle** on the part.

### 2.2 Axis Conventions and the Datuming Hierarchy

The CMM coordinate system follows the **right-hand rule**:

$$
\vec{Z} \;\; \uparrow \text{ (perpendicular to granite table, vertical)}
$$
$$
\vec{X} \;\; \rightarrow \text{ (longest horizontal travel, "primary") }
$$
$$
\vec{Y} \;\; \text{ (secondary horizontal, completes the triad)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Datuming Logic (3-2-1 Principle):**
> - **Primary datum (3 contact points)** → constrains 3 DOF (1 translation + 2 rotations).
> - **Secondary datum (2 contact points)** → constrains 2 more DOF.
> - **Tertiary datum (1 contact point)** → constrains the final 1 DOF.
> Total = **6 DOF constrained** ⇒ the part is uniquely positioned.

### 2.3 Measurement Procedure — The Operational Workflow

The full CMM measurement cycle for a typical PCPEL507 lab part is:

**Step 1 — Warm-up & Environmental Stabilisation**
Allow the machine, master artefacts, and the workpiece to reach thermal equilibrium at **(20 ± 0.5) °C** for a minimum of **4 hours** to minimise thermal drift error.

**Step 2 — Calibration of Probe and Scales**
- Probe qualification: probe a **calibrated reference sphere** (typically Ø 25 mm ruby sphere) from multiple angles to compute the **effective probe diameter** and **stylus tip form deviation**.
- Scale verification: traverse a **step gauge or laser interferometer** to verify the **MPE (Maximum Permissible Error)** envelope.

**Step 3 — Part Loading and Datum Alignment**
- Place the part on the fixture.
- Manually probe 3 points on plane A (Primary Datum), 2 points on plane B (Secondary), 1 point on plane C (Tertiary).
- Software computes the **best-fit datum reference frame (DRF)**.

**Step 4 — Feature Measurement**
- Dimensional features: measure 3–4 points to fit the element (line, circle, plane, cylinder).
- Geometrical features: probe ≥ 8–12 points distributed evenly, capture the full geometry.

**Step 5 — Mathematical Fitting**
- Apply **least-squares** (minimises sum of squared deviations) or **min-max Chebyshev** (minimises the maximum deviation, used for "as small as possible" tolerance zones).

**Step 6 — GD&T Evaluation and Report**
- Compare the fitted feature to the nominal CAD.
- Output pass/fail for each dimensional and geometrical tolerance.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Equation | Explanation | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Distance between two probed points | $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2 + (z_2 - z_1)^2}$ | Euclidean 3-D distance formula | mm |
| 2 | Least-squares plane residual | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(a x_i + b y_i + c z_i - d)^2$ | $S$ is minimised w.r.t. $(a,b,c,d)$ subject to $a^2+b^2+c^2=1$ | mm² |
| 3 | Least-squares circle (2-D) | $\min \sum (r_i - R)^2$ | $R$ = fitted radius, $r_i$ = point-to-centre distance | mm |
| 4 | Flatness tolerance | $t_{flat} = z_{max} - z_{min}$ (after best-fit plane subtraction) | Parallel planes enveloping all points, separation = flatness | mm |
| 5 | Cylindricity tolerance | $t_{cyl} = 2(r_{max} - r_{min})$ along the axis | Two coaxial cylinders enveloping all points | mm |
| 6 | Perpendicularity | $t_{\perp} = \vert \theta - 90° \vert \cdot L$ | Angular deviation × reference length | mm |
| 7 | Position tolerance (diameter zone) | $t_{pos} = 2 \cdot d_{max\,offset}$ | Diameter of the cylindrical tolerance zone centred on the true position | mm |
| 8 | Linear encoder resolution | $r = p / k$ | $p$ = grating pitch (e.g., 20 µm), $k$ = electronic interpolation factor (e.g., 400) → $r = 0.05$ µm | µm |
| 9 | Effective probe diameter | $D_{eff} = D_{cal} + \Delta_{form}$ | Adds form deviation to calibrated tip diameter | mm |
| 10 | Combined standard uncertainty (GUM) | $u_c = \sqrt{u_1^2 + u_2^2 + \cdots + u_n^2}$ | Root-sum-squares of independent uncertainty sources | µm |
| 11 | Expanded uncertainty (95% CL) | $U = k \cdot u_c$ with $k = 2$ | KTU board expects $k=2$ unless specified | µm |
| 12 | Volumetric MPE (ISO 10360-2) | $E_{0,MPE} = A + B \cdot L$ | $A$ = constant term (µm), $B$ = length-dependent term (µm/mm), $L$ = measured length in mm | µm |
| 13 | Thermal expansion correction | $L_T = L_{20}\,[1 + \alpha\,(T - 20)]$ | $\alpha$ = CTE of workpiece; for steel $\alpha \approx 11.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C | mm |
| 14 | Probe-form MPE (ISO 10360-5) | $P_{Form,MPE}$ | Pre-travel + form + size error of probe | µm |
| 15 | True position radial offset | $\Delta r = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ | Used in 2-D position tolerance | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always use **mm** for dimensions in dimensional tolerance and **µm** for form / orientation / location tolerances. Mixing units is the single most common mistake in CMM-based numerical answers.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** First-article inspection of turbine blades (free-form surfaces, ± 5 µm form tolerance).
- **Automotive:** Body-in-white sub-assemblies (CMM-in-line on gantry, 100% inspection every 8 sec).
- **Medical implants:** Knee / hip prosthesis dimensional conformity.
- **Tool room:** Reverse engineering and wear analysis.
- **KTU PCPEL507 lab context:** Manual measurement of a prismatic or rotationally symmetric component (shaft, block, gear blank) for academic demonstration of GD&T concepts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Euclidean Distance Between Two Probed Points

This is the most fundamental equation used in CMM dimensional measurement (length, width, height, diameter by 2-point method).

**Statement:** If the CMM software captures points $P_1(x_1, y_1, z_1)$ and $P_2(x_2, y_2, z_2)$, the true straight-line distance between them is given by the 3-D Euclidean norm.

**Derivation (from Pythagoras extended to 3-D):**

In the X-Y plane, the projection of the segment is:
$$
d_{xy} = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}
$$

Now the full 3-D segment forms a right triangle in the X-Y-Z space with $d_{xy}$ as one leg and $(z_2 - z_1)$ as the other. By Pythagoras:
$$
d = \sqrt{d_{xy}^{\,2} + (z_2 - z_1)^2}
$$

Substituting the expression for $d_{xy}$:
$$
\boxed{\,d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2 + (z_2 - z_1)^2}\,}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
Probed coordinates: $P_1 = (10.000, 5.000, 0.000)$ mm, $P_2 = (75.000, 5.000, 12.000)$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x &= 75.000 - 10.000 = 65.000 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta y &= 5.000 - 5.000 = 0.000 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta z &= 12.000 - 0.000 = 12.000 \text{ mm} \\
d &= \sqrt{(65.000)^2 + (0.000)^2 + (12.000)^2} \\
  &= \sqrt{4225.000 + 0.000 + 144.000} \\
  &= \sqrt{4369.000} \\
d &= 66.0985 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Least-Squares Best-Fit Circle (Radius and Centre)

When $n$ points are probed on a circular feature, the CMM software fits a circle of centre $(x_c, y_c)$ and radius $R$ that minimises:
$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(r_i - R\bigr)^2 \quad \text{where} \quad r_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_c)^2 + (y_i - y_c)^2}
$$

**Optimality conditions** (Gauss–Newton linearisation, dropping the small second-order term $\frac{(r_i - R)}{r_i}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial S}{\partial x_c} &= -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n}(r_i - R)\,\frac{(x_i - x_c)}{r_i} = 0 \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial S}{\partial y_c} &= -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n}(r_i - R)\,\frac{(y_i - y_c)}{r_i} = 0 \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial S}{\partial R}    &= -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n}(r_i - R) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The third equation yields:
$$
R = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i
$$

That is, the **least-squares radius is the arithmetic mean of the individual point-to-centre distances**. The first two equations give a 2 × 2 linear system for $(x_c, y_c)$ that the CMM controller solves in real time.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Form Deviation of a Plane (Flatness)

For a best-fit plane $a x + b y + c z = d$ (with $a^2+b^2+c^2=1$), the signed residual of point $i$ is:
$$
\varepsilon_i = a x_i + b y_i + c z_i - d
$$

The **flatness deviation** is the peak-to-valley range:
$$
t_{flat} = \max_i(\varepsilon_i) - \min_i(\varepsilon_i)
$$

**Numerical example:** Five probed points on a nominally flat surface (mm):
$(0, 0, 0.000)$, $(50, 0, 0.012)$, $(0, 50, 0.005)$, $(50, 50, 0.020)$, $(25, 25, 0.008)$.

Assume the best-fit plane equation is computed as $z = 0.000\,x + 0.000\,y + 0.009$ (mm, for simplicity). The residuals are:
$\varepsilon = [-0.009, +0.003, -0.004, +0.011, -0.001]$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max(\varepsilon) &= +0.011 \text{ mm} \\
\min(\varepsilon) &= -0.009 \text{ mm} \\
t_{flat} &= 0.011 - (-0.009) = 0.020 \text{ mm} = 20 \;\mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Conclusion: The flatness deviation is **20 µm**. If the drawing specifies a flatness tolerance of 30 µm, the part is **in spec**; if 15 µm, it is **out of spec**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Combined Uncertainty (GUM Method)

For a CMM measurement, the major uncertainty sources are:

| Source | Symbol | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Length-measuring system | $u_{LMS}$ | 0.5 µm |
| Probe form / pre-travel | $u_{probe}$ | 0.8 µm |
| Thermal effects | $u_{therm}$ | 0.6 µm |
| Form of the workpiece | $u_{form}$ | 0.4 µm |
| Datum establishment | $u_{datum}$ | 0.5 µm |

**Combined standard uncertainty:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
u_c &= \sqrt{u_{LMS}^2 + u_{probe}^2 + u_{therm}^2 + u_{form}^2 + u_{datum}^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{(0.5)^2 + (0.8)^2 + (0.6)^2 + (0.4)^2 + (0.5)^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{0.25 + 0.64 + 0.36 + 0.16 + 0.25} \\
    &= \sqrt{1.66} \\
u_c &= 1.288 \;\mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Expanded uncertainty (95 % confidence, $k = 2$):**
$$
U = k \cdot u_c = 2 \times 1.288 = 2.576 \;\mu\text{m} \approx 2.6 \;\mu\text{m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Practice:** Always report the final CMM result as $L \pm U$ with both values in the same unit (prefer µm for sub-millimetre, mm for larger). Failing to attach the uncertainty is a common 1–2 mark deduction in lab viva.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — True Position (Position Tolerance, 2-D)

The **true position** of a hole's centre is the theoretical coordinate $(x_t, y_t)$ from the CAD. The CMM measures the actual centre $(x_a, y_a)$ after fitting a least-squares circle.

The radial offset is:
$$
\Delta r = \sqrt{(x_a - x_t)^2 + (y_a - y_t)^2}
$$

For a **diametrical** position tolerance (the modern ASME/ISO convention), the actual position deviation is **$2\,\Delta r$**, and the pass/fail rule is:
$$
2\,\Delta r \;\le\; \varnothing\,t_{pos}
$$

**Numerical example (KTU 2024-style):**
Specified true position: $(40.000, 30.000)$ mm. Measured: $(40.025, 30.018)$ mm. Position tolerance on drawing: $\varnothing\,0.080$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta r &= \sqrt{(0.025)^2 + (0.018)^2} \\
        &= \sqrt{0.000625 + 0.000324} \\
        &= \sqrt{0.000949} \\
        &= 0.0308 \text{ mm} \\
2\Delta r &= 0.0616 \text{ mm} = 61.6 \;\mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $2\Delta r = 61.6\;\mu\text{m} < 80\;\mu\text{m}$, the hole is **within position tolerance** (PASS).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Coordinate Measuring Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Part on Fixture - DRF Established] --> B[Probe Stylus Contacts Surface]
    B --> C[Probe Sensor - Trigger or Scanning]
    C --> D[Analogue to Digital Converter]
    D --> E[Controller CPU - PID Servo Loop]
    E --> F[Linear Encoders - X, Y, Z Scales]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Point Cloud Database - x, y, z]
    G --> H[Geometric Fitting Algorithm]
    H --> I[Least Squares Minimisation]
    H --> J[Min Max Chebyshev Algorithm]
    I --> K[GD and T Evaluation Engine]
    J --> K
    K --> L[CAD Nominal Comparison]
    L --> M[Deviation Report - Pass or Fail]
    M --> N[Digital Inspection Record - PDF or XML]
```

### 4.2 Diagram — Three-Axis Layout of a Bridge-Type CMM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    base[Granite Base - X Axis Scale] --> bridge[Bridge Carriage - Y Axis Scale]
    bridge --> quill[Z Axis Quill with Probe]
    quill --> ruby[Ruby Stylus Tip]
    workpiece[Workpiece on Fixture] -. measured by .-> ruby
```

### 4.3 Diagram — Datum Hierarchy (3-2-1 Locating Principle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Datum A - Primary - 3 contact points] -->|constrains 3 DOF| DRF[Established DRF]
    P2[Datum B - Secondary - 2 contact points] -->|constrains 2 DOF| DRF
    P3[Datum C - Tertiary - 1 contact point] -->|constrains 1 DOF| DRF
    DRF -->|total 6 DOF locked| READY[Part fully referenced for measurement]
```

### 4.4 Diagram — Measurement Workflow Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 - Warm Up 4 Hours at 20 C] --> S2[Step 2 - Probe Calibration with Reference Sphere]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 - Datum Alignment 3-2-1]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 - Feature Probing - Dimensional]
    S3 --> S5[Step 4b - Feature Probing - Geometrical]
    S4 --> S6[Step 5 - Least Squares Fitting]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Step 6 - GD and T Evaluation]
    S7 --> S8[Step 7 - Report Generation]
    S8 --> S9[Step 8 - Archival and Traceability]
```

### 4.5 Diagram — Probe-Tip Form-Deviation Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[True Mathematical Point] --> O[Offset by Effective Probe Radius]
    O --> M[Measured Point on CMM]
    M -.->|minus form deviation| T
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1.** Define a Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM). List its **three principal axes** and the international standard that governs its length-measuring performance. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
A Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM) is a multi-axis dimensional metrology instrument that determines the Cartesian coordinates $(x, y, z)$ of points on the surface of a workpiece using a contact or non-contact probe, and evaluates the geometry from the resulting point cloud.

The three principal linear axes are **X, Y, and Z**, oriented per the **right-hand rule**, with the X and Y axes lying in the horizontal machine table plane and the Z axis vertical.

The governing performance standard is **ISO 10360-2:2009** (Acceptance and re-verification tests for CMM length measurement), which specifies the **Maximum Permissible Error** of the length-measuring system as:
$$
E_{0,MPE} = A + B \cdot L
$$
where $A$ is the constant term in µm and $B$ is the length-dependent term in µm/mm evaluated at the measured length $L$ in mm.

---

> **Q2.** What is the **3-2-1 locating principle** used in CMM datum establishment? Why is it mandatory before feature measurement? **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
The 3-2-1 principle is a kinematically rigorous method of locating a rigid body on six degrees of freedom (3 translations + 3 rotations).

- **3 contact points** on the **primary datum** constrain **3 DOF** (one translation along the datum normal + two rotations about the in-plane axes).
- **2 contact points** on the **secondary datum** constrain **2 more DOF**.
- **1 contact point** on the **tertiary datum** constrains the final **1 DOF**.

Total: **6 DOF constrained** → the part is uniquely and repeatably positioned in space.

It is **mandatory** because all subsequent CMM measurements are referenced to this **datum reference frame (DRF)**. Without an established DRF, dimensional and geometrical features have no unambiguous reference, and the CMM cannot evaluate form, orientation, or location tolerances per **ASME Y14.5 / ISO 1101 GD&T**.

---

### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

> **Q.A(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **functional components** of a Bridge-type CMM. Explain the role of the **probe system** and the **controller**. **(7 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

A Bridge-type CMM consists of the following functional sub-assemblies:

1. **Granite Base and Table:** The machine rests on a precision-ground **granite base** that is thermally stable (CTE ≈ 5 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) and vibration-damped. The table carries the workpiece fixture.
2. **Bridge (X-Axis Beam):** A rigid **aluminium or ceramic bridge** spans the table along the Y-axis. It carries the Y-axis scale and supports the Z-axis quill.
3. **Y-Axis Carriage:** Slides on the bridge via **air bearings** (frictionless, no lubrication), driven by a **servo motor with linear encoder** (resolution ≤ 0.1 µm).
4. **Z-Axis Quill:** Vertical spindle carrying the probe; moves on air bearings with its own Z-scale encoder.
5. **Probe System (The "Sense"):** The probe is the *touch-point* sensor. A **touch-trigger probe (TTP)** has a kinematically constrained stylus that breaks an electrical circuit on contact, sending a 5 V trigger pulse to the controller. The controller **latches the X, Y, Z encoder values** at the instant of trigger. A **scanning probe** continuously measures stylus deflection via strain gauges and produces an analogue output, allowing dense point clouds (1000+ points/s) on free-form surfaces.
6. **Controller:** A dedicated industrial PC running a real-time operating system. It (i) **reads encoder positions**, (ii) **computes probe compensation** (effective radius, tip form, stylus length), (iii) **fits geometric primitives** (line, plane, circle, cylinder, sphere, cone) to the point cloud via least-squares, (iv) **evaluates GD&T tolerances**, and (v) **generates reports**.
7. **Software Suite:** Programs such as CALYPSO, PC-DMIS, QUINDOS, or Metrosoft CM provide CAD-based offline programming, automatic feature recognition, and inspection reporting.

**Functional Sketch (verbal):** Granite table at the base → Bridge across the table (X-axis) → Y-carriage on the bridge → Z-quill with probe descending onto the workpiece.

The probe system is critical because **CMM accuracy is only as good as the probe accuracy**. The controller is critical because it is the *computational brain* that converts raw $(x, y, z)$ coordinates into engineering tolerances.

---
