> **Q.A(b)** A **shaft of nominal diameter 50.000 mm** is measured on a CMM by probing 6 evenly distributed points around the circumference at the mid-span axial section. The probed $(x, y)$ coordinates (in mm) are:
> $P_1 = (25.012, 0.005)$, $P_2 = (12.510, 21.660)$, $P_3 = (-12.495, 21.670)$, $P_4 = (-25.005, -0.010)$, $P_5 = (12.515, -21.665)$, $P_6 = (-12.490, -21.660)$.
> Compute (i) the **least-squares centre**, (ii) the **least-squares radius**, (iii) the **diametral error**, and (iv) the **circularity (roundness)** of the shaft. **(7 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The least-squares centre is the arithmetic mean of all probed $(x, y)$ coordinates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_c &= \frac{1}{6}\bigl(25.012 + 12.510 - 12.495 - 25.005 + 12.515 - 12.490\bigr) \\
    &= \frac{1}{6}(0.047) = 0.00783 \;\text{mm} \\[4pt]
y_c &= \frac{1}{6}\bigl(0.005 + 21.660 + 21.670 - 0.010 - 21.665 - 21.660\bigr) \\
    &= \frac{1}{6}(0.000) = 0.00000 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the least-squares centre is approximately $(0.008, 0.000)$ mm.

**Compute individual point-to-centre distances $r_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \sqrt{(25.012 - 0.00783)^2 + (0.005 - 0)^2} \approx 25.0042 \;\text{mm} \\
r_2 &= \sqrt{(12.510 - 0.00783)^2 + (21.660 - 0)^2} \approx 25.0010 \;\text{mm} \\
r_3 &= \sqrt{(-12.495 - 0.00783)^2 + (21.670 - 0)^2} \approx 24.9975 \;\text{mm} \\
r_4 &= \sqrt{(-25.005 - 0.00783)^2 + (-0.010 - 0)^2} \approx 25.0128 \;\text{mm} \\
r_5 &= \sqrt{(12.515 - 0.00783)^2 + (-21.665 - 0)^2} \approx 25.0038 \;\text{mm} \\
r_6 &= \sqrt{(-12.490 - 0.00783)^2 + (-21.665 - 0)^2} \approx 25.0033 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Least-squares radius (arithmetic mean):**
$$
R_{LS} = \frac{1}{6}(25.0042 + 25.0010 + 24.9975 + 25.0128 + 25.0038 + 25.0033) = 25.0038 \;\text{mm}
$$

**(iii) Diametral error:**
$$
\varnothing_{actual} = 2 \times 25.0038 = 50.0076 \;\text{mm}
$$
$$
\Delta\varnothing = 50.0076 - 50.000 = +0.0076 \;\text{mm} = +7.6\;\mu\text{m (oversize)}
$$

**(iv) Circularity (Roundness):**
$$
t_{round} = r_{max} - r_{min} = 25.0128 - 24.9975 = 0.0153 \;\text{mm} = 15.3\;\mu\text{m}
$$

**Result Summary:**
- Centre: $(0.008, 0.000)$ mm
- Radius: $25.004$ mm → Diameter: $50.008$ mm (oversize by 7.6 µm)
- Roundness: **15.3 µm**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting that the least-squares radius is the **mean of distances** to the centre, not the mean of $x$ or $y$ individually. (–1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Reporting the *radial* error instead of the *diametral* error for size tolerance. (–1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Computing roundness as $R_{max} - R_{min}$ of the *fitted* radius (a single number) instead of the *individual point radii* to the centre. (–2 marks)
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Mixing mm and µm. Always keep units consistent within a step. (–1 mark)

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

> **Q.B(a)** Explain the difference between **dimensional** and **geometrical** features measured on a CMM. Give **three examples** of each and describe the **mathematical principle** used by the CMM to evaluate them. **(7 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Dimensional features** are those that describe the **size** of a feature — i.e., a scalar distance or angular value between two well-defined geometric elements.

*Examples:*
1. **Length** of a block (distance between two parallel planes).
2. **Diameter** of a hole (diameter of the fitted circle or cylinder).
3. **Depth** of a slot (perpendicular distance from the reference plane to the bottom).

These are evaluated by direct application of the **3-D Euclidean distance formula**:
$$
d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2 + (z_2 - z_1)^2}
$$
or by computing the **fitted feature size** (e.g., $D = 2R$ for a circle).

**Geometrical features** describe the **shape, orientation, or location** of a feature relative to a datum reference frame. They are **vector / tolerance-zone based** rather than scalar.

*Examples:*
1. **Flatness** (form): the smallest parallel-plane separation enveloping all points of a nominally flat surface.
2. **Cylindricity** (form): the smallest radial separation between two coaxial cylinders enveloping the surface.
3. **Perpendicularity** (orientation): the angle between a feature axis and the datum axis, expressed as a tolerance zone.
4. **Position** (location): the radial offset of a feature's true position from its theoretical position.

The mathematical principle used is **least-squares fitting** (minimises $S = \sum \varepsilon_i^2$) or **min-max Chebyshev fitting** (minimises the maximum deviation). For form tolerances, the deviation is:
$$
t_{form} = \max(\varepsilon_i) - \min(\varepsilon_i)
$$
For orientation and location tolerances, the deviation is the radial or angular offset of the *fitted* feature from the *theoretical* feature.

**Key Difference Summary Table:**

| Aspect | Dimensional | Geometrical |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Scalar (size) | Vector / tolerance zone (shape, orientation, location) |
| Reference | Self-contained | Requires datum reference frame |
| Output | Single number (e.g., 50.008 mm) | Two-zone envelope (e.g., 15 µm flatness) |
| Standard | ASME Y14.5, ISO 1101 | ASME Y14.5, ISO 1101, ISO 5459 |

---

> **Q.B(b)** A CMM measurement of a **300.000 mm long aluminium bar** is performed at an ambient temperature of **24.5 °C** (calibration temperature is 20 °C). The CTE of aluminium is $\alpha = 23.1 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C. The displayed CMM length is $L_{CMM} = 300.000$ mm.
> Compute (i) the **true length at 20 °C**, (ii) the **correction to be applied** if the bar were measured at the calibration temperature, and (iii) the **expanded measurement uncertainty** if the component uncertainties are: $u_{CMM} = 0.8\;\mu\text{m}$, $u_{probe} = 1.2\;\mu\text{m}$, $u_{datum} = 0.6\;\mu\text{m}$. Use $k = 2$. **(7 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) True length at 20 °C (corrected for thermal expansion):**

The CMM displays the length of the bar as it physically exists at 24.5 °C, i.e., $L_{24.5} = 300.000$ mm. To refer this back to the standard reference temperature of 20 °C:
$$
L_{20} = \frac{L_{24.5}}{1 + \alpha\,(T - 20)} = \frac{300.000}{1 + (23.1 \times 10^{-6})(4.5)}
$$

Computing the denominator:
$$
\alpha\,\Delta T = 23.1 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.5 = 103.95 \times 10^{-6} = 1.0395 \times 10^{-4}
$$
$$
1 + \alpha\,\Delta T = 1.00010395
$$
$$
L_{20} = \frac{300.000}{1.00010395} = 300.000 \times (1 - 1.0395 \times 10^{-4} + \ldots) \approx 300.000 - 0.0312
$$
$$
L_{20} = 299.9688 \;\text{mm}
$$

**(ii) The thermal correction to apply is:**
$$
\Delta L_{therm} = L_{24.5} - L_{20} = 300.000 - 299.9688 = +0.0312 \;\text{mm} = +31.2\;\mu\text{m}
$$

So the bar **expands by 31.2 µm** when heated from 20 °C to 24.5 °C. To report the CMM result at standard temperature, subtract this expansion: $L_{20} = 299.969$ mm.

**(iii) Expanded measurement uncertainty:**

Combined standard uncertainty:
$$
\begin{aligned}
u_c &= \sqrt{u_{CMM}^2 + u_{probe}^2 + u_{datum}^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{(0.8)^2 + (1.2)^2 + (0.6)^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{0.64 + 1.44 + 0.36} \\
    &= \sqrt{2.44} \\
u_c &= 1.562 \;\mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanded uncertainty at $k = 2$ (95 % confidence):
$$
U = k \cdot u_c = 2 \times 1.562 = 3.124 \;\mu\text{m} \approx 3.1 \;\mu\text{m}
$$

**Final Reported Length (at 20 °C):**
$$
\boxed{\,L_{20} = 299.9688 \pm 0.0031 \;\text{mm} \quad (k = 2)\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert $\alpha \Delta T$ in proper units. (–1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Multiplying instead of dividing the CMM reading by $(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$. The reading at 24.5 °C must be **divided** to get back to 20 °C. (–2 marks)
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Using $k = 1$ instead of $k = 2$. (–1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Reporting the result as $L_{20} = 299.9688$ mm without the $\pm U$ term. (–1 mark)

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A CMM is a **3-D Cartesian coordinate** measuring instrument; axes are **X, Y, Z**.
- ✅ Governing standard for accuracy = **ISO 10360-2** (volumetric MPE) and **ISO 10360-5** (probe MPE).
- ✅ The **3-2-1 locating principle** constrains **6 DOF** before any feature is measured.
- ✅ Two principal fitting algorithms: **Least-Squares (L₂ norm)** and **Min-Max Chebyshev (L∞ norm)**.
- ✅ For a circle, the LS radius is the **arithmetic mean of individual point-to-centre distances**.
- ✅ **Flatness** = peak-to-valley of residuals after best-fit plane subtraction.
- ✅ **Cylindricity** = $2 \times (r_{max} - r_{min})$ along the fitted axis.
- ✅ **Position tolerance (diametrical)** = $2 \Delta r$ where $\Delta r$ is the radial offset.
- ✅ **Thermal correction** of workpiece: $L_{20} = L_T / [1 + \alpha (T - 20)]$.
- ✅ **Effective probe diameter** must be qualified using a **calibrated reference sphere** before any measurement.
- ✅ **Combined standard uncertainty** = RSS of independent sources; **Expanded uncertainty** $U = k u_c$ (use $k=2$ unless told otherwise).
- ✅ Always report the final result as **$L \pm U$ in consistent units** (mm for dimension, µm for form/orientation).
- ✅ Environment must be stabilised at **$(20 \pm 0.5)$ °C** for at least 4 hours before any CMM run.
- ✅ For **free-form surfaces** use **scanning probes** (1000+ points/s); for **discrete prismatic features** use **touch-trigger probes**.
- ✅ **ASME Y14.5 / ISO 1101** govern GD&T call-out interpretation; CMM software is *not* a substitute for understanding the standard.
- ✅ Common KTU examiner's penalty zones: (i) **unit mixing**, (ii) **omitting uncertainty**, (iii) **wrong fitting norm** (L₂ vs L∞), (iv) **forgetting datum**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of surface parameters using Talysurf
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPEL507 - INDUSTRIAL INSPECTION AND QUALITY CONTROL LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of surface parameters using Talysurf # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) A **Talysurf** is a precision **stylus-type contact profilometer** (also called a stylus profilometer or surface texture measuring instrument) used in metrology laboratories for the **quantitative evaluation of surface finish, surface roughness, and surface texture parameters** of engineering components. The instrument traces a diamond-tipped stylus at a constant slow speed across the workpiece surface, and the vertical (and sometimes horizontal) deflections of the stylus are transduced into electrical signals that are amplified, filtered, and processed to display standard **2D surface roughness parameters** such as **Ra, Rz, Rq, Rt, Rp, and Rv**, or to generate a magnified graphical **profile trace (Talysurf trace)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Examiner's Preferred Wording):** > "Talysurf is a direct-contact, stylus-based surface profilometer in which a diamond stylus of standard tip radius (typically **2 µm** or **5 µm**) traverses the test surface at a specified speed, and the vertical displacement of the stylus is converted by an inductive/electrical transducer into a signal proportional to the surface profile." ## 1.2 Working Principle — In One Line > A sharp diamond needle **"feels"** the bumps and valleys of a surface while sliding over it, and an attached sensor **translates** the up-down movement of the needle into a **graph and a number** that tells us how rough or smooth the surface is. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Finger-Tip Fingerprint" Analogy Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to judge the texture of an unknown metal block. You naturally drag your fingertip across the surface. Your fingertip *goes up* over a peak and *dips down* into a valley. The pattern of these micro-movements — how often, how deep, and how sharp the ups and downs are — is precisely the *signature* of the surface. The **Talysurf replaces your finger with a calibrated diamond pin** (radius ≈ **2 µm**, far sharper and stiffer than skin) and replaces your brain's "feeling" with a **precision transducer, amplifier, and digital display**. So: - **Stylus** = the fingertip - **Transducer** = the nerves - **Amplifier + filter** = the spinal cord - **Display / chart recorder** = the brain that reports "Ra = 0.8 µm" (the surface verdict) > [!NOTE] > **Why not just use a microscope?** Because a microscope can only see roughness; it cannot give a **single, traceable, ISO-defined number** that manufacturing engineers can write on a drawing. Talysurf gives a **quantitative, repeatable, and standardised** roughness value — which is exactly what KTU / ISO 4287 / ISO 13565 demand. ## 1.4 Primary Surface Parameters Measured (Syllabus Highlight) | Parameter | Full Name | Typical Unit | What it tells us | |-----------|-----------|--------------|------------------| | **Ra** | Arithmetic Mean Roughness | **µm** (or µin) | Overall average roughness — most widely used single number for surface quality | | **Rz** | Mean Roughness Depth (Ten-Point Height) | **µm** | Average of the 5 highest peaks and 5 deepest valleys | | **Rq (Rrms)** | Root Mean Square Roughness | **µm** | Statistical RMS value — more sensitive to occasional peaks | | **Rt (Rmax)** | Total / Maximum Roughness Height | **µm** | Maximum peak-to-valley distance in the assessment length | | **Rp** | Maximum Profile Peak Height | **µm** | Height of the highest peak above the mean line | | **Rv** | Maximum Profile Valley Depth | **µm** | Depth of the deepest valley below the mean line | > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know **Ra and Rz** thoroughly, and to be able to **derive** their mathematical expressions — which we do in **Section 3**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture / Functional Subsystems of a Talysurf A modern Talysurf (e.g., Taylor Hobson Talysurf series, Mitutoyo SJ-series, Mahr Perthometer) consists of the following functional blocks: 1. **Stylus Assembly** — A diamond tip (tip radius **2 µm** or **5 µm**, cone angle typically **60°** or **90°**) mounted on a thin cantilever arm. The arm is spring-loaded so the stylus stays in contact with the workpiece with a defined static measuring force (usually **0.7 mN to 4 mN** depending on model). 2. **Pickup / Transducer** — Converts stylus vertical motion into an electrical signal. Most common types: - **Inductive (LVDT)** pickup — the most common in classic Talysurfs. - **Piezoelectric** pickup — used in newer high-frequency models. - **Capacitive** pickup — used in some research-grade units. 3. **Datum Reference (Skid or Reference Skid)** — A smooth curved skid (radius ≈ **25–50 mm**) that rides on the surface *next to* the stylus to provide a moving datum (the "mean line"). Some modern instruments use a **skidless reference** with an internal linear datum. 4. **Traverse Drive Unit** — A slow-speed DC / stepper motor that drives the stylus skid assembly across the workpiece at the standard **traversing speed of 0.5 mm/s or 1 mm/s** (for ISO 4288 standard). 5. **Amplifier & Filter Section** — The weak transducer signal is amplified, and an **analogue 2CR (2 Capacitance-Resistance) filter** or **Gaussian filter (λc)** is applied to remove long-wavelength form and short-wavelength noise. Standard cut-off (λc) values are **0.08 mm, 0.25 mm, 0.8 mm, 2.5 mm, 8 mm** (ISO 3274 / ISO 4288). 6. **Digital / Analogue Readout** — A digital panel meter displays the chosen parameter (Ra, Rz, etc.) directly in **µm**, and an **X–Y recorder or plotter** draws the magnified profile trace on graph paper. > [!NOTE] > The **magnification** of a Talysurf is typically **vertical 1000×–100,000×** and **horizontal 10×–100×**. So a real 10 µm peak is plotted as a 10 mm–1000 mm tall pen deflection. ## 2.2 Operating Procedure — Logical Step Sequence 1. **Specimen preparation** — The workpiece must be clean, dry, and free from burrs, oil, dust, or loose swarf. Place the specimen on a flat reference table. 2. **Stylus and skid inspection** — Check the diamond stylus under a microscope (tip radius and freedom from damage are critical). A damaged tip will give wrong readings. 3. **Selection of cut-off (λc)** — Choose the **sampling cut-off** based on the expected Ra of the surface (refer to ISO 4288 table). For example: - **Ra 0.006 – 0.02 µm → λc = 0.08 mm** - **Ra 0.02 – 0.1 µm → λc = 0.25 mm** - **Ra 0.1 – 2 µm → λc = 0.8 mm** (most common in workshops) - **Ra 2 – 10 µm → λc = 2.5 mm** - **Ra 10 – 80 µm → λc = 8 mm** 4. **Selection of traversing length** — The **assessment length (ln)** = **N × λc**, where N is normally **5** (so that 5 sampling lengths are averaged, per ISO 4288). The first sampling length is often **discarded** to remove end-effect errors. 5. **Set traversing speed** — Choose **0.5 mm/s** (most common) or **1 mm/s** depending on the filter. 6. **Place stylus gently on the surface** — Lower the pickup arm carefully so the stylus tip touches the surface without shock. 7. **Start the traverse drive** — The stylus traces the surface; the transducer output is recorded. 8. **Read the digital display** — The instrument computes and displays the chosen parameter (Ra, Rz, etc.). 9. **Repeat at 3–5 locations** — Because surface roughness is a stochastic property, take **at least 3 readings** at different positions/orientations and report the **mean value**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Symbol | Name / Description | Formula | Boundary / Notes | |--------|--------------------|---------|------------------| | **Ra** | Arithmetic Mean Roughness | $Ra = \dfrac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ | Most widely used; **µm** | | **Ra (discrete form)** | Computed from N sampled heights | $Ra = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i \vert$ | Used by digital profilometer | | **Rq (Rrms)** | Root-Mean-Square Roughness | $Rq = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} y(x)^{2}\, dx}$ | More sensitive to occasional peaks than Ra | | **Rz** | Ten-Point Mean Roughness Depth | $Rz = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{5}\vert Rp_i \vert + \sum_{i=1}^{5}\vert Rv_i \vert}{5}$ | Average of 5 Rp and 5 Rv in **one sampling length** | | **Rt (Rmax)** | Maximum Peak-to-Valley Height | $Rt = Rp_{max} + Rv_{max}$ | Within the assessment length | | **Rp** | Maximum Profile Peak Height | Highest peak above mean line | µm | | **Rv** | Maximum Profile Valley Depth | Deepest valley below mean line | µm | | **Assessment Length ln** | Total measured length | $ln = N \cdot \lambda_c$ (N = 5) | First λc usually discarded | | **λc** | Cut-off (filter) wavelength | Selected per ISO 4288 | 0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8 mm | | **Sampling Length lr** | One evaluation window | $lr = \lambda_c$ | mm | | **Stylus tip radius** | Standard diamond tip | **2 µm** (most common) or 5 µm | µm | | **Stylus tip angle** | Cone included angle | **60°** or **90°** | degrees | | **Static measuring force** | Force holding stylus on surface | **≈ 0.7 mN – 4 mN** | Newtons | | **Traverse speed** | Stylus travel speed | **0.5 mm/s** or 1 mm/s | mm/s | | **Vertical magnification** | Profile trace amplification | **1000× – 100,000×** | dimensionless | | **Relation Ra vs Rq (pure random profile)** | Statistical identity | $Rq \approx 1.11 \cdot Ra$ | For Gaussian surfaces | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Trap:** Many students write the Rz formula incorrectly by including only *peaks* or *only valleys*. The correct Rz definition uses **5 highest peaks AND 5 deepest valleys** averaged over the sampling length. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Production-line QC** of bearings, gears, shafts, pistons, cylinder bores, valve seats, and medical implants. - **Friction and wear prediction** — surfaces with low Ra (≤ 0.2 µm) have lower friction and longer fatigue life. - **Sealing and gasket surfaces** — a rough sealing face can leak; Ra and Rz are tightly specified in flange drawing callouts. - **Optical and aerospace components** — even nanometre-level defects scatter light; Talysurf is used in process control. - **Research and ISO compliance** — every roughness call-out on an engineering drawing (e.g., Ra 0.8, Rz 6.3) is verified with a Talysurf-class instrument. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Arithmetic Mean Roughness (Ra) ### 3.1.1 Setup of the Mean (Centre) Line When the stylus traces a workpiece surface, the raw trace is composed of **roughness (short wavelength) + waviness (medium wavelength) + form (long wavelength)**. After filtering with the cut-off λc, the filtered profile is taken with respect to a **least-squares mean line (m)**. The mean line is the line such that the sum of squared deviations of the profile y(x) from it is minimised: $$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - m)^{2} = \text{minimum} $$ Differentiating with respect to m and setting to zero: $$ \frac{d}{dm}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - m)^{2}\right] = -2\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - m) = 0 $$ Solving for m: $$ m = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i $$ This confirms the mean line is the **arithmetic mean of the profile ordinates** within the sampling length. ### 3.1.2 Defining Ra from First Principles The absolute vertical deviation at any point x is the magnitude $\vert y(x) \vert$. Averaging this over the assessment length L gives the **Arithmetic Mean Roughness**: $$ Ra = \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx $$ In discrete sampled form (which is what the Talysurf electronics actually compute via an integrating ADC): $$ Ra = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i - m \vert $$ Substituting $m$ as the mean line: $$ Ra = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\vert y_i - \frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} y_j \right\vert $$ > [!NOTE] > The **absolute value** $\vert \cdot \vert$ is essential — without it, the positive (peaks) and negative (valleys) deviations would cancel, giving **Ra = 0** for any real surface. ### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Question:** A Talysurf samples 5 ordinates (in µm) over a sampling length after filtering: $y_1 = +2, y_2 = -1, y_3 = +3, y_4 = -2, y_5 = +1$. Compute **Ra**. **Step 1 — Mean line:** $$ m = \frac{1}{5}(2 - 1 + 3 - 2 + 1) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 \,\mu m $$ **Step 2 — Deviations from mean line:** $$ y_1 - m = 2.0 - 0.6 = +1.4 $$ $$ y_2 - m = -1.0 - 0.6 = -1.6 $$ $$ y_3 - m = 3.0 - 0.6 = +2.4 $$ $$ y_4 - m = -2.0 - 0.6 = -2.6 $$ $$ y_5 - m = 1.0 - 0.6 = +0.4 $$ **Step 3 — Absolute values:** $$ \vert y_i - m \vert = 1.4,\; 1.6,\; 2.4,\; 2.6,\; 0.4 $$ **Step 4 — Sum and divide by N:** $$ Ra = \frac{1}{5}(1.4 + 1.6 + 2.4 + 2.6 + 0.4) = \frac{8.4}{5} = 1.68 \,\mu m $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{Ra = 1.68 \,\mu m} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation of the Root Mean Square Roughness (Rq) By analogy, define the **mean-square** value: $$ \overline{y^{2}} = \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} [y(x)]^{2}\, dx $$ Taking the square root gives the **Root Mean Square Roughness**: $$ Rq = \sqrt{\frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} y(x)^{2}\, dx} $$ Discrete form: $$ Rq = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i^{2}} $$ **For a purely random (Gaussian) surface, a well-known statistical identity holds:** $$ Rq \approx 1.11 \cdot Ra $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU boards often ask: *"For the same surface, which is greater — Rq or Ra?"* — The answer is **Rq > Ra** for any real engineering surface, because squaring amplifies large peaks more than averaging absolute values does. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Ten-Point Mean Roughness (Rz) Within **one sampling length** $l_r = \lambda_c$: 1. Identify the **5 highest peaks** above the mean line: $Rp_1, Rp_2, Rp_3, Rp_4, Rp_5$. 2. Identify the **5 deepest valleys** below the mean line: $Rv_1, Rv_2, Rv_3, Rv_4, Rv_5$. 3. Sum the absolute values and divide by **5**: $$ Rz = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} \vert Rp_i \vert + \sum_{i=1}^{5} \vert Rv_i \vert}{5} $$ Equivalently, for a single peak–valley pair average: $$ Rz = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} (Rp_i + Rv_i) $$ > [!NOTE] > **ISO 4287/1997** changed the symbol for "ten-point height" to **Rz**. The older German **DIN** used **Rz** for *mean roughness depth* (the average of 5 single peak-to-valley Rt values). KTU expects the **modern ISO 4287 definition** shown above. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example for Rz **Question:** The 5 highest peaks and 5 deepest valleys (in µm) measured in one sampling length are: Peaks: 4.0, 3.6, 3.2, 2.8, 2.4 ; Valleys: 3.0, 2.6, 2.2, 1.8, 1.4. Compute **Rz**. **Step 1 — Sum of peaks:** $$ \sum Rp = 4.0 + 3.6 + 3.2 + 2.8 + 2.4 = 16.0 \,\mu m $$ **Step 2 — Sum of valleys:** $$ \sum \vert Rv \vert = 3.0 + 2.6 + 2.2 + 1.8 + 1.4 = 11.0 \,\mu m $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Rz formula:** $$ Rz = \frac{16.0 + 11.0}{5} = \frac{27.0}{5} = 5.4 \,\mu m $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{Rz = 5.4 \,\mu m} $$ ## 3.5 Relation Between Assessment Length, Sampling Length, and Cut-Off The **assessment length** $l_n$ is the total length traversed: $$ l_n = N \cdot l_r = N \cdot \lambda_c $$ By ISO 4288, $N = 5$ (5 sampling lengths averaged), so: $$ l_n = 5 \cdot \lambda_c $$ The first $\lambda_c$ is often excluded from the final average to avoid end effects (run-in and run-out of the trace), so effectively the reported value is the **mean of 4 sampling lengths** — a common source of confusion in KTU viva questions. ## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix (Quick Revision Table) | Parameter | Symbolic Definition | Computed From | Standard Reference | Typical Magnitude for Machined Steel | |-----------|---------------------|---------------|--------------------|----------------------------------------| | Ra | $Ra = \dfrac{1}{L}\int \vert y \vert\, dx$ | All absolute deviations | ISO 4287 | 0.4 – 3.2 µm | | Rq | $Rq = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{L}\int y^{2}\, dx}$ | Squared deviations | ISO 4287 | ≈ 1.11 × Ra | | Rz | $Rz = \dfrac{1}{5}\sum (Rp + Rv)$ | 5 peaks + 5 valleys | ISO 4287 | 4 – 16 µm | | Rt | $Rt = Rp_{max} + Rv_{max}$ | Single largest peak-valley | ISO 4287 | 1.4 × Rz approx. | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Talysurf ```mermaid flowchart TB A["WORKPIECE SURFACE
Test Specimen Mounted on Stage"] B["DIAMOND STYLUS
Tip Radius 2 micrometre
Cone Angle 60 degree"] C["PICKUP / TRANSDUCER
LVDT or Piezoelectric
Converts Vertical Motion to Voltage"] D["AMPLIFIER STAGE
Gain Adjustment
Calibration Reference"] E["FILTER NETWORK
Gaussian 2CR Filter
Cutoff Lambda c per ISO 4288"] F["INTEGRATOR AND ADC
Computes Ra Rz Rq
Digital Sampling"] G["DIGITAL DISPLAY
Direct Readout in micrometre"] H["X Y PLOTTER
Profile Trace Recorder
Magnified Profile Plot"] I["TRAVERSE DRIVE MOTOR
Stepper DC Motor
Speed 0.5 mm per second"] J["DATUM SKID
Reference Curved Skid
Provides Mean Line Reference"] K["OPERATOR PANEL
Parameter Selection
Cutoff Selector"] A -- "Contact" --> B J -- "Rides On" --> A B -- "Vertical Deflection" --> C C -- "Electrical Signal" --> D D -- "Amplified Signal" --> E E -- "Filtered Profile" --> F F -- "Digital Code" --> G F -- "Analog Profile" --> H I -- "Drives Across" --> J K -- "Commands" --> F K -- "Commands" --> I ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** The workpiece is contacted simultaneously by the **stylus** (which senses) and the **skid** (which provides the moving reference). Vertical motion of the stylus is converted to voltage by the pickup, amplified, filtered, digitised, and finally displayed and/or plotted. The traverse drive slides the whole skid-stylus assembly horizontally at a constant speed. ## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Measurement Procedure ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["STEP 1
Clean Specimen
and Inspect Stylus"] --> S2["STEP 2
Select Cutoff
Lambda c as per ISO 4288"] S2 --> S3["STEP 3
Place Stylus
on Workpiece"] S3 --> S4["STEP 4
Start Traverse
at 0.5 mm per second"] S4 --> S5["STEP 5
Transducer Picks
Vertical Signal"] S5 --> S6["STEP 6
Filter Amplify
and Integrate"] S6 --> S7["STEP 7
Display Ra or Rz
in micrometre"] S7 --> S8["STEP 8
Repeat at 3 to 5
Different Locations"] S8 --> S9["STEP 9
Report Mean Value
with Standard Deviation"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Signal Path Showing Cut-off Filter Selection ```mermaid flowchart TB R1["SURFACE
Roughness Profile"] --> R2["PICKUP
Transducer Output"] R2 --> R3["PREAMPLIFIER
Gain Stage 1"] R3 --> R4["FILTER BANK
Selector Switch"] R4 --> R41["Cutoff 0.08 mm
Mirror Finished Surfaces"] R4 --> R42["Cutoff 0.25 mm
Fine Ground Surfaces"] R4 --> R43["Cutoff 0.80 mm
Turned Milled Surfaces"] R4 --> R44["Cutoff 2.5 mm
Planed Shaped Surfaces"] R4 --> R45["Cutoff 8.0 mm
Heavy Forging Surfaces"] R41 --> R5["POST AMPLIFIER
and Integrator"] R42 --> R5 R43 --> R5 R44 --> R5 R45 --> R5 R5 --> R6["RA OR RZ
Digital Readout"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The cut-off filter separates **roughness** from **waviness**. If the wrong cut-off is selected, the reading can be off by a factor of 2 or more — this is the most common *practical mistake* in the lab, and the most common *theoretical trap* in KTU written exams. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the term "Arithmetic Mean Roughness (Ra)" and state its SI / industrial unit.** **Model Answer:** Arithmetic Mean Roughness Ra is the **arithmetic average of the absolute values of the ordinates** of the filtered surface profile measured from the mean line over a specified sampling length. Mathematically: $$ Ra = \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx $$ It is the most widely used single parameter for specifying surface finish on engineering drawings. **Industrial unit: micrometre (µm)**. **(3 marks)** ### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the role of the "skid" in a Talysurf? Why is it needed?** **Model Answer:** The skid is a **smooth, curved reference shoe** that rides on the surface immediately adjacent to the stylus. It provides a **moving mechanical datum (mean line reference)** against which the stylus vertical motion is measured. The skid is needed because: 1. The workpiece cannot be assumed perfectly flat — the skid's curvature automatically tracks large-scale form errors. 2. It removes low-frequency waviness from the measurement without needing complex electronic filtering. 3. It mechanically averages small surface irregularities ahead of the stylus. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice. Each carries 14 marks (7 + 7 sub-parts).* ### ⭐ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Talysurf instrument**. Clearly mention the role of stylus, pickup, datum skid, filter, and traverse drive. State **two advantages** and **one limitation**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Define the surface roughness parameters **Ra, Rz, and Rt**. For a sampling length, the following 5 ordinates (in µm) were recorded with reference to the mean line: +3, -2, +4, -1, +2. Compute **Ra** and **Rq**. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution for (a) **Construction (3 marks):** Draw the block diagram from Section 4.1. A Talysurf consists of: - **Stylus assembly** — diamond tip of radius **2 µm**, cone angle **60°–90°**, mounted on a cantilever arm. - **Pickup / transducer** — LVDT type, converts vertical displacement to a proportional AC voltage. - **Datum skid** — a curved reference shoe that slides on the surface. - **Traverse drive** — stepper/DC motor giving a constant traversing speed of **0.5 mm/s**. - **Amplifier + filter** — 2CR / Gaussian filter with selectable cut-off (0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8 mm). - **Readout / recorder** — digital meter and X–Y plotter for the magnified profile. **Working (3 marks):** The workpiece is placed on a flat table. The diamond stylus is gently lowered onto the surface. The traverse drive moves the skid-stylus assembly horizontally. As the stylus rides over peaks and valleys, the cantilever deflects vertically. The pickup converts this into a voltage signal. The signal is amplified, filtered to remove waviness and noise, and either integrated to compute Ra/Rz or plotted as a magnified profile trace. The parameter value is displayed digitally in µm. **Advantages (0.5 × 2 = 1 mark):** 1. Gives a **quantitative, ISO-traceable** surface roughness number. 2. Generates a **graphical profile trace** for visual inspection of surface defects. **Limitation (0.5 mark):** The stylus is contact-type, so it can **scratch soft materials** (e.g., aluminium, polymers) and is **unsuitable for very rough or wet surfaces**. #### ✅ Model Solution for (b) **Ra and Rz and Rt definitions (1 mark each = 3 marks):** - **Ra** = Arithmetic Mean Roughness, $Ra = \dfrac{1}{L}\int \vert y(x) \vert dx$ - **Rz** = Ten-Point Mean Roughness Depth — average of 5 highest peaks and 5 deepest valleys within sampling length - **Rt** = Total / Maximum roughness height = $Rp_{max} + Rv_{max}$ within assessment length **Numerical — Compute Ra (2 marks):** Given ordinates: $y_1 = +3, y_2 = -2, y_3 = +4, y_4 = -1, y_5 = +2 \, \mu m$ (already with respect to mean line, so $m = 0$). $$ Ra = \frac{1}{5}\bigl(\vert 3\vert + \vert -2\vert + \vert 4\vert + \vert -1\vert + \vert 2\vert \bigr) = \frac{3+2+4+1+2}{5} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4 \,\mu m $$ **Numerical — Compute Rq (2 marks):** $$ Rq = \sqrt{\frac{1}{5}\bigl(3^{2} + (-2)^{2} + 4^{2} + (-1)^{2} + 2^{2}\bigr)} = \sqrt{\frac{9+4+16+1+4}{5}} = \sqrt{\frac{34}{5}} = \sqrt{6.8} = 2.608 \,\mu m $$ $$ \boxed{Ra = 2.4 \,\mu m \quad ; \quad Rq \approx 2.61 \,\mu m} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:** > 1. Forgetting the **absolute value bars** in the Ra formula (then $Ra = 0$ — full marks deducted). > 2. Confusing **Rz with Rt** — Rz uses **average of 5+5 points**; Rt is the **single largest** peak-to-valley. > 3. Writing Ra as "average of the profile" without specifying the **mean-line reference**. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** What is a **stylus profilometer**? Explain the **different types of pickups** used in Talysurf with sketches. **(7 marks)** **(b)** During a Talysurf test, the 5 highest peaks (in µm) in one sampling length are: 5.0, 4.5, 4.0, 3.5, 3.0 and the 5 deepest valleys (in µm) are: 4.0, 3.5, 3.0, 2.5, 2.0. Compute **Rz** and **Rt** if the maximum peak and maximum valley in the assessment length are **6.0 µm and 5.0 µm** respectively. State the cut-off you would choose if the surface is a **turned mild-steel component with expected Ra ≈ 1.6 µm**. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution for (a) **Stylus Profilometer definition (1 mark):** A stylus profilometer is a contact-type surface measuring instrument in which a sharp diamond stylus of standard geometry is drawn across the surface, and its vertical motion is transduced into a signal proportional to the local height of the surface profile. **Types of pickups (with sketches — 4 marks):** 1. **Inductive (LVDT) Pickup** — Two coils with a movable ferromagnetic core attached to the stylus arm. As the stylus moves, the core moves, changing the inductance of the two coils differentially, producing an output voltage proportional to displacement. Most common in conventional Talysurfs. 2. **Piezoelectric Pickup** — Stylus movement stresses a piezoelectric crystal, generating a voltage. Excellent high-frequency response, used in modern digital Talysurfs. 3. **Capacitive Pickup** — Stylus is attached to one plate of a parallel-plate capacitor. Movement changes the gap, hence the capacitance, producing a signal. Used in research-grade instruments. 4. **Strain-Gauge Pickup** — Stylus movement strains a fine foil gauge bonded to the cantilever; resistance change is measured via a Wheatstone bridge. Less common, lower accuracy. **Application note (2 marks):** LVDT is most popular due to ruggedness; piezoelectric is fastest and best for high-frequency surface features. #### ✅ Model Solution for (b) **Compute Rz (3 marks):** $$ Rz = \frac{\sum \vert Rp \vert + \sum \vert Rv \vert}{5} = \frac{(5.0+4.5+4.0+3.5+3.0) + (4.0+3.5+3.0+2.5+2.0)}{5} $$ $$ Rz = \frac{20.0 + 15.0}{5} = \frac{35.0}{5} = 7.0 \,\mu m $$ **Compute Rt (2 marks):** $$ Rt = Rp_{max} + Rv_{max} = 6.0 + 5.0 = 11.0 \,\mu m $$ **Cut-off selection (2 marks):** For expected Ra ≈ 1.6 µm on a turned mild-steel component, from the ISO 4288 table: - **Ra 0.1 – 2.0 µm → Cut-off λc = 0.8 mm** Hence select $\lambda_c = 0.8 \, mm$. Assessment length $l_n = 5 \times 0.8 = 4.0 \, mm$. $$ \boxed{Rz = 7.0 \,\mu m \quad ; \quad Rt = 11.0 \,\mu m \quad ; \quad \lambda_c = 0.8 \, mm} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:** > 1. Using only **5 peaks** in Rz and forgetting to add **5 valleys** (very common — 1 full mark lost). > 2. Confusing **Rz with Rt** — the question asks both; students often compute one and skip the other. > 3. **Cut-off trap** — students write "0.25 mm" or "0.8 mm" without applying the ISO 4288 rule that **Ra 0.1–2 µm** maps to **0.8 mm**. The mapping is graded. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (read before every Talysurf question):** - ☐ **Talysurf** = contact, stylus-type, **2D profilometer** for surface roughness and texture. - ☐ **Stylus** = diamond, tip radius **2 µm** (or 5 µm), cone angle **60°** (or 90°). - ☐ **Pickup** = typically **LVDT** (inductive); also piezoelectric / capacitive. - ☐ **Datum skid** = curved reference shoe giving the **mean-line reference**. - ☐ **Traverse speed** = **0.5 mm/s** (standard) or 1 mm/s. - ☐ **Cut-off λc** = **0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8 mm** (ISO 4288). - ☐ **Ra** formula = $Ra = \dfrac{1}{L}\int \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ — **always use absolute value**. - ☐ **Rq** formula = $Rq = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{L}\int y(x)^{2}\, dx}$ — for Gaussian surface, $Rq \approx 1.11 \cdot Ra$. - ☐ **Rz** formula = average of **5 highest peaks + 5 deepest valleys** within one sampling length. - ☐ **Rt** = single **maximum peak-to-valley** distance in assessment length. - ☐ **Assessment length** $l_n = 5 \times \lambda_c$ (first λc often discarded). - ☐ **Cut-off selection** for Ra 0.1–2.0 µm → λc = 0.8 mm (most common in workshops). - ☐ Always take **3–5 readings** at different positions and report the **mean**. - ☐ **Advantages** of Talysurf: quantitative, ISO-traceable, gives graphical trace. - ☐ **Limitations**: contact-type (can scratch), slow, stylus tip wear over time, 2D only (a 3D profilometer or optical profiler is needed for full surface mapping). - ☐ **Examiner's favourite trick:** asking why $Rq > Ra$ for the same surface (answer: squaring amplifies large peaks). - ☐ **Examiner's favourite trick 2:** asking what happens if wrong cut-off is selected (answer: reading is meaningless — too low cut-off reads noise, too high cut-off reads waviness instead of roughness).

topics.title: Calibration of LVDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL507 - INDUSTRIAL INSPECTION AND QUALITY CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of LVDT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is a passive, contactless, inductive-type displacement transducer that converts linear mechanical displacement of a movable ferromagnetic core into a proportional differential AC voltage signal. It operates on the principle of **mutual electromagnetic induction** between one primary coil and two symmetrically wound secondary coils.

**Calibration of an LVDT** is the experimentally determined procedure of establishing a precise, traceable, and quantitative functional relationship between the **input physical quantity** (core displacement `x` in mm) and the **output electrical quantity** (differential secondary voltage `V_o` in mV) under controlled reference conditions (specified excitation voltage `V_p` at specified frequency `f`, standard temperature **25 °C**, and standard humidity).

The calibrated output equation is expressed as:

$$V_o = S \cdot x + V_{o(z)}$$

where `S` is the **sensitivity** expressed in **mV/mm**, and `V_{o(z)}` is the **residual/zero offset voltage** when the core is at the electrical null (mechanical zero) position.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Push-Pull Tug of War" of Coils

Imagine **two magnets pulling on a rope from opposite ends of a table**, with equal strength. When the rope is exactly at the center, both pulls cancel out → **zero net force** (this is the **Null Position** of the LVDT).

Now imagine sliding the rope slightly to the right. The right magnet pulls less rope, so its pull weakens, while the left magnet now has more rope and pulls harder. The **difference** between the two pulls is no longer zero — it is a measurable value that tells you *how far* the rope moved.

> [!NOTE]
> **In LVDT terms:**
> - The **rope** = movable ferromagnetic core
> - The **two magnets** = secondary coils S1 and S2
> - The **difference in pulls** = differential output voltage `V_o = V_{s1} - V_{s2}`
> - The **measured difference** = the calibration curve we plot

This simple analogy captures the heart of LVDT operation — the LVDT does **not** measure absolute voltage, it measures the **difference** between two induced voltages, which makes it inherently immune to many common-mode disturbances.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Metrics

| Reference Parameter | Standard Value Used in Calibration |
|---|---|
| **Excitation (Primary) Voltage** | **5 V RMS** (typical lab supply) |
| **Excitation Frequency** | **1 kHz to 5 kHz** sine wave (commonly **2.5 kHz**) |
| **Reference Ambient Temperature** | **25 °C ± 2 °C** |
| **Reference Humidity** | **45 % to 75 % RH** |
| **Standard Calibration Range** | **±5 mm** to **±25 mm** (depending on LVDT body length) |
| **Sensitivity of Typical Lab LVDT** | **0.5 mV/V/mm to 25 mV/V/mm** |
| **Linearity (Best Specimen)** | **±0.1 % to ±0.5 %** of Full Scale (FS) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Definition:** "LVDT is an electromechanical transducer used for measuring linear displacement. It produces an electrical output voltage proportional to the displacement of a movable core." Memorise this exact phrasing for 2-mark definition questions.

---

## 1.4 Classification of LVDT for Calibration Purposes

LVDTs used in the KTU PCPEL507 laboratory are classified by:

1. **Range type**: Unidirectional (0 to +d) or **Bidirectional (−d to +d)** — most lab LVDTs are bidirectional.
2. **Output type**: Voltage-output LVDT (with built-in oscillator/demodulator) or raw-coil LVDT (requires external signal conditioning).
3. **Connection type**: 4-wire (primary + two secondaries with common) or 5-wire (primary + S1 + S2 + S1−S2 with shield).
4. **Application**: General-purpose displacement (lab calibration), high-pressure/vacuum sealed, submersible, etc.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Why an LVDT Works

The LVDT contains three coils wound on a common hollow cylindrical former:
- **One primary coil** `P` — excited by a stable AC source `V_p` at frequency `f`.
- **Two identical secondary coils** `S1` and `S2` — connected in **series-opposition** (anti-series), so their EMFs subtract.

A movable high-permeability **ferromagnetic core** slides coaxially inside the former. The position of the core determines the mutual inductance between `P–S1` and `P–S2`.

Let:
- $M_1$ = mutual inductance between Primary and Secondary 1
- $M_2$ = mutual inductance between Primary and Secondary 2

Then:

$$V_{s1} = j \omega M_1 \cdot I_p \quad \text{and} \quad V_{s2} = j \omega M_2 \cdot I_p$$

The differential output:

$$V_o = V_{s1} - V_{s2} = j \omega I_p (M_1 - M_2)$$

When the core is at **Null Position** (geometric centre):

$$M_1 = M_2 \implies V_o = 0$$

When the core is displaced by `+x`:

$$M_1 > M_2 \implies V_o > 0 \quad \text{(positive output)}$$

When the core is displaced by `−x`:

$$M_1 < M_2 \implies V_o < 0 \quad \text{(negative output)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **sign** of `V_o` indicates the **direction** of displacement, and the **magnitude** of `V_o` indicates the **amount** of displacement. This dual capability is why LVDT is the gold standard for bi-directional linear position sensing.

---

## 2.2 Constructional Details (Required for Theory Viva)

| Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Coil Former** | Hollow cylindrical, ceramic / phenolic resin bobbin |
| **Primary Coil (P)** | Center wound, several hundred turns of **42 SWG** enamelled copper wire |
| **Secondary 1 (S1)** | Upper half, identical gauge and turns as S2 |
| **Secondary 2 (S2)** | Lower half, mirror image of S1 |
| **Core** | Soft iron / nickel-iron alloy rod, high permeability (`μ ≈ 5000`) |
| **Shielding** | Outer mu-metal casing for EMI protection |
| **Connecting Lead** | 4-core shielded cable to signal conditioner |

The two secondaries are wound such that when the core is centrally placed, the **induced EMFs are equal in magnitude but opposite in phase** — they cancel exactly → null output.

---

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Calibration Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula a KTU student is expected to write for an LVDT calibration question. Memorise the table — it is the difference between a 7 and a 14 in the ESE.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_o = S \cdot x + V_{o(z)}$ | Calibrated output equation; `S` in **mV/mm** | Calibration curve fitting |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta x}$ | Sensitivity = slope of calibration curve | Sensitivity calculation |
| 3 | $\text{Linearity Error } (\%FS) = \dfrac{\vert V_{\text{actual}} - V_{\text{ideal}} \vert_{\max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | Best-fit straight-line deviation | Linearity test |
| 4 | $\text{Non-linearity} = \dfrac{D_{max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\,\%$ | Maximum deviation from straight line | Error analysis |
| 5 | $\text{Hysteresis Error} = \dfrac{\Delta V_H}{V_{FS}} \times 100\,\%$ | Output difference for forward vs reverse stroke | Hysteresis test |
| 6 | $\text{Repeatability} = \dfrac{\pm \sigma_{\max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\,\%$ | Standard deviation of repeated readings | Repeatability test |
| 7 | $V_{FS} = V_{o,max} - V_{o,min}$ | Full-scale output swing | All error computations |
| 8 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ (for n-bit ADC) | Smallest detectable displacement | Signal conditioning |
| 9 | $M = k \cdot \dfrac{N_p N_s \mu A}{l}$ | Mutual inductance relation | Theory only |
| 10 | $V_o = j\omega I_p (M_1 - M_2)$ | Fundamental output equation | Working principle |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Table Construction Rule:** Notice the use of `\vert` instead of `|` in formula #3. This is mandatory because raw vertical pipes break Markdown table parsers. Use `\vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \vert` in every KTU answer script as well — examiners prefer clean LaTeX notation.

---

## 2.4 The Three Phases of Calibration

A KTU PCPEL507 calibration experiment has three non-negotiable phases:

### Phase A — Pre-Calibration Checks (Static)
- Visual inspection of LVDT body for dents, oxide on core.
- Check insulation resistance of each coil (≥ 50 MΩ at 500 V DC).
- Measure DC resistance of each winding; verify symmetry between S1 and S2.
- Verify excitation source: amplitude `V_p`, frequency `f`, total harmonic distortion < 1 %.

### Phase B — Static Calibration (Forward & Reverse Stroke)
- Mount LVDT in calibration jig (micrometer-driven stage).
- Connect to signal conditioner; energise primary at rated `V_p` and `f`.
- Adjust micrometer to **electrical null** (residual `V_o` < 5 mV). This is the **Mechanical Zero (MZ)**.
- Move micrometer in equal **increments** (e.g., 1 mm steps) over the rated range, recording `V_o` at each step.
- Repeat for **reverse** stroke.
- Repeat the **entire cycle three times** to assess repeatability.

### Phase C — Post-Calibration Analysis
- Plot `V_o` vs displacement `x` for forward, reverse, and average curves.
- Draw best-fit straight line using **least-squares method**.
- Compute sensitivity `S`, linearity error, hysteresis error, repeatability.
- Compute **correlation coefficient R²** — a value > 0.999 indicates excellent linearity.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications of LVDT Calibration

> [!TIP]
> **A 7-mark theory question may ask you to "list applications of calibrated LVDTs".** Memorise these — they appear almost every KTU cycle.

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Hydraulic actuator position feedback, landing gear extension |
| **Civil Engineering** | Bridge pier displacement monitoring, crack growth measurement |
| **Manufacturing** | Tooling length compensation in CNC, in-process gauging |
| **Hydraulics / Pneumatics** | Cylinder piston position, spool valve position feedback |
| **Power Plants** | Valve stem position in steam turbines, control rod position in reactors |
| **Robotics** | Linear joint position feedback |
| **Material Testing** | Tensile testing machine crosshead displacement |
| **Automotive** | Suspension travel sensors, throttle position |

Calibrated LVDTs in these applications must satisfy **traceability to national standards (e.g., NABL in India, NIST in USA)**. The lab experiment teaches you this traceable discipline.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Output Voltage Equation (Working Principle)

### Step 1 — Primary Coil Current

When the primary is excited by an AC voltage `V_p` of angular frequency `ω = 2πf`, the current flowing in the primary coil (assuming primary self-inductance `L_p` and primary resistance `R_p`) is:

$$I_p = \frac{V_p}{R_p + j\omega L_p}$$

For typical LVDT operation, `ω L_p ≫ R_p`, so to a good approximation:

$$I_p \approx \frac{V_p}{j\omega L_p} = \frac{V_p}{j\omega L_p}$$

### Step 2 — EMF Induced in Secondary 1

The mutual inductance between primary and Secondary 1 is `M_1`. The induced EMF in S1 is:

$$V_{s1} = j\omega M_1 \cdot I_p$$

Substituting `I_p`:

$$V_{s1} = j\omega M_1 \cdot \frac{V_p}{j\omega L_p} = \frac{M_1}{L_p} \cdot V_p$$

### Step 3 — EMF Induced in Secondary 2

By the same argument for Secondary 2:

$$V_{s2} = j\omega M_2 \cdot I_p = \frac{M_2}{L_p} \cdot V_p$$

### Step 4 — Differential Output

The two secondaries are connected in series opposition. The net output is therefore:

$$V_o = V_{s1} - V_{s2} = \frac{(M_1 - M_2)}{L_p} \cdot V_p$$

Define the differential mutual inductance:

$$\Delta M = M_1 - M_2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{\Delta M}{L_p} \cdot V_p}$$

### Step 5 — Linearisation for Small Displacements

For small displacements `x` around the null position, `ΔM` is approximately linear in `x`:

$$\Delta M \approx k \cdot x$$

where `k` is a constant determined by the coil geometry and core material. Substituting:

$$V_o = \frac{k \cdot x}{L_p} \cdot V_p = S \cdot x$$

where the **sensitivity** is:

$$S = \frac{k \cdot V_p}{L_p} \quad \text{(V/mm or mV/mm)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Engineering Insight:** Sensitivity `S` is **directly proportional** to excitation voltage `V_p`. Doubling `V_p` doubles the output — this is why calibration must always be performed at the **specified** excitation, and any drift in `V_p` produces a calibration error.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Sensitivity from Calibration Data (Least Squares)

Given `n` calibration points $(x_i, V_i)$, the best-fit straight line $V_o = a + b \cdot x$ has coefficients computed as follows.

### Step 1 — Mean Values

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \quad \text{and} \quad \bar{V} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i$$

### Step 2 — Slope (Sensitivity)

$$b = S = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(V_i - \bar{V})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

### Step 3 — Intercept (Zero Offset)

$$a = V_{o(z)} = \bar{V} - b \bar{x}$$

### Step 4 — Correlation Coefficient

$$R^2 = \frac{\left[\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(V_i - \bar{V})\right]^2}{\left[\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2\right]\left[\sum(V_i - \bar{V})^2\right]}$$

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Lab Report:** Always tabulate raw data with **6 columns** — `x (mm)`, `V_forward (mV)`, `V_reverse (mV)`, `V_average (mV)`, `V_ideal (mV)`, `Linearity Error (%)`. This matches the expected valuation key.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Full Calibration Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the type of problem that appears in Part B (14 marks). Practice it thoroughly.**

**Problem Statement:**
An LVDT is calibrated over a range of **−10 mm to +10 mm** in steps of **2 mm**. The excitation is **5 V RMS at 2 kHz**. The observed output voltages (averaged over three cycles) are:

| Displacement `x` (mm) | Output `V_o` (mV) |
|---:|---:|
| −10 | −498 |
| −8 | −401 |
| −6 | −302 |
| −4 | −198 |
| −2 | −99 |
| 0 | +2 |
| +2 | +101 |
| +4 | +202 |
| +6 | +303 |
| +8 | +402 |
| +10 | +501 |

**Compute (a) Sensitivity, (b) Linearity Error, (c) Zero Offset.**

### Part (a) — Sensitivity `S`

**Step 1:** Identify end points.
At $x = -10$ mm: $V_o = -498$ mV
At $x = +10$ mm: $V_o = +501$ mV

**Step 2:** Full-scale output swing:

$$V_{FS} = V_{o,max} - V_{o,min} = 501 - (-498) = 999 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3:** Full-scale displacement range:

$$\Delta x = 10 - (-10) = 20 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Average sensitivity:

$$S = \frac{V_{FS}}{\Delta x} = \frac{999 \text{ mV}}{20 \text{ mm}} = 49.95 \text{ mV/mm}$$

Equivalently, $S \approx 50$ mV/mm.

### Part (b) — Linearity Error

**Step 1:** Ideal straight line passes through the end points:
- Slope $= S = 49.95$ mV/mm
- Equation: $V_{ideal}(x) = S \cdot x = 49.95 \cdot x$ (assuming zero offset negligible)

**Step 2:** Compute deviation at each point.

For example at $x = +2$ mm:
$$V_{ideal} = 49.95 \times 2 = 99.9 \text{ mV}$$
$$V_{actual} = 101 \text{ mV}$$
$$\Delta V = 101 - 99.9 = +1.1 \text{ mV}$$

For $x = -10$ mm:
$$V_{ideal} = 49.95 \times (-10) = -499.5 \text{ mV}$$
$$V_{actual} = -498 \text{ mV}$$
$$\Delta V = -498 - (-499.5) = +1.5 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3:** Identify maximum deviation. From the table, the maximum deviation is approximately **1.5 mV** (at $x = -10$ mm).

**Step 4:** Compute linearity error:

$$\text{Linearity Error} = \frac{\vert \Delta V \vert_{max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{1.5}{999} \times 100 = 0.15\,\% FS$$

### Part (c) — Zero Offset

From the data, at $x = 0$ mm: $V_o = +2$ mV.

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V_{o(z)} = +2 \text{ mV}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This non-zero output at mechanical zero is due to (a) winding asymmetry in the secondaries, (b) harmonics in the excitation, and (c) stray capacitive coupling. In industry, this is called the **residual null voltage** and is typically reduced by a **trimming potentiometer** in the signal conditioner.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Calibration Method

| Criterion | Static (Bench) Calibration | Dynamic (In-Situ) Calibration |
|---|---|---|
| **Equipment Required** | Micrometer, signal conditioner, DMM | Installed process signal chain |
| **Accuracy** | Very High (primary standard) | Lower (working standard) |
| **Time** | 1–2 hours per cycle | < 15 minutes |
| **KTU Lab Relevance** | **Mandatory (PCPEL507)** | Mentioned only in theory |
| **Displacement Source** | Manual micrometer | Process variable |
| **Cost** | Low (lab use) | High (production downtime) |
| **Standard Followed** | ISO/IEC 17025 | ISO 9001 traceability |

---

## 3.5 Sources of Error in LVDT Calibration

| Error Source | Effect on Output | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Excitation voltage drift** | Proportional error in `V_o` | Use regulated, stabilised supply |
| **Frequency drift** | Changes impedance of coils | Use crystal-controlled oscillator |
| **Temperature variation** | Changes coil resistance, core permeability | Thermal enclosure, NTC compensation |
| **Hysteresis of core material** | Forward vs reverse stroke mismatch | Use low-hysteresis permalloy core |
| **Mechanical backlash in jig** | Position reading offset | Use anti-backlash micrometer |
| **Residual null voltage** | Non-zero output at null | Phase-sensitive demodulation, trim pot |
| **EMI from mains** | 50 Hz noise on output | Shielded cable, proper grounding |
| **Core friction / wear** | Core sticks, non-repeatability | Use polished core, dry lubricant |

## 4.2 Calibration Setup — Complete Signal Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    AC[AC Excitation Source 5V 2kHz] -->|Vp| LVDT1[LVDT Primary Coil P]
    LVDT1 -->|Vs1| SC[Signal Conditioner - Oscillator, Demodulator, Amplifier]
    LVDT1 -->|Vs2| SC
    SC -->|Vo| DMM1[Digital Millivoltmeter]
    SC -->|Vo| CRO[Cathode Ray Oscilloscope - optional]
    DMM1 --> REC[Recorder or Data Sheet]
    MIC[Micrometer Driven Stage] -->|controls x| LVDT1
    LVDT1 -.->|displacement x| MIC
    MIC -->|vernier reading| OBS[Observer / Student]

    style AC fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style LVDT1 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:3px
    style SC fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style DMM1 fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px
    style CRO fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#660099,stroke-width:2px
    style MIC fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,stroke-width:2px
    style OBS fill:#ffe1ff,stroke:#cc0099,stroke-width:2px
```

**Working flow:**
1. The **AC source (AC)** feeds a stable excitation to the LVDT primary.
2. The **micrometer (MIC)** displaces the core by a known amount `x` (read from the vernier).
3. The **signal conditioner (SC)** demodulates the AC output, producing a DC voltage proportional to displacement.
4. The **DMM (DMM1)** measures this DC voltage to ±0.1 mV resolution.
5. The **CRO (CRO)** monitors waveform integrity (used for advanced diagnostics only).

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
graph LR
    RAW[LVDT Coil Output Vs1 and Vs2 AC] --> SUM[Differential Summation Amplifier]
    SUM --> OSC[Oscillator 2kHz Reference Signal]
    OSC --> PSD[Phase Sensitive Demodulator]
    PSD --> LPF[Low Pass Filter Cutoff 10Hz]
    LPF --> AMP[DC Amplifier Gain 1 to 100]
    AMP --> OFFSET[Zero Offset Trim Potentiometer]
    OFFSET --> OUT[DC Output Voltage Vo 0 to 5V or -5V to +5V]

    style RAW fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style SUM fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style OSC fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#660099,stroke-width:2px
    style PSD fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style LPF fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px
    style AMP fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,stroke-width:2px
    style OFFSET fill:#ffe1ff,stroke:#cc0099,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#e1e1ff,stroke:#000099,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD)** is what gives the LVDT its direction-sensing capability. It rectifies the AC output with the **same phase** as the excitation, producing a **positive DC for one direction** and a **negative DC for the other direction**. Without the PSD, the LVDT would only tell you the *magnitude* of displacement, not the *sign*.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024 model]**
**Define LVDT. State any two advantages of LVDT over a potentiometric displacement sensor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is a passive inductive transducer used to convert linear displacement into a proportional electrical (voltage) signal. It consists of a primary coil and two identical secondary coils wound on a common cylindrical former, with a movable ferromagnetic core.

**Two advantages over potentiometric sensors:**
1. **Non-contact operation:** The LVDT core does not touch the coils, so there is no mechanical wear, no friction, and infinite mechanical life. A potentiometer has a wiper that wears out.
2. **Infinite resolution:** Because output is a continuous function of position, the LVDT has theoretically infinite resolution, limited only by the measuring instrument. A potentiometer has discrete resolution determined by wire winding pitch.
3. *(Bonus point: Lower friction → suitable for measuring very small forces)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023 model]**
**What is meant by calibration of a transducer? Why is calibration important for an LVDT?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Calibration** is the process of establishing the functional relationship between the input quantity applied to a transducer and the corresponding output quantity, by comparing it against a **standard reference** under controlled conditions.

**Importance for LVDT:**
1. To determine the **sensitivity** (mV/mm) used in computing displacement from output voltage.
2. To quantify **errors** such as linearity error, hysteresis, and repeatability, ensuring measurement **traceability** to national standards.
3. To detect **drift** in excitation source or coil characteristics, ensuring long-term measurement reliability.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-A — Set 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / KTU PCPEL507 ESE pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle** of an LVDT. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An LVDT with a sensitivity of **50 mV/mm** is excited by a **5 V, 2 kHz** supply. It is used to measure the displacement of a hydraulic cylinder. When the cylinder is fully extended, the LVDT output reads **+1.25 V**; when fully retracted, it reads **−1.20 V**. Determine:
   - (i) The **range** of displacement in mm.
   - (ii) The **zero offset** of the LVDT.
   - (iii) The **linearity error** as a percentage of full scale. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-A (a) [7 marks]

**Construction (Block Diagram Description):**

```
[Mu-metal Shield]
    └── [Coil Former]
            ├── [Primary Coil P] (centre, ~500 turns, 42 SWG)
            ├── [Secondary Coil S1] (upper half, identical to S2)
            └── [Secondary Coil S2] (lower half, mirror of S1)
        [Ferromagnetic Core] (slides axially inside former)
```

The primary coil `P` is wound at the centre of the former. Two secondaries `S1` and `S2` are wound on either side, with **equal number of turns**. The secondaries are connected in **series opposition** (anti-phase). A soft-iron core slides inside the hollow former.

**Working Principle:**

When the primary is energised with an AC voltage `V_p` at frequency `f`, an alternating flux is set up in the core. This flux links the two secondaries and induces EMFs `V_{s1}` and `V_{s2}`.

Let `M_1` and `M_2` be the mutual inductances between primary and S1, S2 respectively.

The induced EMFs are:

$$V_{s1} = j\omega M_1 I_p \quad \text{and} \quad V_{s2} = j\omega M_2 I_p$$

where `I_p` is the primary current.

The differential output is:

$$V_o = V_{s1} - V_{s2} = j\omega I_p (M_1 - M_2)$$

For small displacement `x`:

$$M_1 - M_2 = k \cdot x$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V_o = j\omega I_p k \cdot x = S \cdot x}$$

**Three cases:**
1. **Core at null (centre):** $M_1 = M_2 \implies V_o = 0$
2. **Core displaced upward:** $M_1 > M_2 \implies V_o > 0$ (positive)
3. **Core displaced downward:** $M_1 < M_2 \implies V_o < 0$ (negative)

**Mark distribution (Valuation Key):**
- Construction diagram: 2 marks
- Working explanation: 2 marks
- Derivation of output equation: 3 marks
- **Total: 7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-A (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Sensitivity `S = 50 mV/mm = 0.05 V/mm`
- Excitation `V_p = 5 V`, `f = 2 kHz`
- Full extension: `V_o = +1.25 V`
- Full retraction: `V_o = −1.20 V`

**(i) Range of displacement:**

Full-scale output swing:

$$V_{FS} = V_{max} - V_{min} = 1.25 - (-1.20) = 2.45 \text{ V}$$

Converting to mm using sensitivity:

$$\text{Range} = \frac{V_{FS}}{S} = \frac{2.45 \text{ V}}{0.05 \text{ V/mm}} = \boxed{49 \text{ mm}}$$

**(ii) Zero Offset:**

The mechanical zero occurs when `V_o = 0`. Since the data is asymmetric (+1.25 vs −1.20), there is a **zero offset**.

Mid-point voltage (ideal zero):

$$V_{mid} = \frac{V_{max} + V_{min}}{2} = \frac{1.25 + (-1.20)}{2} = \frac{0.05}{2} = 0.025 \text{ V}$$

Zero offset voltage:

$$V_{o(z)} = +25 \text{ mV} \quad \text{(theoretical zero is shifted by 25 mV)}$$

Corresponding displacement offset:

$$x_{offset} = \frac{0.025}{0.05} = \boxed{0.5 \text{ mm}}$$

> The LVDT reads 25 mV (equivalent to 0.5 mm of phantom displacement) when the core is at true mechanical centre.

**(iii) Linearity Error:**

Assuming the **ideal calibration line** passes through (0, 0), the ideal output at full extension (+24.5 mm if symmetric, but here asymmetric):

Mid-range displacement is 49/2 = 24.5 mm.

Ideal output at +24.5 mm: $V_{ideal} = 0.05 \times 24.5 = 1.225$ V
Actual output at full extension: $V_{actual} = 1.25$ V

$$\Delta V = \vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \vert = \vert 1.25 - 1.225 \vert = 0.025 \text{ V}$$

$$\text{Linearity Error} = \frac{\Delta V}{V_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{0.025}{2.45} \times 100 = \boxed{1.02\,\% \text{ FS}}$$

**Mark distribution (Valuation Key):**
- Part (i) calculation: 2 marks
- Part (ii) offset calculation: 2 marks
- Part (iii) linearity error: 2 marks
- Final boxed answers with units: 1 mark
- **Total: 7 marks**

---

### Question B-B — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 model]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure for **static calibration of an LVDT**. List the apparatus required and state **any four precautions** to be observed during calibration. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** The following readings were obtained during the calibration of an LVDT (excitation: 5 V, 2 kHz). Displacement values are in mm and output voltages in mV.

| x | −10 | −8 | −6 | −4 | −2 | 0 | +2 | +4 | +6 | +8 | +10 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| V_avg | −498 | −400 | −301 | −199 | −100 | +1 | +100 | +201 | +299 | +400 | +501 |

Compute:
   - (i) The **sensitivity** in mV/mm.
   - (ii) The **residual zero offset** voltage.
   - (iii) The **maximum non-linearity** as % FS.
   - (iv) Comment on the **quality** of calibration (R² or qualitative judgement). **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-B (a) [7 marks]

**Procedure for Static Calibration of LVDT:**

**Apparatus Required (list any 6):**
1. LVDT under test
2. LVDT signal conditioner (with built-in oscillator and demodulator)
3. Stable AC excitation source (5 V, 2 kHz sine wave)
4. Calibrated micrometer-driven linear translation stage (±25 mm range, 0.01 mm resolution)
5. Digital millivoltmeter or DMM (resolution ±0.1 mV)
6. Connecting shielded cables
7. Mounting clamp and base plate
8. CRO (optional, for waveform inspection)

**Procedure (Stepwise):**

1. **Visual and electrical inspection** of the LVDT for physical damage; measure insulation resistance of each coil (should be ≥ 50 MΩ at 500 V DC).

2. **Mount the LVDT** rigidly in the calibration jig such that the core can slide freely along the axis. Connect primary to the excitation source and secondaries to the signal conditioner input.

3. **Set the excitation** to rated voltage `V_p = 5 V RMS` at frequency `f = 2 kHz`. Allow 10 minutes for warm-up.

4. **Find the electrical null** by sliding the core until the output voltmeter reads minimum. Adjust trim pot if available to bring residual voltage below 5 mV. Mark this position as **Mechanical Zero (MZ)**.

5. **Calibrate the micrometer** to read `x = 0` mm at the MZ position.

6. **Move the core in +x direction** in steps of 1 mm (or as specified) over the full range. Record `V_o` at each step — this is the **forward stroke** data.

7. **Return the core** to MZ and then move in −x direction in same steps, recording `V_o` — this is the **reverse stroke** data.

8. **Repeat steps 6 and 7** three times to assess repeatability. Average the three readings at each point.

9. **Plot the calibration curve** — `V_o` (Y-axis) vs `x` (X-axis) for forward, reverse, and average data.

10. **Compute sensitivity, linearity error, hysteresis, and repeatability** as defined in the formula sheet.

**Four Precautions:**

1. **Use a regulated excitation source** — any drift in `V_p` produces a directly proportional drift in `V_o` (since `V_o ∝ V_p`).
2. **Keep the temperature stable** — coil resistance and core permeability change with temperature.
3. **Use shielded cables and proper grounding** — LVDT outputs are low-level (mV) and susceptible to 50 Hz mains hum.
4. **Move the core slowly and wait for the reading to stabilise** at each step (allow ≥ 5 seconds for filter settling).
5. *(Bonus: Avoid backlash by always approaching each position from the same direction in forward stroke.)*

**Mark distribution (Valuation Key):**
- Apparatus list: 1 mark
- Procedure (any 8 steps): 4 marks
- Precautions (any 4): 2 marks
- **Total: 7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-B (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
| x (mm) | V_avg (mV) |
|---:|---:|
| −10 | −498 |
| −8 | −400 |
| −6 | −301 |
| −4 | −199 |
| −2 | −100 |
| 0 | +1 |
| +2 | +100 |
| +4 | +201 |
| +6 | +299 |
| +8 | +400 |
| +10 | +501 |

**(i) Sensitivity:**

Full-scale output: $V_{FS} = 501 - (-498) = 999$ mV
Full-scale displacement: $\Delta x = 20$ mm

$$S = \frac{V_{FS}}{\Delta x} = \frac{999}{20} = \boxed{49.95 \text{ mV/mm} \approx 50 \text{ mV/mm}}$$

**(ii) Residual Zero Offset:**

At $x = 0$ mm: $V_o = +1$ mV.

$$\boxed{V_{o(z)} = +1 \text{ mV}}$$

This is well within acceptable limits (< 5 mV).

**(iii) Maximum Non-linearity:**

Ideal line passes through (0, 0) and (10, 501) approximately, or use end-point method:

$$S_{ideal} = \frac{501 - (-498)}{10 - (-10)} = \frac{999}{20} = 49.95 \text{ mV/mm}$$

The ideal line equation: $V_{ideal}(x) = 49.95 \cdot x$

Compute deviations at each point:

| x | V_actual | V_ideal | ΔV |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| −10 | −498 | −499.5 | +1.5 |
| −8 | −400 | −399.6 | −0.4 |
| −6 | −301 | −299.7 | −1.3 |
| −4 | −199 | −199.8 | +0.8 |
| −2 | −100 | −99.9 | −0.1 |
| 0 | +1 | 0 | +1.0 |
| +2 | +100 | +99.9 | +0.1 |
| +4 | +201 | +199.8 | +1.2 |
| +6 | +299 | +299.7 | −0.7 |
| +8 | +400 | +399.6 | +0.4 |
| +10 | +501 | +499.5 | +1.5 |

Maximum deviation: $\vert \Delta V \vert_{max} = 1.5$ mV (at $x = \pm 10$ mm)

$$\text{Non-linearity} = \frac{1.5}{999} \times 100 = \boxed{0.15\,\% \text{ FS}}$$

**(iv) Quality of Calibration:**

A non-linearity of **0.15 % FS** is well within industrial tolerance (typically ±0.5 % FS for general-purpose LVDTs, ±0.1 % FS for precision units). The data shows excellent linearity, **symmetric response around zero**, and a **negligible zero offset (1 mV)**.

**Conclusion:** The LVDT is in **excellent working condition** and the calibration is **highly accurate**. The transducer is fit for use in precision displacement measurement applications such as CNC tooling and material testing machines.

**Mark distribution (Valuation Key):**
- Part (i) sensitivity: 2 marks
- Part (ii) zero offset: 1 mark
- Part (iii) non-linearity: 3 marks
- Part (iv) qualitative comment: 1 mark
- **Total: 7 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on LVDT Calibration Questions:**

1. **Forgetting Units in Final Answer** — Writing `S = 49.95` without `mV/mm` costs **1 mark** outright. Always carry units through every calculation.

2. **Confusing Linearity Error with Non-linearity** — Linearity is measured against the **best-fit straight line**; non-linearity is measured against the **end-point straight line**. The KTU valuation key distinguishes these. Use **end-point method** unless specifically asked otherwise.

3. **Missing Direction of Displacement** — When asked "why LVDT is bidirectional", students often forget that **Phase-Sensitive Demodulation (PSD)** is what gives the directional information. The raw coils only give magnitude of `M_1 - M_2`, but the demodulator determines sign. Mention PSD explicitly.

4. **Confusing Sensitivity of LVDT vs Sensitivity of System** — The LVDT coil itself has sensitivity in **mV per mm per volt of excitation** (mV/V/mm), while the system (with signal conditioner) has sensitivity in **mV/mm** or **V/mm**. Specify which one you are calculating.

5. **Forgetting to Average Forward and Reverse Strokes** — Hysteresis error requires BOTH forward and reverse data. Reporting only one direction loses 1–2 marks.

6. **Wrong Formula for Full Scale** — Many students write `V_FS = V_max` instead of `V_FS = V_max − V_min`. The full-scale swing is the **range**, not the maximum value.

7. **Plotting Wrong Axis** — Convention is `V_o` on **Y-axis** and `x` on **X-axis**. Drawing it reversed loses presentation marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print this before the exam!**

### Core Concepts (must-know)
- ☐ LVDT = **L**inear **V**ariable **D**ifferential **T**ransformer — passive inductive transducer.
- ☐ Three coils: **1 Primary (P)** + **2 Secondaries (S1, S2)** wound on a common former.
- ☐ Secondaries are connected in **series opposition** (anti-phase).
- ☐ A movable **ferromagnetic core** controls mutual inductances M₁ and M₂.
- ☐ Output equation: $V_o = j\omega I_p (M_1 - M_2)$.

### Key Equations (must-memorise)
- ☐ Sensitivity: $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta x}$ (units **mV/mm**).
- ☐ Output equation: $V_o = S \cdot x + V_{o(z)}$.
- ☐ Linearity error: $\dfrac{\vert \Delta V \vert_{max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\,\%$.
- ☐ Hysteresis: $\dfrac{\Delta V_H}{V_{FS}} \times 100\,\%$.

### Calibration Procedure (must-know order)
- ☐ Visual inspection → mounting → connection → energise → null adjust → forward stroke → reverse stroke → repeat 3 cycles → plot → analyse.

### Numerical Targets
- ☐ Lab LVDT sensitivity: **~50 mV/mm** (at 5 V excitation).
- ☐ Linearity of good LVDT: **< 0.5 % FS**.
- ☐ Zero offset tolerance: **< 5 mV**.
- ☐ Excitation standard: **5 V RMS, 2 kHz**.

### Advantages (must-list 4 in any theory answer)
- ☐ Infinite resolution, contactless, infinite mechanical life, bidirectional, high sensitivity, robust.

### Limitations (mention at least 2)
- ☐ Limited to small displacements (≤ ±25 mm typical), requires AC excitation, requires signal conditioning (PSD), sensitive to EMI without shielding.

### Applications (must-list 3)
- ☐ Hydraulic cylinder position, CNC tool length, tensile testing machine, bridge monitoring, valve position, robotics joint sensing.

### Viva-Voce Favourites
- ☐ "Why is the core ferromagnetic?" → To concentrate magnetic flux.
- ☐ "Why is second half output phase reversed?" → To get series-opposition subtraction.
- ☐ "Why is AC excitation used and not DC?" → DC cannot induce EMF in secondary; induction requires changing flux.
- ☐ "Why is sensitivity proportional to excitation voltage?" → Because induced EMF `e = -N dΦ/dt` and flux is proportional to primary current, which is proportional to `V_p`.
- ☐ "Why are mu-metal shields used?" → Mu-metal has very high permeability; it diverts external magnetic field lines away from the coils.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of force using load cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL507 - INDUSTRIAL INSPECTION AND QUALITY CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of force using load cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Measurement of Force Using Load Cell

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Load Cell (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **load cell** is a precisely engineered electromechanical **force transducer** that converts an applied mechanical force (tension or compression) into a measurable, proportional electrical signal (typically in millivolts per volt) by exploiting the elastic deformation of a strain-gauged elastic element. It is the industry-standard primary sensor used in weighing systems, material testing machines, and force calibration benches in accordance with ISO 376 and ASTM E74 standards.

In a KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory context, the **measurement of force using a load cell** is treated as a *complete measurement chain experiment* that validates the end-to-end conversion:

$$\text{Applied Force} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Mechanical Strain} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Electrical Signal} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Displayed Force}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapped):**
> This experiment maps directly to **Course Outcome CO2** – *Apply precision measurement instruments and inspection methods for quality control.* The cognitive level tested is **Apply / Analyze (Bloom Level III–IV)**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bathroom Scale Story"

Imagine stepping onto a **digital bathroom scale** at home.

1. Your body weight pushes down on a **spring-like metallic body** inside the scale.
2. That body bends ever so slightly (microscopically).
3. Tiny **strain gauges** glued onto the body stretch along with it, changing their electrical resistance.
4. The electronics read this change and flash your weight on the LCD.

A laboratory load cell works on **exactly the same principle** — just with:
- **Better-grade materials** (tool-steel or aluminium alloys),
- **Tighter tolerances** (Class 0.02, 0.05, 0.1 accuracy classes),
- **More strain gauges** (4-arm Wheatstone bridge configuration),
- **Temperature compensation**, and
- **Calibration traceable to NABL/NPL standards.**

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Box:** Think of the load cell as a "muscle that flexes" — the more force you apply, the more it flexes, and the more electrical signal it generates. The *amount of flex* is the key measurable variable.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Load Cells (KTU Exam Hot Topic)

| Type | Working Principle | Typical Capacity | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strain-Gauge (S-type, Button, Beam)** | Resistance change due to strain | 1 N – 5000 kN | Universal force/weighing |
| **Hydraulic Load Cell** | Pressure change in fluid | 5 kN – 10000 kN | Heavy industrial, explosion-proof |
| **Pneumatic Load Cell** | Pressure change in air column | 1 N – 250 kN | Cleanroom, food industry |
| **Piezoelectric Load Cell** | Charge generation in crystals | 1 N – 500 kN | Dynamic, high-frequency forces |
| **Capacitive Load Cell** | Capacitance change with gap | 5 N – 1000 kN | High stability, low drift |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU PCPEL507 lab uses the **Strain-Gauge type load cell** (most common in academic labs), mounted on a **Universal Force Testing Machine** or **Dead-Weight Force Calibrator**.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.80665 \; \text{m/s}^2$ (used to convert mass → force)
- **Young's Modulus of tool-steel element:** $E \approx 200 \; \text{GPa}$
- **Gauge Factor (GF) of constantan foil strain gauge:** $GF \approx 2.0$ to $2.1$
- **Excitation voltage (recommended):** $V_{ex} = 10 \; \text{V DC}$
- **Standard accuracy class for industrial load cells:** **OIML R60 Class C3** (3000 verification intervals)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why and How"
