If variability is out of control, the X̄ chart limits become unreliable, because they depend on R̄. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Statistic** | **Formula** | **Units** | **When Used** | |---|---|---|---| | Subgroup Mean | X̄ᵢ = ΣXᵢⱼ / n | Same as measurement | Every subgroup | | Subgroup Range | Rᵢ = Xmax − Xmin | Same as measurement | Every subgroup | | Grand Mean | X̄̄ = ΣX̄ᵢ / k | Same as measurement | Once (X̄ chart CL) | | Average Range | R̄ = ΣRᵢ / k | Same as measurement | Once (R chart CL) | | X̄ Upper Control Limit | UCL_X̄ = X̄̄ + A₂·R̄ | Same as measurement | Once | | X̄ Lower Control Limit | LCL_X̄ = X̄̄ − A₂·R̄ | Same as measurement | Once | | R Upper Control Limit | UCL_R = D₄·R̄ | Same as measurement | Once | | R Lower Control Limit | LCL_R = D₃·R̄ | Same as measurement | Once | | Process σ estimate | σ̂ = R̄ / d₂ | Same as measurement | Process capability | ### Standard Control Chart Constants Table (Critical for KTU Exams) | **n** | **A₂** | **D₃** | **D₄** | **d₂** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 | | 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.574 | 1.693 | | 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 | | 5 | **0.577** | **0** | **2.114** | 2.326 | | 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 | | 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 | | 8 | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 2.847 | | 9 | 0.337 | 0.184 | 1.816 | 2.970 | | 10 | 0.308 | 0.223 | 1.777 | 3.078 | > [!NOTE] > For **n ≤ 6**, **D₃ = 0**, meaning the **LCL_R = 0** (range cannot be negative). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive industry:** monitoring piston diameter, brake pad thickness. - **Pharmaceuticals:** tablet weight, hardness, dissolution time. - **Electronics:** resistor resistance, capacitor capacitance, PCB hole diameter. - **Foundries and machining:** shaft concentricity, surface roughness (Ra), thread pitch. The X̄-R chart pair enables manufacturers to detect **drift, tool wear, raw material change, operator error, and machine malfunction** early — well before defective products reach the customer. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of X̄ Chart Control Limits For a normally distributed quality characteristic *X* with true mean **μ** and true standard deviation **σ**, the sampling distribution of the subgroup mean X̄ has: $$\text{Mean of } \bar{X} = \mu$$ $$\text{Standard deviation of } \bar{X} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$ The 3-sigma control limits for X̄ are therefore: $$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu + \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}, \quad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu - \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$ Since **μ** and **σ** are unknown, they are estimated using: $$\hat{\mu} = \bar{\bar{X}}, \quad \hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$ Substituting: $$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} \bar{R} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$$ where **A₂ = 3 / (d₂·√n)**. Similarly, **LCL_X̄ = X̄̄ − A₂·R̄**. ## 3.2 Derivation of R Chart Control Limits The sampling distribution of the range R is characterized by: $$\text{Mean of } R = d_2 \sigma, \quad \text{Std. dev. of } R = d_3 \sigma$$ Therefore the 3-sigma limits for R are: $$UCL_R = d_2 \sigma + 3 d_3 \sigma = (d_2 + 3d_3)\sigma, \quad LCL_R = (d_2 - 3d_3)\sigma$$ Dividing by the mean of R (d₂·σ) yields the dimensionless multipliers: $$D_4 = 1 + \frac{3d_3}{d_2}, \quad D_3 = 1 - \frac{3d_3}{d_2}$$ Since R̄ estimates d₂·σ, the practical formulas become: $$UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}, \quad LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}, \quad CL_R = \bar{R}$$ ## 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Numerical Example A precision machining shop produces **steel shafts** with a nominal diameter of **25.00 mm**. The Quality Engineer draws **k = 10 subgroups**, each of size **n = 5**, at hourly intervals. The measurements (in mm) are recorded below: | Subgroup (i) | X₁ | X₂ | X₃ | X₄ | X₅ | X̄ᵢ | Rᵢ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 25.02 | 25.05 | 24.98 | 25.00 | 25.04 | 25.018 | 0.07 | | 2 | 25.01 | 25.03 | 25.00 | 25.05 | 25.02 | 25.022 | 0.05 | | 3 | 24.99 | 25.02 | 25.01 | 25.04 | 25.00 | 25.012 | 0.05 | | 4 | 25.00 | 25.03 | 25.01 | 25.02 | 24.99 | 25.010 | 0.04 | | 5 | 25.03 | 25.00 | 25.04 | 25.01 | 25.02 | 25.020 | 0.04 | | 6 | 24.98 | 25.01 | 25.03 | 25.00 | 25.02 | 25.008 | 0.05 | | 7 | 25.01 | 25.04 | 25.02 | 25.00 | 25.03 | 25.020 | 0.04 | | 8 | 25.00 | 24.99 | 25.02 | 25.01 | 25.03 | 25.010 | 0.04 | | 9 | 25.02 | 25.05 | 25.00 | 25.03 | 25.01 | 25.022 | 0.05 | | 10 | 24.99 | 25.02 | 25.01 | 25.00 | 25.04 | 25.012 | 0.05 | ### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Compute Grand Averages $$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{10} \sum_{i=1}^{10} \bar{X}_i$$ $$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{25.018 + 25.022 + 25.012 + 25.010 + 25.020 + 25.008 + 25.020 + 25.010 + 25.022 + 25.012}{10}$$ $$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{250.154}{10} = 25.0154 \text{ mm}$$ $$\bar{R} = \frac{1}{10} \sum_{i=1}^{10} R_i = \frac{0.07+0.05+0.05+0.04+0.04+0.05+0.04+0.04+0.05+0.05}{10}$$ $$\bar{R} = \frac{0.48}{10} = 0.0480 \text{ mm}$$ ### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Select Constants for n = 5 From the standard table: **A₂ = 0.577, D₃ = 0, D₄ = 2.114, d₂ = 2.326**. ### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Compute X̄ Chart Limits $$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 25.0154 + (0.577)(0.0480)$$ $$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 25.0154 + 0.0277 = 25.0431 \text{ mm}$$ $$CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} = 25.0154 \text{ mm}$$ $$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 25.0154 - 0.0277 = 24.9877 \text{ mm}$$ ### 3.3.4 Step 4 — Compute R Chart Limits $$UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(0.0480) = 0.1015 \text{ mm}$$ $$CL_R = \bar{R} = 0.0480 \text{ mm}$$ $$LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(0.0480) = 0 \text{ mm}$$ ### 3.3.5 Step 5 — Analytical Matrix (Limit Summary Table) | **Chart** | **UCL** | **CL** | **LCL** | |---|---|---|---| | X̄ Chart (mm) | 25.0431 | 25.0154 | 24.9877 | | R Chart (mm) | 0.1015 | 0.0480 | 0.0000 | ### 3.3.6 Step 6 — Interpretation Comparing each X̄ᵢ with the X̄ chart limits [24.9877, 25.0431]: - Min X̄ᵢ = 25.008 ∈ [24.9877, 25.0431] ✓ - Max X̄ᵢ = 25.022 ∈ [24.9877, 25.0431] ✓ - **All 10 points lie within control limits.** Comparing each Rᵢ with the R chart limits [0, 0.1015]: - Min Rᵢ = 0.04 ≥ 0 ✓ - Max Rᵢ = 0.07 ≤ 0.1015 ✓ - **All 10 points lie within control limits.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The process is in **statistical control** — both location (X̄) and spread (R) are stable. There is **no evidence of assignable causes** of variation. The process variation observed is due only to **common-cause (random) variation**. ### 3.3.7 Step 7 — Estimated Process Standard Deviation $$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.0480}{2.326} = 0.02064 \text{ mm}$$ This can be used for **process capability indices** (Cp, Cpk) in advanced KTU questions. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — X̄ and R Chart Construction Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Raw Process Data
k subgroups x n measurements each] --> B[Compute Subgroup Means
X-bar i = sum of n measurements / n] B --> C[Compute Subgroup Ranges
R-i = X-max minus X-min] C --> D[Compute Grand Mean
X-double-bar = sum X-bar-i / k] C --> E[Compute Average Range
R-bar = sum R-i / k] D --> F[Select Control Chart Constants
A2, D3, D4 from n] E --> F F --> G[Compute X-bar Chart Limits
UCL = X-double-bar + A2 R-bar
CL = X-double-bar
LCL = X-double-bar minus A2 R-bar] F --> H[Compute R Chart Limits
UCL = D4 R-bar
CL = R-bar
LCL = D3 R-bar] G --> I[Plot X-bar i points on X-bar Chart] H --> J[Plot R-i points on R Chart] I --> K{Interpret R Chart FIRST
All R-i within UCL and LCL?} K -->|NO| L[Process Out of Control
Investigate Variability Causes] K -->|YES| M{Interpret X-bar Chart
All X-bar i within UCL and LCL?} M -->|NO| N[Process Out of Control
Investigate Location Causes] M -->|YES| O[Process In Statistical Control
Only Common Cause Variation] O --> P[Optional: Compute Process Capability
sigma-hat = R-bar / d2
Cp, Cpk] ``` ## 4.2 Visual Schematic of X̄ and R Charts Side by Side ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph XBAR [X-BAR CHART - Monitors Process Mean] UCLX[UCL X-bar = 25.0431 mm] CLX[CL X-bar = 25.0154 mm] LCLX[LCL X-bar = 24.9877 mm] end subgraph RCHART [R CHART - Monitors Process Variability] UCLR[UCL R = 0.1015 mm] CLR[CL R = 0.0480 mm] LCLR[LCL R = 0.0000 mm] end XBAR --- RCHART style UCLX fill:#ffcccc style LCLX fill:#ffcccc style UCLR fill:#ffcccc style LCLR fill:#ffcccc style CLX fill:#ccffcc style CLR fill:#ccffcc ``` ## 4.3 Pattern Recognition Decision Tree for Out-of-Control Signals ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Point Plotted on Chart] --> Q1{Point beyond
3-sigma limits?} Q1 -->|YES| A1[Action: Stop Process
Investigate Assignable Cause] Q1 -->|NO| Q2{2 out of 3 consecutive
points beyond 2-sigma
same side?} Q2 -->|YES| A2[Action: Warning
Process Drift Suspected] Q2 -->|NO| Q3{7 or more consecutive
points on one side
of center line?} Q3 -->|YES| A3[Action: Shift in Process Mean
Check Tool Wear or Material] Q3 -->|NO| Q4{7 consecutive points
trending up or down?} Q4 -->|YES| A4[Action: Systematic Trend
Check Gradual Tool Wear] Q4 -->|NO| Q5{15 consecutive points
within plus or minus 1-sigma?} Q5 -->|YES| A5[Action: Over-Controlling
Stratification May Exist] Q5 -->|NO| OK[Process Appears
In Control] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Q: Define control charts for variables. Why are X̄ and R charts plotted together?** **Model Answer:** Control charts for variables are statistical tools used to monitor a measurable quality characteristic (length, weight, diameter) of a process. They are constructed using two statistics: the **mean (X̄)** and the **range (R)** of small subgroups. The X̄ chart detects **shifts in process location (mean)**, while the R chart detects **changes in process variability (spread)**. They are plotted together because: - The X̄ chart limits are computed using R̄ (so R must be valid). - The R chart indicates whether process dispersion is stable. - Only when **both** charts are in control can the process be declared stable. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: For subgroup size n = 5, write the formulas for control limits of X̄ and R charts. What is the value of A₂?** **Model Answer:** For **n = 5**, the standard tabulated constants are **A₂ = 0.577, D₃ = 0, D₄ = 2.114**. The control limits are: $$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \quad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$ $$UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}, \quad LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$$ Since D₃ = 0 for n = 5, LCL_R = 0 (because the range cannot be negative). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` The following table shows measurements (in mm) of inner diameter of bearings taken in **k = 10** subgroups of size **n = 4**: | Subgroup | X₁ | X₂ | X₃ | X₄ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 20.05 | 20.02 | 20.04 | 20.01 | | 2 | 19.98 | 20.00 | 20.03 | 20.02 | | 3 | 20.04 | 20.06 | 20.02 | 20.05 | | 4 | 20.01 | 19.99 | 20.03 | 20.00 | | 5 | 20.02 | 20.05 | 20.00 | 20.03 | | 6 | 20.06 | 20.04 | 20.07 | 20.05 | | 7 | 19.99 | 20.01 | 20.00 | 19.98 | | 8 | 20.03 | 20.05 | 20.02 | 20.04 | | 9 | 20.00 | 19.97 | 20.02 | 19.99 | | 10 | 20.04 | 20.06 | 20.03 | 20.05 | **(a) Construct the X̄ and R charts.** (7 Marks) **(b) Interpret the charts and comment on the state of the process.** (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution for Question A: **Step 1 — Compute X̄ᵢ and Rᵢ for each subgroup:** | i | X̄ᵢ (mm) | Rᵢ (mm) | |---|---|---| | 1 | (20.05+20.02+20.04+20.01)/4 = 20.030 | 20.05−20.01 = 0.04 | | 2 | (19.98+20.00+20.03+20.02)/4 = 20.0075 | 20.03−19.98 = 0.05 | | 3 | (20.04+20.06+20.02+20.05)/4 = 20.0425 | 20.06−20.02 = 0.04 | | 4 | (20.01+19.99+20.03+20.00)/4 = 20.0075 | 20.03−19.99 = 0.04 | | 5 | (20.02+20.05+20.00+20.03)/4 = 20.025 | 20.05−20.00 = 0.05 | | 6 | (20.06+20.04+20.07+20.05)/4 = 20.055 | 20.07−20.04 = 0.03 | | 7 | (19.99+20.01+20.00+19.98)/4 = 19.995 | 20.01−19.98 = 0.03 | | 8 | (20.03+20.05+20.02+20.04)/4 = 20.035 | 20.05−20.02 = 0.03 | | 9 | (20.00+19.97+20.02+19.99)/4 = 19.995 | 20.02−19.97 = 0.05 | | 10 | (20.04+20.06+20.03+20.05)/4 = 20.045 | 20.06−20.03 = 0.03 | **Step 2 — Compute grand averages:** $$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{20.030+20.0075+20.0425+20.0075+20.025+20.055+19.995+20.035+19.995+20.045}{10} = \frac{200.2375}{10} = 20.02375 \text{ mm}$$ $$\bar{R} = \frac{0.04+0.05+0.04+0.04+0.05+0.03+0.03+0.03+0.05+0.03}{10} = \frac{0.39}{10} = 0.039 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3 — For n = 4: A₂ = 0.729, D₃ = 0, D₄ = 2.282.** **Step 4 — X̄ Chart limits:** $$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 20.02375 + (0.729)(0.039) = 20.02375 + 0.02843 = 20.05218 \text{ mm}$$ $$CL_{\bar{X}} = 20.02375 \text{ mm}$$ $$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 20.02375 - 0.02843 = 19.99532 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 5 — R Chart limits:** $$UCL_R = (2.282)(0.039) = 0.088998 \approx 0.0890 \text{ mm}$$ $$CL_R = 0.039 \text{ mm}$$ $$LCL_R = 0 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation:** - **R Chart:** All Rᵢ values (0.03 to 0.05) lie within [0, 0.0890] ✓ → **R chart is in control.** - **X̄ Chart:** Subgroup 6 has X̄₆ = 20.055, which exceeds UCL = 20.0522 by 0.003 mm → **X̄ chart is OUT OF CONTROL** at point 6. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > Students commonly make these errors: > 1. **Forgetting to compute Rᵢ first** — they jump straight to X̄̄. **Marks deducted: 1–2.** > 2. **Using wrong control chart constants** — confusing A₂ for n=4 with n=5. **Marks deducted: 2.** > 3. **Not comparing individual points with limits** — just plotting without numerical verification. **Marks deducted: 2.** > 4. **Writing "in control" without checking all 10 points** — only checking visually on a rough sketch. **Marks deducted: 1.** > 5. **Ignoring that the R chart must be interpreted FIRST** before X̄ chart conclusions are valid. **Conclusion:** The process is **out of statistical control**. At sample 6, an assignable cause has shifted the process mean upwards — likely cause: a sudden change in machine setting, tool change, or raw material lot variation. The process must be stopped and investigated before continuing production. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) Explain the step-by-step procedure for constructing X̄ and R charts with suitable formulas.** (7 Marks) **(b) For a manufacturing process, 12 subgroups of size n = 5 are taken. The grand mean is X̄̄ = 50.20 mm and average range R̄ = 0.60 mm. Compute the control limits for X̄ and R charts and estimate the process standard deviation.** (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) Construction Procedure — Step-by-Step Explanation (7 Marks):** 1. **Define the quality characteristic** to be monitored (e.g., diameter, weight). 2. **Decide subgroup size n** (typically 4 or 5) and sampling frequency. 3. **Collect k ≥ 25 subgroups** (k = 10–15 for academic lab) of measurements in chronological order. 4. **Compute subgroup statistics:** - Subgroup mean: **X̄ᵢ = ΣXᵢⱼ / n** - Subgroup range: **Rᵢ = Xmax − Xmin** 5. **Compute grand statistics:** - **X̄̄ = ΣX̄ᵢ / k** - **R̄ = ΣRᵢ / k** 6. **Select control chart constants A₂, D₃, D₄** for the chosen n. 7. **Compute control limits:** - X̄ chart: UCL = X̄̄ + A₂R̄, CL = X̄̄, LCL = X̄̄ − A₂R̄ - R chart: UCL = D₄R̄, CL = R̄, LCL = D₃R̄ 8. **Plot the points** (X̄ᵢ on X̄ chart; Rᵢ on R chart) in time order and connect them. 9. **Draw center lines and control limits** as horizontal lines. 10. **Interpret** — first the R chart (variability), then the X̄ chart (location). Identify out-of-control patterns and assignable causes. **(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):** Given: **k = 12**, **n = 5**, **X̄̄ = 50.20 mm**, **R̄ = 0.60 mm**. Constants for n = 5: **A₂ = 0.577, D₃ = 0, D₄ = 2.114, d₂ = 2.326**. **X̄ Chart limits:** $$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 50.20 + (0.577)(0.60) = 50.20 + 0.3462 = 50.5462 \text{ mm}$$ $$CL_{\bar{X}} = 50.20 \text{ mm}$$ $$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 50.20 - 0.3462 = 49.8538 \text{ mm}$$ **R Chart limits:** $$UCL_R = (2.114)(0.60) = 1.2684 \text{ mm}$$ $$CL_R = 0.60 \text{ mm}$$ $$LCL_R = (0)(0.60) = 0 \text{ mm}$$ **Process Standard Deviation Estimate:** $$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.60}{2.326} = 0.2580 \text{ mm}$$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **X̄ chart → process mean (location).** R chart → process variability (spread). Both must be in control. - **Subgroup size n:** typically 2–10; commonly **n = 4 or n = 5** in industry and academic labs. - **Minimum subgroups k ≥ 25** for production control charts; **k = 10–15 acceptable** for lab experiments. - **Constants for n = 5 (most tested):** **A₂ = 0.577, D₃ = 0, D₄ = 2.114, d₂ = 2.326.** - **Order of interpretation:** Always interpret the **R chart FIRST**. If R is out of control, X̄ chart limits are unreliable. - **LCL_R = 0** for n ≤ 6 because range cannot be negative. - **3-sigma logic:** Control limits represent μ ± 3σ, capturing 99.73% of variation under normality. - **Out-of-control signals:** Point beyond limits, run of 7+, trends, cycles, stratification. - **Process σ estimate:** **σ̂ = R̄ / d₂** — used later for Cp, Cpk, and Six Sigma studies. - **Center line on X̄ chart = X̄̄** (grand mean); **Center line on R chart = R̄** (average range). - **Common cause vs. assignable cause:** Only assignable causes should trigger process correction. - **Pattern interpretation (Western Electric rules):** Check for shifts, trends, runs, and cycles — all are red flags in KTU exams.

topics.title: Construction and interpretation of C chart from measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL507 - INDUSTRIAL INSPECTION AND QUALITY CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Construction and interpretation of C chart from measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **C-chart (Count Chart)** is a type of statistical process control (SPC) chart used in **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** to monitor the number of **non-conformities (defects)** in a sample or inspection unit when the **sample size (n) is constant**. It is specifically designed for **counting-type quality characteristics** where the variable of interest follows a **Poisson distribution** model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCPEL507 - Module 2):**
> The C-chart plots the **number of defects per unit (c)** against the **sample number**, with control limits established as **three-sigma (3σ) limits** above and below the **process mean (c̄)**. It is the principal attribute control chart used when both *sample size* and *opportunity for defects* remain uniform.

### Key Terminology for KTU Examinations

| Term | Definition | KTU Notation |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Non-conformity** | A quality characteristic that does not meet the specified requirement | Defect |
| **Defect** | A specific instance of non-conformity on an inspected unit | Single occurrence |
| **Sample Size (n)** | Number of inspection units in each subgroup | Must be **constant** |
| **Mean Defects (c̄)** | Average number of defects per unit across all samples | Center Line (CL) |
| **UCL** | Upper Control Limit | c̄ + 3√c̄ |
| **LCL** | Lower Control Limit | c̄ − 3√c̄ |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are a **quality inspector at a garment factory**. Your job is to count the number of **loose threads, stains, or stitching errors** on each shirt that comes off the production line. Every day, you pick **one shirt (or a fixed bundle of 5 shirts)** and count all the defects you find on it.

- If the factory is running smoothly, you might find around **3–4 defects per shirt** every single day.
- One day, suddenly, you find **15 defects on a single shirt**. 🚨 This is **unusual** — something has gone wrong (maybe a new trainee, a broken needle, or a bad fabric batch).
- Another day, you find **0 defects** — also unusual, possibly because the machine was idle or someone cherry-picked the shirt.

The **C-chart** is simply a **graphical alarm system**. It plots your daily defect counts on paper, draws **three lines** (a center line and two control limits), and tells you:
- ✅ "Process is in control" — points fall **inside** the limits
- ❌ "Process is out of control" — points fall **outside** the limits or show **non-random patterns**

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Idea:** The C-chart assumes defects occur **randomly and independently** across an inspection unit (like raindrops on a surface). If a sudden spike appears, it signals that a **special cause of variation** has entered the process — and the engineer must investigate.

### Real-World Industrial Applications

- **Automotive:** Counting paint defects (runs, sags, orange peel) on car body panels
- **Electronics:** Counting solder joint defects on printed circuit boards (PCBs)
- **Textile:** Counting weaving defects per square meter of fabric
- **Glass Industry:** Counting bubbles, scratches, and inclusions per sheet of glass
- **Paper Industry:** Counting dirt specks per 100 m² of paper roll

---

## 1.3 Underlying Probability Distribution

The C-chart is mathematically derived from the **Poisson Distribution**, which models the **probability of a given number of events occurring in a fixed interval of time or space** when these events happen with a known constant mean rate and independently of the time since the last event.

The Poisson probability mass function is:

$$P(c) = \frac{e^{-\bar{c}} \cdot \bar{c}^{c}}{c!}$$

Where:
- $P(c)$ = Probability of observing exactly $c$ defects
- $\bar{c}$ = Mean number of defects (process average)
- $e$ = Mathematical constant ≈ **2.71828**
- $c!$ = Factorial of $c$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption for Validity of C-Chart:**
> 1. Defects must occur **independently** of each other
> 2. Defects must occur with a **constant mean rate**
> 3. The **sample size (n)** must remain **constant** across all subgroups
> 4. The probability of more than one defect at the exact same point is **negligible**

---

## 1.4 Relationship with Other Attribute Charts (KTU Comparison)

| Chart Type | Variable Plotted | Sample Size | When to Use |
|------------|------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **p-chart** | Proportion of defective items | Variable (n varies) | Fraction of *items* defective |
| **np-chart** | Number of defective items | Constant (n fixed) | Count of *items* defective |
| **c-chart** ✅ | Number of defects per unit | **Constant** | Count of *defects* in a single unit |
| **u-chart** | Defects per unit (average) | Variable (n varies) | Count of *defects* across varying sample sizes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Defective** = the *entire item* is rejected (p-chart, np-chart)
> - **Defect** = a *single flaw* on an otherwise acceptable item (c-chart, u-chart)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of the C-Chart

The C-chart construction is based on the fundamental principle of **statistical hypothesis testing** applied to manufacturing processes. The core philosophy is to **distinguish between two types of process variation**:

1. **Common Cause Variation (Chance Cause):** Inherent, random, statistical variation that is **always present** in any stable process.
2. **Special Cause Variation (Assignable Cause):** External, non-random disturbances that **change the process** and must be identified and eliminated.

The **three-sigma (3σ) control limits** establish a **statistical fence** such that:
- For a Poisson process, the **variance equals the mean** ($\sigma^2 = \bar{c}$)
- Therefore, the **standard deviation** is $\sigma = \sqrt{\bar{c}}$
- The control limits are set at **±3 standard deviations** from the mean
- This creates a **99.73% probability** that any plotted point will fall within the limits **if the process is in control**

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for C-Chart Construction

The construction of a C-chart follows a **systematic six-step procedure** that must be memorized for KTU practical examinations:

### Step 1: Data Collection
- Collect **k subgroups** (typically k = 20 to 30) of measurements
- Each subgroup must have the **same sample size (n)**
- Count the number of defects ($c_i$) in each subgroup
- Record data in a tabular format

### Step 2: Compute the Process Mean
- Sum all defect counts: $\sum_{i=1}^{k} c_i$
- Divide by the number of subgroups (k): $\bar{c} = \frac{\sum c_i}{k}$

### Step 3: Calculate the Standard Deviation
- For Poisson distribution: $\sigma_c = \sqrt{\bar{c}}$

### Step 4: Establish the Center Line (CL)
- The center line is the process mean: $CL = \bar{c}$

### Step 5: Calculate the Control Limits
- Upper Control Limit: $UCL = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$
- Lower Control Limit: $LCL = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$
- **Rule:** If $LCL < 0$, set $LCL = 0$ (defects cannot be negative)

### Step 6: Plot and Interpret
- Plot the sample numbers on the **x-axis**
- Plot the defect counts ($c_i$) on the **y-axis**
- Draw horizontal lines for CL, UCL, and LCL
- Plot each $c_i$ value as a point
- Connect points with straight lines (optional)
- Analyze for **out-of-control signals**

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — These formulas appear in nearly every KTU exam question on C-charts.**

| Parameter | Formula | Description | Units |
|-----------|---------|-------------|-------|
| **Process Mean** | $\bar{c} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} c_i}{k}$ | Average defects per unit | defects/unit |
| **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma_c = \sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Poisson process standard deviation | defects/unit |
| **Center Line (CL)** | $CL = \bar{c}$ | Process average | defects/unit |
| **Upper Control Limit** | $UCL = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Upper statistical boundary | defects/unit |
| **Lower Control Limit** | $LCL = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Lower statistical boundary | defects/unit |
| **LCL Adjustment Rule** | $LCL = \max(0, \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}})$ | If negative, set to zero | defects/unit |
| **Trial UCL** (after revision) | $UCL_{new} = \bar{c}_{new} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}_{new}}$ | After removing out-of-control points | defects/unit |
| **Process Capability** | $C_p = \dfrac{UCL - LCL}{6\sigma} = 1.0$ (theoretical) | For 3-sigma design | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** The value of $3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ is the **three-sigma control limit width**. Always carry this calculation to **at least 3 decimal places** in KTU exams to avoid precision loss marks.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The C-chart is not merely an academic exercise — it is a **cornerstone tool of Six Sigma, Lean Manufacturing, and ISO 9001 quality management systems**. Its industrial applications include:

| Industry Sector | Application of C-Chart |
|-----------------|------------------------|
| **Semiconductor Manufacturing** | Counting photolithography defects per wafer |
| **Aerospace** | Counting surface flaws on turbine blades |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Counting particulate matter in injectable solutions |
| **Steel Manufacturing** | Counting surface inclusions per square meter of sheet |
| **Packaging** | Counting print misalignments per 1000 labels |
| **Foundry** | Counting sand inclusions per casting |

### Why C-Chart Matters in Industry

- **Early Warning System:** Detects process drift before defective products reach the customer
- **Cost Reduction:** Prevents scrap, rework, and warranty claims
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Required by **ISO 9001, IATF 16949, AS9100** standards
- **Continuous Improvement:** Forms the statistical backbone of **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) methodology
- **Supplier Quality Management:** Used to monitor incoming material quality

---

## 2.5 Out-of-Control Signals (Western Electric Rules)

Beyond simple points falling outside the control limits, the following **non-random patterns** also indicate an out-of-control process:

| Rule # | Pattern Description | Interpretation |
|--------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | One point beyond ±3σ limits | Sudden process shift |
| 2 | 2 of 3 consecutive points beyond ±2σ (same side) | Process trending |
| 3 | 4 of 5 consecutive points beyond ±1σ (same side) | Process drift |
| 4 | 8 consecutive points on one side of center line | Sustained shift |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often check ONLY for points outside the control limits. Examiners specifically test knowledge of **pattern-based rules** (Western Electric / Nelson rules) in Part A questions. Always mention at least one pattern rule in your interpretation.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation of C-Chart Control Limits

### Derivation 1: Why the Standard Deviation Equals √c̄

The Poisson distribution has a unique property derived from its probability mass function. The variance and mean are both equal to the parameter $\lambda$ (here denoted as $\bar{c}$).

**Starting Point — Poisson PMF:**

$$P(c) = \frac{e^{-\bar{c}} \cdot \bar{c}^{c}}{c!}$$

**Variance of Poisson Distribution (Standard Result):**

$$\sigma^2 = E[(c - \bar{c})^2] = E[c^2] - (E[c])^2$$

For a Poisson random variable, this evaluates to:

$$\sigma^2 = \bar{c} \implies \sigma = \sqrt{\bar{c}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** In a Poisson process, the **mean** of the distribution equals its **variance**. This is the mathematical foundation that allows us to use $\bar{c}$ alone to determine control limits — no separate variance estimate is needed.

---

### Derivation 2: Three-Sigma Control Limit Equations

**Step 1 — Define the Shewhart 3-Sigma Control Limit Principle:**

A process is considered "in control" if the plotted statistic lies within three standard deviations of its expected value. The general control limit formula is:

$$L = \mu \pm 3\sigma$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Poisson Parameters for c-chart:**

For the C-chart, we substitute $\mu = \bar{c}$ and $\sigma = \sqrt{\bar{c}}$:

$$L = \bar{c} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$$

**Step 3 — Separate into Upper and Lower Limits:**

$$UCL = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$$

$$LCL = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Non-Negativity Constraint:**

Since a defect count cannot be negative:

$$LCL = \max\left(0, \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}\right)$$

---

## 3.2 Complete Worked Example (KTU Lab Exam Standard)

### Problem Statement

> A quality control inspector at an **automotive paint shop** is monitoring the number of paint defects per car body panel. The inspector examines **20 randomly selected panels** each day, and counts the total number of defects (runs, sags, orange peel, dust nibs) on each panel. The data collected over **20 days** is shown below. **Construct the C-chart and interpret the process state.**

### Given Data Table

| Sample No. | Defects ($c_i$) | Sample No. | Defects ($c_i$) |
|:----------:|:---------------:|:----------:|:---------------:|
| 1 | 4 | 11 | 5 |
| 2 | 6 | 12 | 3 |
| 3 | 3 | 13 | 4 |
| 4 | 5 | 14 | 7 |
| 5 | 2 | 15 | 4 |
| 6 | 4 | 16 | 5 |
| 7 | 6 | 17 | 3 |
| 8 | 3 | 18 | 4 |
| 9 | 5 | 19 | 2 |
| 10 | 4 | 20 | 5 |

**Total number of subgroups (k) = 20**
**Sample size (n) = 1 panel per sample (constant)** ✅

---

### Step 1: Calculate the Sum of Defects

$$\sum_{i=1}^{20} c_i = 4+6+3+5+2+4+6+3+5+4+5+3+4+7+4+5+3+4+2+5$$

Grouping for clarity:

$$\sum c_i = (4+6+3+5+2) + (4+6+3+5+4) + (5+3+4+7+4) + (5+3+4+2+5)$$

$$\sum c_i = 20 + 22 + 23 + 19 = 84$$

---

### Step 2: Calculate the Process Mean (Center Line)

$$\bar{c} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} c_i}{k} = \frac{84}{20}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{c} = 4.2 \text{ defects per panel}}$$

This is the **Center Line (CL)** of the C-chart.

---

### Step 3: Calculate the Standard Deviation

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\bar{c}} = \sqrt{4.2}$$

$$\sigma_c = 2.0494 \text{ defects per panel}$$

---

### Step 4: Calculate the Three-Sigma Control Limits

**Upper Control Limit:**

$$UCL = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} = 4.2 + 3(2.0494)$$

$$UCL = 4.2 + 6.1482$$

$$\boxed{UCL = 10.348 \text{ defects per panel}}$$

**Lower Control Limit:**

$$LCL = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} = 4.2 - 3(2.0494)$$

$$LCL = 4.2 - 6.1482 = -1.9482$$

Since this is negative, apply the non-negativity rule:

$$\boxed{LCL = 0 \text{ defects per panel}}$$

---

### Step 5: Summary of Control Chart Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Interpretation |
|-----------|--------|-------|----------------|
| Center Line | CL | **4.200** | Average defects per panel |
| Upper Control Limit | UCL | **10.348** | Maximum acceptable random variation |
| Lower Control Limit | LCL | **0.000** | Adjusted from negative value |
| Standard Deviation | σ | **2.049** | Process variability |
| Number of Subgroups | k | 20 | Statistical adequacy |

---

### Step 6: Plot the C-Chart (Data Points)

| Sample | $c_i$ | vs UCL (10.348) | vs LCL (0) | Status |
|:------:|:-----:|:---------------:|:----------:|:------:|
| 1 | 4 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 2 | 6 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 3 | 3 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 4 | 5 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 5 | 2 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 6 | 4 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 7 | 6 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 8 | 3 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 9 | 5 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 10 | 4 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 11 | 5 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 12 | 3 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 13 | 4 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 14 | 7 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 15 | 4 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 16 | 5 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 17 | 3 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 18 | 4 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 19 | 2 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |
| 20 | 5 | Inside | Inside | ✅ In-control |

---

### Step 7: Interpretation of the C-Chart

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:**
> All 20 sample points fall **within the control limits** (0 ≤ $c_i$ ≤ 10.348). The process variation appears **random** with no visible trends, runs, or cycles. The paint defect process is **statistically in control** at an average of **4.2 defects per panel**. However, the engineering team should note that 4.2 defects per panel may still be **unacceptable from a quality standpoint** — statistical control does **not** imply specification compliance. A **process capability study** against customer specifications should be conducted.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Framework for C-Chart Interpretation

| Observed Pattern | Statistically Valid? | Likely Assignable Cause | Recommended Action |
|------------------|---------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| All points within limits, random pattern | ✅ In-control | Common cause variation | Continue monitoring |
| 1 point above UCL | ❌ Out-of-control | Sudden process upset | Investigate immediately, find special cause |
| 1 point below LCL (when LCL > 0) | ❌ Out-of-control | Measurement error, over-inspection | Verify inspection method |
| 7+ points trending upward | ❌ Out-of-control | Tool wear, gradual degradation | Schedule preventive maintenance |
| 8+ points above CL | ❌ Out-of-control | Process mean shift | Recalibrate equipment |
| 8+ points below CL | ❌ Out-of-control | Process improvement, new method | Document and standardize |
| Cyclic pattern | ❌ Out-of-control | Environmental cycle (shift, temperature) | Investigate cyclic factors |

---

## 3.4 Revised Control Limits (Trial Limits)

If any points had exceeded the control limits, we would **revise the limits** by removing those points and recalculating:

**Step A — Remove Out-of-Control Points:**

$$\sum c_{i,\text{revised}} = \sum c_i - \sum c_{i,\text{out-of-control}}$$

**Step B — Recalculate Mean:**

$$\bar{c}_{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum c_{i,\text{revised}}}{k_{\text{revised}}}$$

**Step C — Recalculate Limits:**

$$UCL_{\text{new}} = \bar{c}_{\text{new}} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}_{\text{new}}}$$

$$LCL_{\text{new}} = \bar{c}_{\text{new}} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}_{\text{new}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Exam Tip:** Always attempt to **recalculate revised limits** even if no points are out of control. Examiners award bonus marks for demonstrating the ability to handle both in-control and out-of-control scenarios.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — C-Chart Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect Defect Data] --> B{Is Sample Size Constant?}
    B -- No --> C[Use U-chart instead]
    B -- Yes --> D[Count Defects per Subgroup ci]
    D --> E[Tabulate k Subgroups]
    E --> F[Compute Sum of Defects]
    F --> G[Calculate Process Mean: c-bar = Sum ci / k]
    G --> H[Compute Std Deviation: sigma = sqrt c-bar]
    H --> I[Set Center Line: CL = c-bar]
    H --> J[Compute UCL = c-bar + 3 sqrt c-bar]
    H --> K[Compute LCL = c-bar - 3 sqrt c-bar]
    K --> L{Is LCL Negative?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Set LCL = 0]
    L -- No --> N[Keep LCL Value]
    M --> O[Plot C-chart with CL UCL LCL]
    N --> O
    O --> P{Any Point Outside Limits?}
    P -- No --> Q[Process In Control: Continue Monitoring]
    P -- Yes --> R[Identify Assignable Cause]
    R --> S[Remove Out-of-Control Points]
    S --> T[Recalculate Revised Limits]
    T --> O
    Q --> U[End: Report Process Status]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define C-chart and state its application. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **C-chart (Count chart)** is a type of attribute control chart used in Statistical Quality Control (SQC) to monitor the **number of non-conformities (defects)** in a sample of **constant size**. It is based on the **Poisson distribution** and plots the number of defects per unit against the sample number, with control limits calculated as the process mean ± three standard deviations.

**Application:** The C-chart is widely used in industries such as **automotive paint inspection, electronics soldering quality, glass manufacturing, and fabric defect monitoring** where the number of defects per unit must be tracked over time to detect process disturbances. **It is specifically used when the sample size remains constant** across all subgroups and the variable of interest is the **count of defects** rather than the proportion of defective items. *(3 Marks)*

---

### Question 2: What are the control limits of a C-chart? Why is the LCL sometimes set to zero? [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The control limits of a C-chart are:

$$UCL = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$$

$$CL = \bar{c}$$

$$LCL = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$$

where $\bar{c}$ is the average number of defects per unit.

**Reason for LCL = 0:** The LCL is sometimes set to **zero** when the calculated value of $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ becomes **negative**. Since a defect count cannot be physically negative, the LCL is mathematically truncated to zero. This commonly occurs when $\bar{c}$ is small (less than 9), making the lower 3-sigma bound fall below zero. *(3 Marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Construction and Interpretation of C-Chart [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, May 2024]**

A textile company monitors weaving defects in fabric rolls. The number of defects counted in 25 random samples (each of size 1 m²) is given below. **Construct the C-chart, calculate the control limits, and interpret whether the process is in control.**

| Sample | Defects | Sample | Defects | Sample | Defects | Sample | Defects | Sample | Defects |
|:------:|:-------:|:------:|:-------:|:------:|:-------:|:------:|:-------:|:------:|:-------:|
| 1 | 2 | 6 | 4 | 11 | 3 | 16 | 5 | 21 | 3 |
| 2 | 3 | 7 | 1 | 12 | 4 | 17 | 2 | 22 | 4 |
| 3 | 5 | 8 | 3 | 13 | 2 | 18 | 6 | 23 | 2 |
| 4 | 4 | 9 | 5 | 14 | 3 | 19 | 4 | 24 | 3 |
| 5 | 2 | 10 | 4 | 15 | 4 | 20 | 3 | 25 | 5 |

#### Part (a): Calculate the control limits for the C-chart. [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Sum of Defects**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{25} c_i = 2+3+5+4+2 + 4+1+3+5+4 + 3+4+2+3+4 + 5+2+6+4+3 + 3+4+2+3+5$$

Grouping:
- Samples 1-5: 2+3+5+4+2 = 16
- Samples 6-10: 4+1+3+5+4 = 17
- Samples 11-15: 3+4+2+3+4 = 16
- Samples 16-20: 5+2+6+4+3 = 20
- Samples 21-25: 3+4+2+3+5 = 17

$$\sum c_i = 16 + 17 + 16 + 20 + 17 = 86$$

**Step 2: Process Mean**

$$\bar{c} = \frac{86}{25} = 3.44 \text{ defects per m}^2$$

**Step 3: Standard Deviation**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{3.44} = 1.855$$

**Step 4: Control Limits**

$$UCL = 3.44 + 3(1.855) = 3.44 + 5.564 = 9.004$$

$$LCL = 3.44 - 3(1.855) = 3.44 - 5.564 = -2.124$$

Since LCL is negative:

$$LCL = 0$$

**Final Control Limits:**

$$\boxed{CL = 3.44, \quad UCL = 9.00, \quad LCL = 0}$$

#### Part (b): Interpret the C-chart and state your conclusions. [7 Marks]

**Interpretation Table:**

| Sample | $c_i$ | Position | Status |
|:------:|:-----:|:--------:|:------:|
| 1 | 2 | Within (0 to 9) | ✅ In-control |
| 2 | 3 | Within | ✅ In-control |
| 3 | 5 | Within | ✅ In-control |
| 4 | 4 | Within | ✅ In-control |
| 5 | 2 | Within | ✅ In-control |
| 6 | 4 | Within | ✅ In-control |
| 7 | 1 | Within | ✅ In-control |
| 8 | 3 | Within | ✅ In-control |
| 9 | 5 | Within | ✅ In-control |
| 10 | 4 | Within | ✅ In-control |
| 11-25 | 2-6 | All within | ✅ In-control |

**Conclusion (7 Marks):**

1. **All 25 sample points** fall **within the control limits** (0 ≤ $c_i$ ≤ 9.00).
2. The process is **statistically in control** with an average of **3.44 defects per m²**.
3. The variation observed is consistent with **common cause variation** inherent in the weaving process.
4. **No assignable cause** of variation is evident at this stage.
5. **Recommendation:** The process should continue to be monitored using the same C-chart. However, the engineering team should evaluate whether **3.44 defects/m²** is acceptable against **customer specifications** — statistical control does not guarantee specification compliance.
6. If customer requirements are stricter, a **process improvement initiative** (e.g., Six Sigma DMAIC project) should be undertaken to reduce the average defect count.

---

### Question B: C-Chart with Revised Limits [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

In a printed circuit board (PCB) manufacturing line, the number of solder defects per board is recorded for 20 samples. The data is:

| Sample | Defects | Sample | Defects |
|:------:|:-------:|:------:|:-------:|
| 1 | 3 | 11 | 4 |
| 2 | 5 | 12 | 2 |
| 3 | 4 | 13 | 5 |
| 4 | 6 | 14 | 3 |
| 5 | 2 | 15 | 4 |
| 6 | 4 | 16 | 12 |
| 7 | 3 | 17 | 5 |
| 8 | 5 | 18 | 3 |
| 9 | 4 | 19 | 4 |
| 10 | 6 | 20 | 5 |

**(a) Construct the initial C-chart and identify any out-of-control points. (b) Revise the control limits after removing out-of-control points and provide a final interpretation.**

#### Part (a): Initial C-Chart Construction [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Sum of Defects**

$$\sum c_i = 3+5+4+6+2+4+3+5+4+6+4+2+5+3+4+12+5+3+4+5$$

$$\sum c_i = 3+5+4+6+2 = 20 \text{ (Samples 1-5)}$$

$$\sum c_i = 4+3+5+4+6 = 22 \text{ (Samples 6-10)}$$

$$\sum c_i = 4+2+5+3+4 = 18 \text{ (Samples 11-15)}$$

$$\sum c_i = 12+5+3+4+5 = 29 \text{ (Samples 16-20)}$$

$$\sum c_i = 20+22+18+29 = 89$$

**Step 2: Process Mean**

$$\bar{c} = \frac{89}{20} = 4.45$$

**Step 3: Standard Deviation**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{4.45} = 2.1096$$

**Step 4: Initial Control Limits**

$$UCL = 4.45 + 3(2.1096) = 4.45 + 6.3288 = 10.7788$$

$$LCL = 4.45 - 6.3288 = -1.8788 \implies LCL = 0$$

**Initial Limits:** CL = 4.45, UCL = 10.78, LCL = 0

**Step 5: Identify Out-of-Control Points**

Examining the data: **Sample 16 has 12 defects**, which is **above UCL = 10.78**.

$$\boxed{\text{Sample 16 is OUT-OF-CONTROL}}$$

This point indicates a **special cause of variation** that must be investigated. Possible causes: solder paste contamination, reflow oven temperature excursion, or operator error.

#### Part (b): Revised Control Limits [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Remove Out-of-Control Point**

$$\sum c_{i,\text{revised}} = 89 - 12 = 77$$

$$k_{\text{revised}} = 20 - 1 = 19$$

**Step 2: Revised Process Mean**

$$\bar{c}_{\text{new}} = \frac{77}{19} = 4.0526$$

**Step 3: Revised Standard Deviation**

$$\sigma_{\text{new}} = \sqrt{4.0526} = 2.0131$$

**Step 4: Revised Control Limits**

$$UCL_{\text{new}} = 4.0526 + 3(2.0131) = 4.0526 + 6.0394 = 10.092$$

$$LCL_{\text{new}} = 4.0526 - 6.0394 = -1.987 \implies LCL = 0$$

**Revised Final Limits:**

$$\boxed{CL = 4.05, \quad UCL = 10.09, \quad LCL = 0}$$

**Final Interpretation:**

After removing the assignable cause (Sample 16), the process mean has decreased from 4.45 to 4.05 defects per board. The revised C-chart shows that all remaining 19 points are within the new control limits. The process is now **statistically in control** at an improved level. The engineering team should:
- **Document the assignable cause** (e.g., solder paste batch issue) for future reference
- **Implement preventive measures** to avoid recurrence
- **Use the revised limits** for ongoing SPC monitoring

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS MISTAKES IN C-CHART QUESTIONS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the LCL truncation rule (Loss: 1 mark):** When $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} < 0$, students often write a negative LCL. Always apply: $LCL = \max(0, \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}})$.
>
> 2. **Not stating the Poisson distribution assumption (Loss: 1 mark):** C-chart formulas are valid only under Poisson assumption. Always mention that **mean = variance** for Poisson.
>
> 3. **Confusing C-chart with p-chart (Loss: 2-3 marks):** C-chart is for **defects (count of flaws)**, NOT for **defectives (count of rejected items)**. Mixing these is a fatal error.
>
> 4. **Skipping the unit/sample size verification (Loss: 1 mark):** C-chart requires **constant sample size**. Always verify and state this assumption explicitly.
>
> 5. **Failure to interpret out-of-control points (Loss: 2 marks):** Simply plotting is not enough. You MUST **discuss** whether points are in/out of control and **suggest corrective actions** for out-of-control signals.
>
> 6. **Calculation precision errors (Loss: 1 mark):** Use at least 3 decimal places in intermediate steps. Final answers should be rounded appropriately.
>
> 7. **Not discussing Western Electric / pattern rules (Loss: 1 mark):** Points within limits but showing **runs or trends** are also out-of-control. Mention this in your interpretation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION NOTES — Read before every KTU exam:**

### Core Concepts to Memorize

- ☐ **C-chart** monitors **defects per unit** (count of flaws), not defective items
- ☐ Sample size (n) must be **constant** for C-chart validity
- ☐ Based on **Poisson distribution**: $\mu = \sigma^2 = \bar{c}$
- ☐ **Three formulas to memorize:** $CL = \bar{c}$, $UCL = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$, $LCL = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$
- ☐ Apply **LCL = 0 rule** when calculated LCL is negative
- ☐ $\bar{c} = \frac{\sum c_i}{k}$ where k is the number of subgroups
- ☐ Out-of-control signals: points outside limits OR non-random patterns

### KTU Exam-Specific Pointers

- ☐ Typical question weightage: **3 marks (definition) + 14 marks (full construction)**
- ☐ Standard KTU format: Part (a) calculation (7 marks) + Part (b) interpretation (7 marks)
- ☐ Always show **tabulated data and intermediate calculations**
- ☐ Always **state assumptions** (Poisson, constant sample size, independence)
- ☐ Always **conclude with engineering recommendations**, not just numerical answers
- ☐ Practice plotting: examiners often expect a **sketch of the C-chart**
- ☐ Be ready to handle **revised limits** questions (remove out-of-control points and recalculate)

### Common Numerical Values to Remember

- ☐ When $\bar{c} = 1$, control limits are 0 to 4
- ☐ When $\bar{c} = 4$, control limits are approximately 0 to 10
- ☐ When $\bar{c} = 9$, control limits are 0 to 18 (LCL becomes 0)
- ☐ Rule of thumb: $3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ defines the "control margin"

### Distinguish From Other Charts

- ☐ **p-chart:** proportion defective (variable n)
- ☐ **np-chart:** number defective (constant n)
- ☐ **c-chart:** number of defects (constant n) ✅
- ☐ **u-chart:** defects per unit (variable n)
- ☐ **X̄-R chart:** variable data (measurements)
- ☐ **X̄-σ chart:** variable data (standard deviation known)

### Industrial Applications to Mention

- ☐ Paint defects, solder defects, fabric defects, glass defects, paper defects
- ☐ Mention ISO 9001 / IATF 16949 compliance for higher marks
- ☐ Connect to **Six Sigma DMAIC** methodology in Part B answers

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction and interpretation of OC curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL507 - INDUSTRIAL INSPECTION AND QUALITY CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Construction and interpretation of OC curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve** is a graphical representation that depicts the discriminatory power of an acceptance sampling plan. It plots the **probability of acceptance ($P_a$)** of a lot against the **incoming lot fraction defective ($p$)**, for a given sample size ($n$) and acceptance number ($c$).

Mathematically, the OC curve is the locus of points:
$$P_a = f(p \mid n, c)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The OC curve is the *backbone* of all acceptance sampling decisions. It quantifies the risk associated with accepting or rejecting a lot of given quality. Every standard published by BIS (IS 2500), MIL-STD-414, and Dodge-Romig tables is built upon OC curve mathematics.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **medical diagnostic test** for a disease:
- The test is run on a sample of patients ($n$).
- If ≤ $c$ positive cases are found, the population is declared "healthy" (lot accepted).
- The OC curve tells the doctor: *"If 1% of the population is actually sick, what is the probability the test will wrongly clear them?"*

In manufacturing: 
- **Producer's view:** "If I send good quality (low $p$), what is the chance the inspector will reject it?" → This is **Producer's Risk ($\alpha$)**.
- **Consumer's view:** "If bad quality (high $p$) is submitted, what is the chance it will slip through?" → This is **Consumer's Risk ($\beta$)**.

The OC curve is essentially a **risk-profile fingerprint** for any sampling plan.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Acceptable Quality Level (AQL)** = the $p$ value at which $P_a$ is high (typically 0.95).
- **Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD)** = the $p$ value at which $P_a$ is low (typically 0.10).
- **Producer's Risk ($\alpha$)** = 1 − $P_a$ at AQL.
- **Consumer's Risk ($\beta$)** = $P_a$ at LTPD.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Ratio (OR)** = LTPD / AQL — A smaller OR indicates a *steeper* (more discriminating) OC curve.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of OC Curve

When a sample of size $n$ is drawn from a lot with fraction defective $p$, the number of defectives $d$ in the sample follows a **Binomial distribution** (for large lots) or **Hypergeometric distribution** (for finite lots).

### The Probability of Acceptance ($P_a$)

The lot is accepted if the number of defectives in the sample is ≤ $c$. So:

$$P_a(p) = P(d \le c) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} \, p^{d} \, (1-p)^{n-d}$$

This is the **Type B OC curve** (binomial-based, for infinite lot model).

## 2.2 Approximations Used in Construction

| Approximation | When Used | Formula for $P_a$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Binomial (exact)** | All cases (small $n$) | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ |
| **Poisson** | $n \ge 50$, $p \le 0.10$, $np \le 10$ | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{d}}{d!}$ where $\lambda = np$ |
| **Normal** | $n > 50$, $0.1 < p < 0.9$, $np, n(1-p) > 5$ | $P_a = \Phi\!\left( \dfrac{c + 0.5 - np}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}} \right)$ |

## 2.3 Characteristics of an Ideal vs. Real OC Curve

- **Ideal OC curve:** A vertical step at $p = p_0$ (perfect discrimination — impossible in practice).
- **Real OC curve:** A smooth S-shaped curve sloping from upper-left (high $P_a$ at low $p$) to lower-right (low $P_a$ at high $p$).
- **Steeper curve** → better discrimination → larger sample size $n$.
- **Shallower curve** → poor discrimination → smaller $n$ or higher $c$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Probability of Acceptance | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Exact (Binomial) |
| 2 | Poisson $P_a$ | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^{d}}{d!}$ | Valid for $p \le 0.10$, $n \ge 50$ |
| 3 | Normal $P_a$ | $P_a = \Phi\!\left( \dfrac{c+0.5 - np}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}} \right)$ | With continuity correction |
| 4 | Producer's Risk | $\alpha = 1 - P_a(\text{AQL})$ | Typically $\alpha = 0.05$ |
| 5 | Consumer's Risk | $\beta = P_a(\text{LTPD})$ | Typically $\beta = 0.10$ |
| 6 | Operating Ratio | $\text{OR} = \dfrac{\text{LTPD}}{\text{AQL}}$ | Smaller OR = sharper curve |
| 7 | AOQL (Avg. Outgoing Quality Limit) | $\text{AOQL} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | For rectifying inspection |
| 8 | ASN (Avg. Sample Number) | $\text{ASN} = n_1 + (1-P_{a1})n_2$ | For double sampling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** OC curves are the basis for every MIL-STD-105E, ISO 2859-1, and BIS IS 2500 sampling table. Quality engineers in automotive (IATF 16949), pharma, and electronics industries use OC curves daily to design incoming inspection plans.

## 2.5 Type A vs Type B OC Curves

| Feature | Type A | Type B |
|---|---|---|
| Lot model | Finite lot, hypergeometric | Infinite lot, binomial/Poisson |
| Validity | More realistic for small lots | Standard textbook approach |
| Use | Research, simulation | KTU examinations & standard tables |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Binomial-Based $P_a$

Consider a lot of $N$ items with $D$ defectives, fraction defective $p = D/N$. A random sample of $n$ items is drawn. The probability of getting exactly $d$ defectives (hypergeometric):

$$P(d) = \frac{\binom{D}{d}\binom{N-D}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}$$

For **infinite lot** ($N \to \infty$, with $p$ fixed), this reduces to the Binomial:

$$P(d) = \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}$$

The lot is accepted if $d \le c$. So:

$$P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}$$

## 3.2 Reduction to Poisson Approximation

When $n$ is large and $p$ is small (with $np = \lambda$ finite), using limits:

$$\lim_{n\to\infty}\binom{n}{d} p^{d}(1-p)^{n-d} = \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^{d}}{d!}$$

Hence:

$$P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^{d}}{d!}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Construction of OC Curve (KTU Standard)

**Given:** Sampling plan with $n = 50$, $c = 2$. Construct the OC curve for $p = 0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, 0.10$.

Since $n = 50 \ge 50$ and $p \le 0.10$, **Poisson approximation** is appropriate. Here $\lambda = np$.

### Step 1: p = 0.01, λ = 0.50

$$P_a = e^{-0.50}\left[\frac{(0.5)^0}{0!} + \frac{(0.5)^1}{1!} + \frac{(0.5)^2}{2!}\right]$$

$$P_a = 0.6065 \times \left[1 + 0.500 + 0.125\right] = 0.6065 \times 1.625 = 0.9856$$

### Step 2: p = 0.02, λ = 1.00

$$P_a = e^{-1.00}\left[1 + 1 + \frac{1}{2}\right] = 0.3679 \times 2.500 = 0.9197$$

### Step 3: p = 0.03, λ = 1.50

$$P_a = e^{-1.50}\left[1 + 1.5 + \frac{(1.5)^2}{2}\right] = 0.2231 \times 3.625 = 0.8088$$

### Step 4: p = 0.05, λ = 2.50

$$P_a = e^{-2.50}\left[1 + 2.5 + \frac{(2.5)^2}{2}\right] = 0.0821 \times 6.625 = 0.5438$$

### Step 5: p = 0.07, λ = 3.50

$$P_a = e^{-3.50}\left[1 + 3.5 + \frac{(3.5)^2}{2}\right] = 0.0302 \times 10.625 = 0.3209$$

### Step 6: p = 0.10, λ = 5.00

$$P_a = e^{-5.00}\left[1 + 5 + \frac{25}{2}\right] = 0.00674 \times 18.500 = 0.1247$$

## 3.4 Compiled Results Table

| $p$ | $\lambda = np$ | $P_a$ (computed) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.005 | 0.25 | 0.9982 |
| 0.010 | 0.50 | 0.9856 |
| 0.020 | 1.00 | 0.9197 |
| 0.030 | 1.50 | 0.8088 |
| 0.050 | 2.50 | 0.5438 |
| 0.070 | 3.50 | 0.3209 |
| 0.100 | 5.00 | 0.1247 |
| 0.150 | 7.50 | 0.0206 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:**
> - At $p = 0.01$ (1% defective), there is a **98.56% chance** the lot is accepted — good for the producer.
> - At $p = 0.10$ (10% defective), only a **12.47% chance** of acceptance — protecting the consumer.

## 3.5 Extracting AQL, LTPD, α, β

From the table above:

- **AQL at $P_a = 0.95$** → corresponds to $p \approx 0.015$ (between 0.01 and 0.02 by interpolation).
- **LTPD at $P_a = 0.10$** → corresponds to $p \approx 0.10$.
- **Producer's Risk** $\alpha = 1 - 0.9856 = 0.0144$ at $p = 0.01$.
- **Consumer's Risk** $\beta = 0.1247$ at $p = 0.10$.
- **Operating Ratio** $\text{OR} = 0.10 / 0.015 \approx 6.67$.

## 3.6 Mermaid Block to be placed in Section 4

(Refer to Section 4 for the constructed schematic.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Construction Workflow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Sampling Plan] --> B[Identify n, c from plan]
    B --> C{Choose Approximation}
    C -->|n less than 50| D[Use Binomial Exact]
    C -->|n greater than 50, p less than 0.10| E[Use Poisson with lambda equals np]
    C -->|n greater than 50, p moderate| F[Use Normal with continuity correction]
    D --> G[Compute Pa for each p value]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Tabulate p versus Pa pairs]
    H --> I[Plot Pa on Y axis, p on X axis]
    I --> J[Smooth S shaped OC Curve]
    J --> K[Mark AQL at Pa equals 0.95]
    J --> L[Mark LTPD at Pa equals 0.10]
    J --> M[Compute alpha and beta values]
```

## 4.2 OC Curve Architecture (ASCII Visualisation)

```
   Pa
 1.0 |****
     |    ***
 0.95|- - - - * - - - - - -  <- AQL (Pa = 0.95)
     |        ***
 0.80|          ****
     |            *****
 0.50|                ********
     |                       *********
 0.10|- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - ***** - -  <- LTPD (Pa = 0.10)
     |                                     ******
 0.0 |________________________________*************__ p
     0   0.01  0.02  0.03  0.05  0.07   0.10   0.15
                  Fraction Defective (p)
```

## 4.3 Effect of Sample Size $n$ on OC Curve Shape

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Small n and high c] -->|Shallow slope| B[Poor Discrimination]
    C[Large n and low c] -->|Steep slope| D[Sharp Discrimination]
    E[Acceptance Number c increases] -->|Curve shifts right| F[More lots accepted]
    G[Sample Size n increases] -->|Curve becomes steeper| H[Better discrimination]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define an Operating Characteristic (OC) curve. What does it represent?**

**Model Answer:**
An Operating Characteristic (OC) curve is a graph of the probability of acceptance ($P_a$) of a lot versus the incoming lot fraction defective ($p$), for a given acceptance sampling plan ($n$, $c$). It represents the **discriminating ability** of the sampling plan — distinguishing between good lots and bad lots. A steep OC curve indicates a sharp, well-discriminating plan, while a flat curve indicates poor discrimination. It is the fundamental tool for evaluating producer's and consumer's risk.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between AQL and LTPD with reference to the OC curve.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | AQL (Acceptable Quality Level) | LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Maximum % defective considered acceptable as a process average | Worst % defective the consumer can tolerate |
| On OC curve | Point where $P_a$ is **high** (≈ 0.95) | Point where $P_a$ is **low** (≈ 0.10) |
| Risk associated | Producer's Risk ($\alpha$) | Consumer's Risk ($\beta$) |
| Typical value | 0.1% – 1% | 1% – 10% |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** What is an OC curve? Explain the significance of AQL, LTPD, Producer's Risk, and Consumer's Risk with the help of a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a single sampling plan with $n = 100$ and $c = 2$, construct the OC curve using the Poisson approximation for $p = 0.005, 0.010, 0.020, 0.030, 0.050$. From the curve, identify AQL and LTPD. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part B (a)

**Definition:** The OC curve plots the probability of acceptance ($P_a$) versus the fraction defective ($p$) of submitted lots.

**Key points:**

- **AQL** = the fraction defective at which $P_a \approx 0.95$ → relates to **Producer's Risk $\alpha = 0.05$**.
- **LTPD** = the fraction defective at which $P_a \approx 0.10$ → relates to **Consumer's Risk $\beta = 0.10$**.

**Sketch:** (Refer Section 4.2 for the S-curve diagram.) The diagram should clearly label the AQL point on the upper-left flat region and LTPD on the lower-right tail, with the operating ratio band in between.

### Model Solution — Part B (b)

**Given:** $n = 100$, $c = 2$. Use Poisson with $\lambda = np$.

| $p$ | $\lambda = np$ | $P_a = e^{-\lambda}\left[1 + \lambda + \dfrac{\lambda^2}{2}\right]$ | Computed $P_a$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.005 | 0.5 | $0.6065 \times 1.625$ | **0.9856** |
| 0.010 | 1.0 | $0.3679 \times 2.500$ | **0.9197** |
| 0.020 | 2.0 | $0.1353 \times 5.000$ | **0.6767** |
| 0.030 | 3.0 | $0.0498 \times 8.500$ | **0.4232** |
| 0.050 | 5.0 | $0.00674 \times 18.500$ | **0.1247** |

**From interpolation:**
- $P_a = 0.95$ occurs at $p \approx 0.008$ → **AQL ≈ 0.008 (0.8%)**
- $P_a = 0.10$ occurs at $p \approx 0.055$ → **LTPD ≈ 0.055 (5.5%)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the construction of an OC curve using the Binomial distribution. State the formula and assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a sampling plan with $n = 75$, $c = 1$, calculate $P_a$ for $p = 0.02$ and $p = 0.06$ using the Poisson approximation. Comment on the discriminating power. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part B (a) — Alternative

The OC curve is constructed from:
$$P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^{d}(1-p)^{n-d}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Lot is large (infinite population approximation).
2. Defectives occur independently.
3. Lot fraction defective $p$ remains constant.

For each value of $p$ (0, 0.01, 0.02, …, 0.10), $P_a$ is calculated using cumulative binomial tables and plotted.

### Model Solution — Part B (b) — Alternative

**Given:** $n = 75$, $c = 1$.

**For $p = 0.02$:** $\lambda = np = 1.5$

$$P_a = e^{-1.5}\left[1 + 1.5\right] = 0.2231 \times 2.5 = 0.5578$$

**For $p = 0.06$:** $\lambda = np = 4.5$

$$P_a = e^{-4.5}\left[1 + 4.5\right] = 0.0111 \times 5.5 = 0.0611$$

**Comment:** $P_a$ drops from 0.558 (at 2% defective) to 0.061 (at 6% defective) — a sharp decline indicating good discriminating power.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the continuity correction** ($+0.5$ term) when using the Normal approximation → loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing Type A (hypergeometric) and Type B (binomial)** definitions in the same answer → loses CO definition marks.
> 3. **Not labeling both axes and units** on the OC curve diagram → loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention Poisson validity conditions** ($p \le 0.10$, $np \le 10$) → loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Incorrect interpolation** for AQL/LTPD without showing the linear-interpolation calculation → loses 2 marks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ OC curve = Graph of $P_a$ vs $p$ for a fixed $(n, c)$ plan.
- ✅ Always state assumptions (infinite lot, Poisson validity conditions).
- ✅ Poisson formula: $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^{d}}{d!}$.
- ✅ Normal formula: $P_a = \Phi\!\left( \dfrac{c + 0.5 - np}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}} \right)$ (with continuity correction).
- ✅ AQL → $P_a \approx 0.95$; LTPD → $P_a \approx 0.10$.
- ✅ Producer's Risk $\alpha = 1 - P_a(\text{AQL})$; Consumer's Risk $\beta = P_a(\text{LTPD})$.
- ✅ Operating Ratio OR = LTPD / AQL (smaller OR → steeper curve → better plan).
- ✅ Increasing $n$ → steeper curve; increasing $c$ → curve shifts right.
- ✅ Binomial = exact; Poisson = approx. for large $n$, small $p$; Normal = approx. for moderate $p$.
- ✅ For KTU lab record: always tabulate $\ge 6$ values of $p$, plot the curve on graph paper with proper axis labels.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiment on acceptance sampling
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Acceptance Sampling – Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL507 - INDUSTRIAL INSPECTION AND QUALITY CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiment on acceptance sampling

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Acceptance Sampling** is a statistical quality control procedure in which a *random sample* is drawn from a *production lot (batch)*, the number of non-conforming (defective) items in the sample is counted, and a decision to **accept** or **reject** the entire lot is taken based on a pre-defined acceptance number `c` and the observed defect count `d`.

Mathematically, for a **Single Sampling Plan (SSP)** with lot size `N`, sample size `n`, and acceptance number `c`:

$$ d \le c \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{ACCEPT the lot} $$

$$ d > c \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{REJECT the lot} $$

The compact symbolic notation used in international standards (IS 2500, MIL-STD-105E, ISO 2859-1) is:

$$ \mathbf{N, \, n, \, c} \quad \text{(Lot size, Sample size, Acceptance number)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Acceptance sampling does **NOT** estimate lot quality — it only acts as a **decision-making filter** between supplier and consumer. It is a *middle-ground compromise* between **100% inspection** (costly, fatiguing, destructive impossible) and **no inspection** (dangerous).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Fruit Basket Inspection" Intuition

Imagine you are a supermarket manager receiving **1000 apples** (the **lot**) from a farmer.

- You cannot taste every apple (100% inspection is impractical).
- Instead, you randomly pick **50 apples** (`n = 50`) from different parts of the basket.
- You decide: *"If I find **at most 1 rotten apple** (`c = 1`) in my sample of 50, I'll accept the entire basket. Otherwise, I'll send it back."*

This is precisely **acceptance sampling**:
- The **farmer** = Producer (wants his lot accepted).
- The **manager** = Consumer (wants only good-quality lots).
- The **basket** = Lot under inspection.
- The **50 apples tasted** = Random sample.
- The **decision rule** = Sampling plan (n, c).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not inspect all 1000 apples?**
> 1. **Cost** – labour and time.
> 2. **Fatigue errors** – inspectors miss defects over long shifts.
> 3. **Destructive testing** – e.g., testing the *life* of a bulb by burning it.
> 4. **Bulk packaging** – items may be sealed in boxes.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU-Mandated Bold Terms)

The following metrics are **must-know KTU board definitions**:

- **Lot Size (N)** — Total number of items in the batch submitted for inspection.
- **Sample Size (n)** — Number of items randomly drawn from the lot.
- **Acceptance Number (c)** — Maximum number of defectives allowed in the sample for lot acceptance.
- **Probability of Acceptance (Pₐ)** — Likelihood that the lot will be accepted given a true fraction defective `p`.
- **Producer's Risk (α)** — Probability of rejecting a *good* lot.
- **Consumer's Risk (β)** — Probability of accepting a *bad* lot.
- **Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)** — Expected fraction defective in lots *after* rectification of rejected lots.
- **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)** — Maximum value of AOQ across all possible `p` values.
- **Average Sample Number (ASN)** — Expected number of items inspected per lot under a given plan.
- **Average Total Inspection (ATI)** — Expected total inspection effort per lot.

---

## 1.4 Types of Acceptance Sampling Plans

| Plan Type | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Sampling Plan (SSP)** | One sample drawn → one decision (Accept/Reject) | Most common in lab experiments |
| **Double Sampling Plan (DSP)** | First sample may be inconclusive → second sample drawn | Higher discrimination between good/bad lots |
| **Multiple Sampling Plan** | Several sequential samples may be drawn | Reduces ASN on average |
| **Rectifying Inspection Plan** | Rejected lots are 100% inspected and defectives replaced | Used for AOQ analysis |
| **Skip-Lot Plan** | Only some lots are inspected if supplier has good history | Used for trusted vendors |

---

## 1.5 Why This Experiment Appears in KTU PCPEL507 Lab

The KTU 2024 Scheme lab manual lists acceptance sampling as **Experiment 2** to demonstrate:

1. Drawing random samples from a known population with varying fraction defective.
2. Constructing the **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve** experimentally.
3. Verifying theoretical `Pₐ` values against observed acceptance frequencies.
4. Computing **AOQ, ATI, ASN** for both Single and Double sampling plans.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU lab viva, students are commonly asked:
> *"What is the difference between acceptance sampling and process control?"*
> **Answer:** Process control monitors *stability* of an ongoing process using control charts (X̄, R, p, c); acceptance sampling is a *final-product* lot-by-lot *decision-making* tool applied between producer and consumer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Single Sampling Plan (Step-by-Step)

1. **Lot presentation:** A lot of `N` items is received from the producer.
2. **Sample draw:** Using a random number table or lottery method, `n` items are drawn from `N`.
3. **Inspection:** Each item in the sample is tested for conformance. Defective count `d` is recorded.
4. **Decision rule:**
   - If `d ≤ c` → **Accept** the entire lot (it may contain some defectives that slipped through, but the risk is acceptable).
   - If `d > c` → **Reject** the entire lot. Rectification (100% screening) or return-to-supplier follows.
5. **Repeat:** Procedure is repeated lot-by-lot.

---

## 2.2 Derivation of Probability of Acceptance (Pₐ)

The number of defectives `d` in a sample of size `n`, drawn from a lot with true fraction defective `p`, follows the **Hypergeometric Distribution** (when sampling without replacement from a finite lot) or the **Binomial Distribution** (when sampling with replacement or N >> n).

### Case A — Binomial Approximation (N >> n, used in KTU problems)

$$ P_{a}(p) \;=\; \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} \, p^{\,d} \, (1-p)^{\,n-d} $$

### Case B — Poisson Approximation (when n is large and p is small, e.g., p ≤ 0.10)

$$ P_{a}(p) \;\approx\; \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^{d}}{d!} $$

### Case C — Hypergeometric (small lot, finite population)

$$ P_{a}(p) \;=\; \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{\binom{Np}{d}\binom{N-Np}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}} $$

where `Np = D` is the *expected* number of defectives in the lot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention:** For lab calculations and exam numericals, KTU typically allows the **Binomial** approximation unless explicitly stated. The Poisson approximation is permitted for sparse-defect cases. Use whichever is asked or simpler.

---

## 2.3 Producer's Risk (α) and Consumer's Risk (β)

Two quality points define a sampling plan:
- **Acceptable Quality Level (AQL)** — `p₁` : the worst quality the consumer is willing to accept routinely.
- **Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD)** — `p₂` : the worst quality the consumer will tolerate rarely (also called *Rejectable Quality Level, RQL*).

The plan must satisfy:

$$ P_{a}(p_{1}) \;\geq\; 1 - \alpha \quad \text{(Producer's protection)} $$

$$ P_{a}(p_{2}) \;\leq\; \beta \quad \text{(Consumer's protection)} $$

Common KTU defaults: **α = 0.05 (5%)**, **β = 0.10 (10%)**.

---

## 2.4 Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) and AOQL

For a **rectifying** single sampling plan, every rejected lot is 100% inspected and defectives replaced. The expected fraction defective *leaving* the inspection system is:

$$ \text{AOQ}(p) \;=\; \frac{P_{a}(p) \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} $$

**Rationale:** With probability `Pₐ`, the lot is accepted with `Np` defectives remaining (the `n` inspected items may or may not contain defectives — on average `np` were inspected, of which `np` were defective; the uninspected `N−n` items still contain approximately `p(N−n)` defectives). The `Pₐ·p·(N−n)/N` term represents the *average fraction defective* in the *uninspected* portion, which survives to the customer.

The **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)** is the maximum of `AOQ(p)` over all `p ∈ [0, 1]`:

$$ \text{AOQL} \;=\; \max_{0 \le p \le 1} \text{AOQ}(p) $$

---

## 2.5 Average Total Inspection (ATI)

$$ \text{ATI}(p) \;=\; n \;+\; (N - n)\bigl[1 - P_{a}(p)\bigr] $$

**Logic:** With probability `Pₐ`, only `n` items are inspected. With probability `1 − Pₐ` (rejection), the remaining `N − n` items are also inspected (rectification). The expected total is therefore `n + (N−n)(1−Pₐ)`.

---

## 2.6 Average Sample Number (ASN) — Double Sampling

For a **Double Sampling Plan** with first sample `n₁`, second sample `n₂`, and acceptance numbers `c₁, c₂` plus rejection numbers `r₁, r₂`:

- **If** `d₁ ≤ c₁` → Accept on first sample (ASN contribution = `n₁`).
- **If** `d₁ ≥ r₁` → Reject on first sample (ASN contribution = `n₁`).
- **If** `c₁ < d₁ < r₁` → Draw second sample of `n₂` (ASN contribution = `n₁ + n₂`).

$$ \text{ASN}(p) \;=\; n_{1} \;+\; n_{2} \cdot P_{\text{go to 2nd sample}} $$

where

$$ P_{\text{go to 2nd}} \;=\; \sum_{d=c_{1}+1}^{r_{1}-1} \binom{n_{1}}{d} p^{d}(1-p)^{n_{1}-d} $$

$$ \text{ATI}(p) \;=\; \text{ASN}(p) \;+\; \bigl[N - \text{ASN}(p)\bigr]\bigl[1 - P_{a}(p)\bigr] $$

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Markdown Table — No Pipe Symbols Inside Cells)

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Probability of Acceptance (Binomial) | $\displaystyle P_{a} = \sum_{d=0}^{c}\binom{n}{d}p^{d}(1-p)^{n-d}$ | Valid for $N \gg n$; used when $N \ge 10n$ |
| 2 | Probability of Acceptance (Poisson) | $\displaystyle P_{a} \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c}\dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^{d}}{d!}$ | Valid when $p \le 0.10$ and $n \ge 20$ |
| 3 | Probability of Acceptance (Hypergeometric) | $\displaystyle P_{a} = \sum_{d=0}^{c}\dfrac{\binom{Np}{d}\binom{N-Np}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}$ | Exact for finite lot |
| 4 | Producer's Risk | $\alpha = 1 - P_{a}(p_{1})$ | Probability of rejecting a good lot at AQL |
| 5 | Consumer's Risk | $\beta = P_{a}(p_{2})$ | Probability of accepting a bad lot at LTPD |
| 6 | Average Outgoing Quality | $\displaystyle \text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_{a}\cdot p\cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | After rectifying rejected lots |
| 7 | AOQL | $\text{AOQL} = \max_{p}\text{AOQ}(p)$ | Worst-case outgoing quality |
| 8 | Average Total Inspection | $\text{ATI} = n + (N-n)(1-P_{a})$ | Total inspection per lot |
| 9 | Average Sample Number (Double) | $\text{ASN} = n_{1} + n_{2}\sum_{d=c_{1}+1}^{r_{1}-1}\binom{n_{1}}{d}p^{d}(1-p)^{n_{1}-d}$ | Expected sample items drawn |
| 10 | LTPD relation to β | $P_{a}(p_{2}) = \beta$ | Defines consumer protection |
| 11 | AQL relation to α | $P_{a}(p_{1}) = 1 - \alpha$ | Defines producer protection |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Acceptance Sampling |
|---|---|
| **Electronics Manufacturing** | Incoming inspection of IC chips, PCBs, capacitors. |
| **Automotive Industry** | Lots of bolts, bearings, brake-pads received from tier-2 suppliers. |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Tablet blister packs — destructive sterility testing. |
| **Defence (MIL-STD-105E)** | Standard attribute sampling plans for military procurement. |
| **Textile Industry** | Bolt of fabric inspected for weave defects. |
| **Food & Beverages** | Shelf-life destructive testing of canned goods. |
