# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Worked Numerical — Single Sampling Plan OC Curve Construction

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A consumer receives lots of size `N = 2000`. A single sampling plan `(n = 50, c = 1)` is adopted. Compute the probability of acceptance `Pₐ` at fraction defectives `p = 0.01, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.10`. Sketch the OC curve and find the Producer's Risk at AQL = 0.02 and Consumer's Risk at LTPD = 0.08.

### Step 1 — Identify the distribution

Since `N = 2000` and `n = 50`, we have `N ≥ 10n`, so the **Binomial Approximation** is justified.

### Step 2 — General formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a}(p) &= \sum_{d=0}^{c}\binom{n}{d}\,p^{d}(1-p)^{n-d} \\
&= \binom{50}{0}p^{0}(1-p)^{50} \;+\; \binom{50}{1}p^{1}(1-p)^{49} \\
&= (1-p)^{50} \;+\; 50p(1-p)^{49}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate at `p = 0.01`

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a}(0.01) &= (1-0.01)^{50} + 50(0.01)(0.99)^{49} \\
&= (0.99)^{50} + 0.50\,(0.99)^{49}
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing the powers:
- `(0.99)^{49} = 0.6102` (approx)
- `(0.99)^{50} = 0.6050`

$$
P_{a}(0.01) = 0.6050 + 0.50 \times 0.6102 = 0.6050 + 0.3051 = \mathbf{0.9101}
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate at `p = 0.02`

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a}(0.02) &= (0.98)^{50} + 50(0.02)(0.98)^{49} \\
&= (0.98)^{50} + 1.00 \cdot (0.98)^{49}
\end{aligned}
$$

- `(0.98)^{49} = 0.3642`
- `(0.98)^{50} = 0.3642 \times 0.98 = 0.3569`

$$
P_{a}(0.02) = 0.3569 + 1.00 \times 0.3642 = \mathbf{0.7211}
$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate at `p = 0.04`

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a}(0.04) &= (0.96)^{50} + 50(0.04)(0.96)^{49} \\
&= (0.96)^{50} + 2.0\,(0.96)^{49}
\end{aligned}
$$

- `(0.96)^{49} = 0.1384`
- `(0.96)^{50} = 0.1329`

$$
P_{a}(0.04) = 0.1329 + 2.0 \times 0.1384 = 0.1329 + 0.2768 = \mathbf{0.4097}
$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate at `p = 0.06`

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a}(0.06) &= (0.94)^{50} + 50(0.06)(0.94)^{49} \\
&= (0.94)^{50} + 3.0\,(0.94)^{49}
\end{aligned}
$$

- `(0.94)^{49} = 0.0526`
- `(0.94)^{50} = 0.0495`

$$
P_{a}(0.06) = 0.0495 + 3.0 \times 0.0526 = 0.0495 + 0.1578 = \mathbf{0.2073}
$$

### Step 7 — Evaluate at `p = 0.08`

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a}(0.08) &= (0.92)^{50} + 50(0.08)(0.92)^{49} \\
&= (0.92)^{50} + 4.0\,(0.92)^{49}
\end{aligned}
$$

- `(0.92)^{49} = 0.01996`
- `(0.92)^{50} = 0.01837`

$$
P_{a}(0.08) = 0.01837 + 4.0 \times 0.01996 = 0.01837 + 0.07984 = \mathbf{0.0982}
$$

### Step 8 — Evaluate at `p = 0.10`

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a}(0.10) &= (0.90)^{50} + 50(0.10)(0.90)^{49} \\
&= (0.90)^{50} + 5.0\,(0.90)^{49}
\end{aligned}
$$

- `(0.90)^{49} = 0.00556`
- `(0.90)^{50} = 0.00515`

$$
P_{a}(0.10) = 0.00515 + 5.0 \times 0.00556 = 0.00515 + 0.0278 = \mathbf{0.0330}
$$

### Step 9 — Tabulated Result (Analytical Matrix)

| p (Fraction Defective) | Pₐ (Binomial) | Decision Interpretation |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0.01 | 0.9101 | 91% chance of acceptance — very good lot |
| 0.02 | 0.7211 | 72% chance — AQL region (Producer's Risk applies) |
| 0.04 | 0.4097 | 41% — Indifference zone |
| 0.06 | 0.2073 | 21% — Likely rejection |
| 0.08 | 0.0982 | 9.8% — Near LTPD (Consumer's Risk applies) |
| 0.10 | 0.0330 | 3.3% — Strongly rejected |

### Step 10 — Compute Producer's Risk at AQL = 0.02

$$
\alpha \;=\; 1 - P_{a}(0.02) \;=\; 1 - 0.7211 \;=\; \mathbf{0.2789 \;\;(\approx 27.9\%)}
$$

This means: *Even if the producer submits lots at the agreed AQL of 2%, there is a 27.9% chance the lot will be wrongly rejected.* A 5% producer's risk is desired — this plan is **lenient to the consumer**.

### Step 11 — Compute Consumer's Risk at LTPD = 0.08

$$
\beta \;=\; P_{a}(0.08) \;=\; \mathbf{0.0982 \;\;(\approx 9.8\%)}
$$

This means: *Even at 8% defectives (the LTPD), there is a 9.8% chance the bad lot slips through.* This is close to the desired 10% consumer's risk — the plan is **satisfactory for consumer protection**.

### Step 12 — AOQ Calculation at `p = 0.04`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AOQ}(0.04) &= \frac{P_{a}\cdot p\cdot(N-n)}{N} \\
&= \frac{0.4097 \times 0.04 \times (2000-50)}{2000} \\
&= \frac{0.4097 \times 0.04 \times 1950}{2000} \\
&= \frac{0.4097 \times 78}{2000} \\
&= \frac{31.96}{2000} \\
&= \mathbf{0.01598 \;\;(\approx 1.6\%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 13 — ATI Calculation at `p = 0.04`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ATI}(0.04) &= n + (N-n)\bigl[1 - P_{a}(p)\bigr] \\
&= 50 + 1950 \times (1 - 0.4097) \\
&= 50 + 1950 \times 0.5903 \\
&= 50 + 1151.1 \\
&= \mathbf{1201.1 \; \text{items/lot}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Double Sampling Plan

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A double sampling plan has `n₁ = 50, c₁ = 1, r₁ = 4, n₂ = 50, c₂ = 4`. Compute `ASN` and `Pₐ` at `p = 0.02`.

### Step 1 — Probability of acceptance on first sample

A first-sample lot is accepted if `d₁ ≤ 1`.

$$
P_{a1}(0.02) \;=\; (0.98)^{50} + 50(0.02)(0.98)^{49} \;=\; \mathbf{0.7211 \;\;(same\;as\;SSP\;above)}
$$

### Step 2 — Probability of rejection on first sample

A first-sample lot is rejected if `d₁ ≥ 4`.

$$
P_{r1}(0.02) \;=\; \sum_{d=4}^{50}\binom{50}{d}(0.02)^{d}(0.98)^{50-d}
$$

Using complementary probability and computing the small `d` terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{r1} &= 1 - P_{a1} - P_{\text{to 2nd sample}} \\
P_{\text{to 2nd}} &= P(d_{1}=2) + P(d_{1}=3)
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute:
- `P(d₁ = 2) = C(50,2)(0.02)²(0.98)^{48} = 1225 × 0.0004 × 0.3714 = 0.1820`
- `P(d₁ = 3) = C(50,3)(0.02)³(0.98)^{47} = 19600 × 0.000008 × 0.3790 = 0.0594`

$$
P_{\text{to 2nd}} = 0.1820 + 0.0594 = \mathbf{0.2414}
$$

### Step 3 — ASN

$$
\text{ASN}(0.02) = n_{1} + n_{2}\cdot P_{\text{to 2nd}} = 50 + 50 \times 0.2414 = \mathbf{62.07 \;items}
$$

**Key observation:** ASN = 62.07 is *greater* than `n = 50` (the single sampling plan sample), but for *some* `p` values, ASN of a DSP can be *less* than `n` — this is the chief advantage of double sampling.

---

## 3.3 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — All Formula Evaluations

| Step | Quantity | Value at p=0.02 | Value at p=0.04 | Value at p=0.08 |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Pₐ (SSP, n=50, c=1) | 0.7211 | 0.4097 | 0.0982 |
| 2 | α = 1 − Pₐ(p₁) at AQL=0.02 | 0.2789 | — | — |
| 3 | β = Pₐ(p₂) at LTPD=0.08 | — | — | 0.0982 |
| 4 | AOQ = Pₐ·p·(N−n)/N | 0.0281 | 0.01598 | 0.00383 |
| 5 | ATI = n + (N−n)(1−Pₐ) | 593.1 | 1201.1 | 1806.5 |
| 6 | ASN (DSP) | 62.07 | 73.10 | 54.20 |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Acceptance Sampling Experiment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Producer Submits Lot of N Items] --> B[Generate Random Numbers from Random Number Table]
    B --> C[Draw Sample of n Items from Lot]
    C --> D[Inspect Each Sample Item for Defects]
    D --> E[Count Defective Items: d]
    E --> F{Decision Rule: d ≤ c?}
    F -- Yes --> G[ACCEPT Lot — Ship to Customer]
    F -- No --> H[REJECT Lot — Rectify or Return]
    H --> I[100% Inspection of Rejected Lot]
    I --> J[Replace Defectives with Good Items]
    J --> K[Ship Rectified Lot]
    G --> L[Record Outcome: Accept/Reject]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Compute Pa, AOQ, ATI, ASN]
    M --> N[Plot OC Curve — Pa vs p]
    N --> O[Compare with Theoretical OC Curve]
    O --> P[Verify Plan Meets α and β Specifications]
```

---

## 4.2 OC Curve Block Schematic — Plan Discrimination Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Low p — Good Lot Region] -->|p = AQL| B1[Pa High ≈ 1 − α]
    B1 --> C1[Accept Almost Always]
    A2[High p — Bad Lot Region] -->|p = LTPD| B2[Pa Low ≈ β]
    B2 --> C2[Reject Almost Always]
    D[Indifference Zone] --> E[Pa between 0.10 and 0.90]
    E --> F[Plan Discrimination Region]

    A1 -.->|Producer's Risk α| B1
    A2 -.->|Consumer's Risk β| B2
```

---

## 4.3 Double Sampling Plan Decision Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Lot of N arrives] --> S1[Draw First Sample n1]
    S1 --> S2{d1 ≤ c1?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[ACCEPT Lot on First Sample]
    S2 -- No --> S4{d1 ≥ r1?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[REJECT Lot on First Sample]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Draw Second Sample n2]
    S6 --> S7[d1 + d2 ≤ c2?]
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[ACCEPT Lot on Combined Sample]
    S7 -- No --> S9[REJECT Lot on Combined Sample]
```

---

## 4.4 Relationship Map — Metrics Interconnection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Lot N, Sample n, Defect c] --> R2[Pa vs p Curve]
    R2 --> R3[Producer's Risk α]
    R2 --> R4[Consumer's Risk β]
    R2 --> R5[AOQ vs p Curve]
    R5 --> R6[AOQL Maximum]
    R2 --> R7[ATI vs p Curve]
    R1 --> R8[ASN for Double Sampling]
    R8 --> R9[ATI for Double Sampling]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Q:** Define *acceptance sampling*. State any **two advantages** and **two limitations** of acceptance sampling as compared to 100% inspection. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Acceptance sampling is a statistical quality control technique in which a random sample of size `n` is drawn from a lot of size `N`, and a decision to accept or reject the entire lot is taken based on the number of defectives `d` observed in the sample, compared against an acceptance number `c`.

**Advantages (1 Mark each, any two):**
1. **Reduced inspection cost** — Only `n` items are inspected instead of all `N` items.
2. **Handles destructive testing** — Suitable when testing destroys the item (e.g., life testing of bulbs, crash testing of vehicles).
3. **Reduces inspector fatigue** — Smaller inspection load leads to fewer errors.
4. **Economical for large lots** — Particularly effective when N is very large.

**Limitations (1 Mark total, any two combined):**
1. **Risk of accepting bad lots** and **rejecting good lots** — sample-based decisions are inherently probabilistic.
2. **Provides less information** about the lot compared to 100% inspection.
3. **Requires trained statisticians** for plan design and OC curve analysis.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]`

**Q:** Define the Operating Characteristic (OC) curve. What does the steepness of an OC curve indicate about the sampling plan? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
The Operating Characteristic (OC) curve of an acceptance sampling plan is a plot of the **probability of acceptance (Pₐ)** of a lot as a function of the **true fraction defective (p)** in the lot submitted for inspection. It shows how well the plan discriminates between good and bad lots.

**Steepness Interpretation (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Steeper OC curve** → The plan has **high discrimination power**. Even small increases in `p` cause a sharp drop in `Pₐ`. This is desirable for the consumer.
2. **Flatter OC curve** → The plan **poorly discriminates** between good and bad lots — it accepts many bad lots and rejects many good lots.
3. An **ideal OC curve** would be a vertical step-function at the AQL — accepting 100% below AQL and rejecting 100% above AQL — but this is impossible with a finite sample.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2]`

**A lot of size `N = 1000` is inspected using a single sampling plan with `n = 80, c = 2`.**

**(a)** Compute the probability of acceptance (Pₐ) at fraction defectives `p = 0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07`. Plot the OC curve. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) and Average Total Inspection (ATI) at `p = 0.05`. Also state the significance of AOQL. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Step 1:** Since `N = 1000` and `n = 80`, with `N ≥ 10n`, use **Binomial approximation**.

$$
P_{a}(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{2}\binom{80}{d}p^{d}(1-p)^{80-d}
$$

Expanding:

$$
P_{a}(p) = (1-p)^{80} + 80p(1-p)^{79} + \frac{80 \cdot 79}{2}p^{2}(1-p)^{78}
$$

Simplifying the coefficient:

$$
P_{a}(p) = (1-p)^{80} + 80p(1-p)^{79} + 3160\,p^{2}(1-p)^{78}
$$

**Step 2: Evaluate at p = 0.01:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a}(0.01) &= (0.99)^{80} + 80(0.01)(0.99)^{79} + 3160(0.01)^{2}(0.99)^{78} \\
&= 0.4475 + 0.3616 + 0.01447 \\
&= \mathbf{0.8236}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate at p = 0.03:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a}(0.03) &= (0.97)^{80} + 80(0.03)(0.97)^{79} + 3160(0.03)^{2}(0.97)^{78} \\
&= 0.0877 + 0.2171 + 0.2672 \\
&= \mathbf{0.5720}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate at p = 0.05:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a}(0.05) &= (0.95)^{80} + 80(0.05)(0.95)^{79} + 3160(0.05)^{2}(0.95)^{78} \\
&= 0.0171 + 0.1153 + 0.3868 \\
&= \mathbf{0.5192}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate at p = 0.07:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a}(0.07) &= (0.93)^{80} + 80(0.07)(0.93)^{79} + 3160(0.07)^{2}(0.93)^{78} \\
&= 0.00316 + 0.0303 + 0.2900 \\
&= \mathbf{0.3235}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Tabulated Results:**

| p | Pₐ |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0.01 | 0.8236 |
| 0.03 | 0.5720 |
| 0.05 | 0.5192 |
| 0.07 | 0.3235 |

**OC Curve:** A plot of Pₐ (y-axis) vs p (x-axis) shows a monotonically decreasing curve from ~0.82 at p=0.01 to ~0.32 at p=0.07. The curve is *moderately steep* indicating reasonable discrimination.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** N = 1000, n = 80, c = 2, p = 0.05, Pₐ = 0.5192.

**Step 1 — Compute AOQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AOQ}(0.05) &= \frac{P_{a} \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} \\
&= \frac{0.5192 \times 0.05 \times (1000 - 80)}{1000} \\
&= \frac{0.5192 \times 0.05 \times 920}{1000} \\
&= \frac{0.5192 \times 46}{1000} \\
&= \frac{23.883}{1000} \\
&= \mathbf{0.02388 \;(\approx 2.39\%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ATI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ATI}(0.05) &= n + (N - n)[1 - P_{a}] \\
&= 80 + 920 \times (1 - 0.5192) \\
&= 80 + 920 \times 0.4808 \\
&= 80 + 442.34 \\
&= \mathbf{522.34 \;\; \text{items per lot}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Significance of AOQL:**

The **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)** is the maximum value of AOQ over all possible values of `p ∈ [0, 1]`. Its significance is:

1. It represents the **worst-case** average quality that will reach the customer, even under rectification.
2. It is a **guarantee** to the consumer — irrespective of the incoming lot quality, the outgoing quality will never exceed the AOQL on average.
3. It is the key parameter used to **select** a sampling plan when the consumer specifies an outgoing quality requirement.

In this problem, AOQ at p = 0.05 is 2.39%. The maximum AOQ (AOQL) would occur at some intermediate `p` value (often near `c/n`) and would be slightly higher than 2.39% — typically around 2.5%–2.7% for this plan.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Producer's Risk (α)** and **Consumer's Risk (β)** with reference to AQL and LTPD. For a plan with `n = 100, c = 2`, compute α at AQL = 0.01 and β at LTPD = 0.06. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Double Sampling Plan**? For a DSP with `n₁ = 60, c₁ = 1, r₁ = 3, n₂ = 60, c₂ = 3`, compute the probability of acceptance at `p = 0.02`. Also compute the ASN. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 Marks):**

**Acceptable Quality Level (AQL):** AQL, denoted `p₁`, is the maximum fraction defective that a consumer considers *acceptable* as a process average. Lots at or below AQL should be accepted most of the time.

**Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD):** LTPD, denoted `p₂`, is the fraction defective that the consumer considers *the worst tolerable*. Lots at or above LTPD should be rejected most of the time.

**Producer's Risk (α):** The probability of **rejecting a good lot** of quality AQL. Formally:

$$
\alpha = 1 - P_{a}(p_{1}) = 1 - P_{a}(\text{AQL})
$$

It represents the *frustration* of a good producer whose acceptable lots are wrongly rejected. Standard value: α = 0.05 (5%).

**Consumer's Risk (β):** The probability of **accepting a bad lot** of quality LTPD. Formally:

$$
\beta = P_{a}(p_{2}) = P_{a}(\text{LTPD})
$$

It represents the *risk* to the consumer of receiving a poor lot. Standard value: β = 0.10 (10%).

**Numerical Computation (4 Marks):**

For `n = 100, c = 2`:

$$
P_{a}(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{2}\binom{100}{d}p^{d}(1-p)^{100-d} = (1-p)^{100} + 100p(1-p)^{99} + 4950\,p^{2}(1-p)^{98}
$$

**At AQL = 0.01:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a}(0.01) &= (0.99)^{100} + 100(0.01)(0.99)^{99} + 4950(0.01)^{2}(0.99)^{98} \\
&= 0.3660 + 0.3697 + 0.1849 \\
&= \mathbf{0.9206}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\alpha = 1 - P_{a}(0.01) = 1 - 0.9206 = \mathbf{0.0794 \;\;(\approx 7.94\%)}
$$

**At LTPD = 0.06:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a}(0.06) &= (0.94)^{100} + 100(0.06)(0.94)^{99} + 4950(0.06)^{2}(0.94)^{98} \\
&= 0.00217 + 0.01385 + 0.04377 \\
&= \mathbf{0.05979}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\beta = P_{a}(0.06) = \mathbf{0.0598 \;\;(\approx 5.98\%)}
$$

**Conclusion:** Producer's risk = 7.94% (slightly higher than ideal 5%). Consumer's risk = 5.98% (better than typical 10% requirement). The plan is **satisfactory for both parties**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Conceptual Explanation (2 Marks):**

A **Double Sampling Plan (DSP)** is a two-stage acceptance sampling procedure where a first sample of size `n₁` is drawn. Based on the number of defectives `d₁`:
- If `d₁ ≤ c₁` → **Accept** the lot (no second sample needed).
- If `d₁ ≥ r₁` (rejection number) → **Reject** the lot.
- If `c₁ < d₁ < r₁` → Draw a **second sample** of size `n₂` and combine defectives from both samples. If total `d₁ + d₂ ≤ c₂` → Accept; otherwise reject.

The advantage is **smaller average sample number (ASN)** for both very good and very bad lots.

**Numerical Computation (5 Marks):**

Given: `n₁ = 60, c₁ = 1, r₁ = 3, n₂ = 60, c₂ = 3`. p = 0.02.

**Step 1 — Probability of acceptance on first sample (`d₁ ≤ 1`):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{a1}(0.02) &= (0.98)^{60} + 60(0.02)(0.98)^{59} \\
&= 0.2976 + 0.3645 \\
&= \mathbf{0.6621}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability of going to second sample (`d₁ = 2`):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(d_{1}=2) &= \binom{60}{2}(0.02)^{2}(0.98)^{58} \\
&= 1770 \times 0.0004 \times 0.3102 \\
&= \mathbf{0.2196}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Conditional probability of acceptance on second sample:**

Given we proceed to second sample, the remaining defective count is `d₂`. We accept if `2 + d₂ ≤ 3`, i.e., `d₂ ≤ 1`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(d_{2} \le 1 \vert d_{1}=2) &= (0.98)^{60} + 60(0.02)(0.98)^{59} \\
&= 0.2976 + 0.3645 \\
&= 0.6621 \quad \text{(same as Step 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Probability of acceptance on second sample (overall):**

$$
P_{a2}(0.02) = P(d_{1}=2) \times P(d_{2} \le 1) = 0.2196 \times 0.6621 = \mathbf{0.1454}
$$

**Step 5 — Total probability of acceptance:**

$$
P_{a}(0.02) = P_{a1} + P_{a2} = 0.6621 + 0.1454 = \mathbf{0.8075}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute ASN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{to 2nd}} &= P(d_{1}=2) = 0.2196 \\
\text{ASN} &= n_{1} + n_{2} \cdot P_{\text{to 2nd}} \\
&= 60 + 60 \times 0.2196 \\
&= 60 + 13.18 \\
&= \mathbf{73.18 \; \text{items}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** For a single sampling plan with equivalent protection (e.g., n = 80, c = 2), the ASN of this DSP at p = 0.02 is **73.18**, which is *less* than 80, demonstrating the **inspection savings** of double sampling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the coefficient** `80 × 79 / 2 = 3160` for the `p²` term in the c = 2 case. Students often write just `p²` without the binomial coefficient — this is a **favourite 1-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Confusing AOQ with AOQL.** AOQ is the outgoing quality at a *specific* `p`; AOQL is the *maximum* AOQ over all `p`. Mixing these up costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Mistaking Producer's Risk formula.** Some students write `α = Pₐ(AQL)` — this is WRONG. The correct formula is `α = 1 − Pₐ(AQL)`.
> 4. **Ignoring the second sample acceptance** in DSP problems. The total `Pₐ` must include BOTH first-sample and second-sample acceptances.
> 5. **Using Hypergeometric** when Binomial is sufficient. KTU permits Binomial for N ≥ 10n. Choose the simpler distribution unless directed otherwise.
> 6. **Units and significant figures:** Express Pₐ to **4 decimal places**, AOQ as a percentage, and risks α, β as percentages or decimal fractions consistently.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Acceptance sampling is a **lot-by-lot decision tool**, NOT a process-control tool.
- ✅ The notation `(N, n, c)` fully defines a **single sampling plan**.
- ✅ Probability of acceptance uses **Binomial** (most common), **Poisson** (rare defects), or **Hypergeometric** (small finite lot).
- ✅ `Pₐ(p) = Σ C(n,d) p^d (1−p)^(n−d)` from `d=0` to `c` — the **central formula** of all acceptance sampling problems.
- ✅ **AQL** = good-lot quality, **LTPD** = bad-lot quality.
- ✅ Producer's Risk: `α = 1 − Pₐ(AQL)` — should be **≤ 5%**.
- ✅ Consumer's Risk: `β = Pₐ(LTPD)` — should be **≤ 10%**.
- ✅ OC curve plots `Pₐ` vs `p`; steeper curve → better discrimination.
- ✅ **AOQ** measures outgoing quality after rectification; **AOQL** is its worst-case value.
- ✅ **ATI** = total inspection effort per lot; **ASN** = expected sample items for multi-stage plans.
- ✅ Double sampling plans reduce ASN on average compared to single sampling.
- ✅ Always state which distribution (Binomial/Poisson/Hypergeometric) is being used and why.
- ✅ Tabulate values of `Pₐ`, `AOQ`, `ATI`, `ASN` versus `p` for full marks in lab record.
- ✅ Plot the OC curve with labelled axes: x-axis = p (fraction defective), y-axis = Pₐ (probability of acceptance).
- ✅ For KTU lab: write the **observation table** with random numbers used, defective count, and decision for each lot.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiments to measure process capability of a process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL507 - INDUSTRIAL INSPECTION AND QUALITY CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiments to measure process capability of a process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Process Capability** is a statistical measure of the inherent ability of a manufacturing or production process to produce output within the specified engineering tolerance limits (USL and LSL). It quantifies the relationship between the natural variability of a stable process ($\sigma$) and the design specification width, without considering how the process is centered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition for Board Exam:**
> *"Process capability is the measurable, inherent, and repeatable performance characteristic of a process under stable conditions, expressed numerically through indices such as $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$, which compare process spread ($6\sigma$) with the tolerance band $(USL - LSL)$."*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket and Water" Model

Imagine you are filling a **bucket** (which represents the **tolerance band** between the Lower Specification Limit — LSL and Upper Specification Limit — USL) with water from a **tap** that drips with slight randomness (representing **process variation**, $6\sigma$).

- If the **bucket is very large** (wide tolerance) and the **water drips narrowly** (small $6\sigma$), the process is **highly capable** — almost all water lands inside the bucket.
- If the **bucket is small** (tight tolerance) or the **water splashes widely** (large $6\sigma$), the process is **incapable** — water spills outside the bucket, meaning defects are produced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU lab examinations, students are expected to physically conduct an experiment (e.g., measuring pin diameter, shaft length, or a CNC-machined component) and statistically evaluate its capability against a given specification.

### Standard Process Capability Metrics (Must-Know for KTU)

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning | Use |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_p$ | Compares tolerance width to process spread | Assumes process is **centered** at the target |
| **Process Capability Index (off-center)** | $C_{pk}$ | Adjusts for process **centering** | Most commonly reported in industry |
| **Process Performance Index** | $P_p$ | Overall (long-term) capability using **standard deviation $s$** | Used for **entire batch** data |
| **Process Performance Index** | $P_{pk}$ | Adjusted overall capability | Used for **unstable or new** processes |
| **Taguchi Capability Index** | $C_m$ or $C_{pm}$ | Considers deviation from target $T$ | Penalizes off-target processes |
| **Six-Sigma Level** | $Z$ | Number of standard deviations from mean to nearest spec | $\geq 6$ for **World-Class** processes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Pre-Requisites Before Conducting the Experiment

A process capability study is **only valid** when the process is in a state of **statistical control**. Therefore, before computing capability indices, a **Control Chart** (X̄–R or X̄–S chart) MUST be plotted and verified to be in control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of KTU Valuation:** If a student computes $C_p$ or $C_{pk}$ without first establishing statistical control (no control chart step), marks are deducted. Always establish **stability** before **capability**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Framework

1. **Define the Quality Characteristic:** Select the parameter (e.g., outer diameter, surface roughness, weight, hardness).
2. **Establish Specifications:** Note the USL, LSL, and Nominal/Target value $T$.
3. **Collect Rational Subgroups:** Take $k$ samples (typically $k \geq 25$) each of subgroup size $n$ (usually $n = 4$ or $n = 5$).
4. **Plot X̄–R Chart:** Compute $\bar{X}_i$ and $R_i$ for each subgroup; verify all points lie within control limits.
5. **Compute Grand Statistics:**
   - Process Mean: $\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k}$
   - Average Range: $\bar{R} = \frac{\sum R_i}{k}$
6. **Estimate Process Standard Deviation:**
   $$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$
   where $d_2$ is a tabulated constant depending on subgroup size $n$.
7. **Compute Capability Indices:** $C_p$, $C_{pk}$ (and optionally $C_{pm}$).
8. **Interpret & Conclude:** Compare indices to industry benchmarks ($C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is "capable"; $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ is "excellent"; $C_{pk} < 1.00$ is "incapable").

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Boundary Condition / Note |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **$C_p$ Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}}$ | Valid only if process is **centered** at midpoint; assumes $\bar{\bar{X}} = T$ |
| **$C_{pk}$ Index** | $C_{pk} = \min \left[ \dfrac{USL - \bar{\bar{X}}}{3\hat{\sigma}}, \dfrac{\bar{\bar{X}} - LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}} \right]$ | Real-world index; accounts for **off-center** processes |
| **$C_{pk}$ Alternative** | $C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k)$ where $k = \dfrac{\vert T - \bar{\bar{X}} \vert}{(USL - LSL)/2}$ | $k$ is the **centering factor**, $0 \leq k \leq 1$ |
| **$C_{pm}$ (Taguchi)** | $C_{pm} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 + (\bar{\bar{X}} - T)^2}}$ | Penalizes **target deviation** |
| **$P_p$ (Overall)** | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | Uses sample std. dev. $s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}$ |
| **$P_{pk}$ (Overall)** | $P_{pk} = \min \left[ \dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3s}, \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3s} \right]$ | Used for **long-term** performance |
| **Process Standard Deviation** | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | $d_2$: $n=2 \Rightarrow 1.128$; $n=3 \Rightarrow 1.693$; $n=4 \Rightarrow 2.059$; $n=5 \Rightarrow 2.326$ |
| **Defect Rate (PPM)** | $PPM = 1{,}500{,}000 \times [1 - \Phi(3C_{pk})]$ | Quick estimate based on $C_{pk}$ |
| **Six-Sigma Metric** | $Z_{min} = 3C_{pk}$ | $Z \geq 6$ for Six-Sigma quality |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Process capability indices are the backbone of **Supplier Quality Assurance (SQA)**, **Automotive PPAP** (Production Part Approval Process), and **AS9100** audits. Every OEM (Bosch, Tata, Maruti) requires $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ before approving a new supplier.

### Subgroup Size vs. $d_2$ Constant — Quick Reference

| Subgroup Size $n$ | $d_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 2 | 1.128 | 0 | 3.267 |
| 3 | 1.693 | 0 | 2.575 |
| 4 | 2.059 | 0 | 2.282 |
| 5 | 2.326 | 0 | 2.114 |
| 6 | 2.534 | 0 | 2.004 |
| 7 | 2.704 | 0.076 | 1.924 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Process Deviation Estimator $\hat{\sigma}$

The **average range** $\bar{R}$ is related to the true standard deviation $\sigma$ through the **relative range** statistic. For a subgroup of size $n$ drawn from a normal distribution, the expected value of the range $R$ is:

$$E(R) = d_2 \cdot \sigma$$

Therefore, the **point estimator** of $\sigma$ is obtained by equating the expected range to the observed average range:

$$\bar{R} = d_2 \cdot \hat{\sigma} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$

> This is the most common estimator used in KTU lab experiments because it requires only the range (computable by hand) rather than full standard deviation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the $C_p$ Index

Let the process output $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Under the **empirical rule** for normal distributions, **99.73%** of all process output lies within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$, i.e., a spread of $6\sigma$.

The engineering tolerance band is $(USL - LSL)$.

Process capability compares these two widths:

$$C_p = \frac{\text{Allowable Spread}}{\text{Actual Process Spread}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

> **Interpretation:** $C_p \geq 1$ means the natural process variation fits inside the spec; $C_p < 1$ means the process is wider than the spec — guaranteed defect producer.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the $C_{pk}$ Index

$C_p$ assumes the process mean equals the **midpoint** of the spec: $\mu = \frac{USL + LSL}{2}$. In reality, processes drift.

Define two **one-sided capability** measures:

$$C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} \quad \text{(upper capability)}$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \quad \text{(lower capability)}$$

The **minimum** of these is reported, since the tighter side is the bottleneck for defects:

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$$

Alternatively, using the **centering factor** $k$:

$$k = \frac{\vert T - \mu \vert}{\frac{1}{2}(USL - LSL)}$$

$$C_{pk} = C_p(1 - k)$$

> If $k = 0$ (perfectly centered), $C_{pk} = C_p$. If $k = 1$ (process mean on a spec limit), $C_{pk} = 0$ — completely incapable.

---

### 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem Statement:**
The diameter of a shaft is specified as $25.00 \pm 0.05$ mm. A capability study uses $k = 25$ subgroups of size $n = 5$. The data yields:
- Grand mean: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 25.02$ mm
- Average range: $\bar{R} = 0.04$ mm
- Target: $T = 25.00$ mm

Compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $C_{pm}$, and the centering factor $k$. Comment on process capability.

---

**Step 1 — Identify Specification Limits**

$$USL = 25.00 + 0.05 = 25.05 \text{ mm}$$

$$LSL = 25.00 - 0.05 = 24.95 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Tolerance width} = USL - LSL = 0.10 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate $\hat{\sigma}$**

For $n = 5$, from the $d_2$ table: $d_2 = 2.326$.

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.04}{2.326}$$

$$\hat{\sigma} = 0.01720 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_p$**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{0.10}{6 \times 0.01720}$$

$$C_p = \frac{0.10}{0.10320} = 0.9690$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Centering Factor $k$**

$$k = \frac{\vert T - \bar{\bar{X}} \vert}{\frac{1}{2}(USL - LSL)} = \frac{\vert 25.00 - 25.02 \vert}{0.05} = \frac{0.02}{0.05} = 0.40$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_{pk}$**

$$C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k) = 0.9690 \times (1 - 0.40) = 0.9690 \times 0.60$$

$$C_{pk} = 0.5814$$

> *Cross-check using direct formula:*
> $$C_{pu} = \frac{25.05 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.01720} = \frac{0.03}{0.05160} = 0.5814$$
> $$C_{pl} = \frac{25.02 - 24.95}{0.05160} = \frac{0.07}{0.05160} = 1.3566$$
> $$C_{pk} = \min(0.5814,\ 1.3566) = 0.5814 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Compute $C_{pm}$ (Taguchi Index)**

$$C_{pm} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 + (\bar{\bar{X}} - T)^2}}$$

$$C_{pm} = \frac{0.10}{6\sqrt{(0.01720)^2 + (0.02)^2}} = \frac{0.10}{6\sqrt{0.0002958 + 0.0004}}$$

$$C_{pm} = \frac{0.10}{6\sqrt{0.0006958}} = \frac{0.10}{6 \times 0.02638}$$

$$C_{pm} = \frac{0.10}{0.1583} = 0.6317$$

**Step 7 — Final Interpretation Table**

| Index | Value | Industry Standard Verdict |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| $C_p$ | 0.969 | **Incapable** ($< 1.00$) |
| $C_{pk}$ | 0.581 | **Incapable** ($< 1.00$); produces ~46,000 PPM defects |
| $C_{pm}$ | 0.632 | **Incapable**; penalizes off-target shift |
| Centering | 0.40 | Process is **40% off-center**; requires adjustment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** With $C_{pk} = 0.58 < 1.00$, the process is **statistically incapable**. The required corrective actions are:
> 1. **Re-center** the machine (shift $\bar{\bar{X}}$ from 25.02 → 25.00).
> 2. **Reduce variability** (improve tooling, recalibrate, retrain operator).
> 3. **Widen specification** only if the design allows.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (Interpretation Reference)

| $C_{pk}$ Value | Process Condition | Action Required |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $C_{pk} \geq 2.00$ | **World-class / Six-Sigma** | Maintain; monitor sparingly |
| $1.67 \leq C_{pk} < 2.00$ | **Excellent (Five-Sigma)** | Continue standard SPC |
| $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ | **Capable (Four-Sigma)** | Acceptable for most production |
| $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ | **Marginally Capable** | Tight control needed; risk |
| $C_{pk} < 1.00$ | **Incapable** | **Stop production**; engineering review |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Process Capability Study Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Quality Characteristic and Specifications USL, LSL, T] --> B[Step 2: Collect k Subgroups of Size n]
    B --> C[Step 3: Calculate Subgroup Mean Xbar_i and Range R_i]
    C --> D[Step 4: Plot Xbar-R Control Chart and Verify Stability]
    D --> E{Process In Statistical Control?}
    E -- No --> F[Investigate Assignable Causes and Remove Them]
    F --> C
    E -- Yes --> G[Step 5: Compute Grand Mean Xdoublebar and Average Range Rbar]
    G --> H[Step 6: Estimate Sigma using Rbar divided by d2]
    H --> I[Step 7: Calculate Cp and Cpk Indices]
    I --> J[Step 8: Compare with Industry Standards]
    J --> K{Cpk Greater Than or Equal 1.33?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Process is CAPABLE - Accept Production]
    K -- No --> M[Process is INCAPABLE - Engineering Action Required]
    M --> N[Action 1: Re-center Process]
    M --> O[Action 2: Reduce Variability]
    M --> P[Action 3: Re-examine Specification]
```

### 4.2 Concept Map: Inputs, Computations, Outputs of a Capability Study

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inputs] --> B[Process Data X_i]
    A --> C[Specifications USL LSL T]
    B --> D[Compute Xdoublebar and Rbar]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Estimate Sigma]
    E --> F[Capability Calculations]
    F --> G[Cp]
    F --> H[Cpk]
    F --> I[Cpm]
    F --> J[Z Sigma]
    G --> K[Outputs Decision]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Verdict}
    L --> M[Capable Process]
    L --> N[Marginally Capable]
    L --> O[Incapable Process]
```

### 4.3 Tolerance Band vs. Process Spread (Visual Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[USL Upper Specification Limit] --> B[Tolerance Band Width = USL minus LSL]
    C[LSL Lower Specification Limit] --> B
    D[Process Mean mu] --> E[Natural Process Spread = 6 sigma]
    E --> F{Is 6 sigma less than USL minus LSL?}
    F -- Yes --> G[CP greater than 1 CAPABLE]
    F -- No --> H[CP less than 1 INCAPABLE]
    D --> I{Is mu at Target T?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Cpk equals Cp Centered]
    I -- No --> K[Cpk less than Cp Off-Center]
```

### 4.4 Distinguishing Short-Term vs. Long-Term Capability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Capability Study] --> B[Short-Term Within-Subgroup]
    A --> C[Long-Term Overall]
    B --> D[Uses Sigma estimated from Rbar divided by d2]
    B --> E[Reports Cp and Cpk]
    C --> F[Uses s Sample Standard Deviation]
    C --> G[Reports Pp and Ppk]
    D --> H[Reflects Machine Repeatability]
    F --> I[Reflects Machine plus Environment Drift]
    H --> J[Typically Cp greater than or equal Pp]
    I --> J
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define Process Capability and Process Capability Index $C_p$.**
`[KTU University Exam - Apr 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Process Capability** is the measurable, inherent ability of a stable process to produce output within the engineering tolerance limits, expressed as the ratio of tolerance width to process spread.

The **Process Capability Index $C_p$** is mathematically defined as:

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

where $USL$ and $LSL$ are upper and lower specification limits, and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the process. A $C_p \geq 1.00$ indicates the process spread fits within the tolerance; a $C_p \geq 1.33$ is generally required for production approval.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ with a suitable example.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | $C_p$ | $C_{pk}$ |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Definition** | Compares tolerance width to $6\sigma$ spread | Compares nearest spec limit to $3\sigma$ |
| **Centering** | Assumes process is centered at midpoint | Accounts for process **off-centering** |
| **Formula** | $\frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $\min\left[\frac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ |
| **Real-world** | Theoretical / ideal case | Always used in industry |

**Example:** A shaft of $\phi 20 \pm 0.05$ mm with $\mu = 20.03$, $\sigma = 0.015$:
- $C_p = \frac{0.10}{6 \times 0.015} = 1.11$
- $C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{0.02}{0.045}, \frac{0.08}{0.045}\right] = \min(0.44, 1.78) = 0.44$

> The process is **off-center**; $C_{pk}$ correctly flags it as incapable, while $C_p$ misleadingly suggests capability.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU Pattern):** Answer **any ONE** full question. Both sub-parts (a) and (b) are **compulsory** if attempted.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to conduct a process capability study in a manufacturing industry. List the formulas for $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and the $d_2$ estimator.
**(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

The **process capability study** is conducted using the following systematic procedure:

**Step 1 — Define the Quality Characteristic:** Identify the critical-to-quality (CTQ) parameter (e.g., bore diameter, surface finish, weight).

**Step 2 — Set Specifications:** Obtain the USL, LSL, and Target $T$ from the design engineer.

**Step 3 — Collect Rational Subgroups:** Take a minimum of **25 subgroups**, each of size $n = 4$ or $5$, ensuring measurements are taken in random sequence.

**Step 4 — Plot Control Charts:** Construct **X̄–R chart** to verify statistical control:
$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R};\quad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$
$$UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R};\quad LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$$
Remove assignable causes if any point falls outside limits; restart study.

**Step 5 — Compute Grand Statistics:**
$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k};\quad \bar{R} = \frac{\sum R_i}{k}$$

**Step 6 — Estimate Process $\sigma$ using $d_2$:**
$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$
For $n = 5$, $d_2 = 2.326$.

**Step 7 — Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$:**
$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}};\quad C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{USL-\bar{\bar{X}}}{3\hat{\sigma}},\ \frac{\bar{\bar{X}}-LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}}\right]$$

**Step 8 — Interpret:** Compare to industry standard $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (capable) or $\geq 1.67$ (excellent).

**(b)** The specification for the inner diameter of a bearing is $40.00 \pm 0.08$ mm. Twenty-five subgroups of size $n = 5$ were measured. The data yielded $\bar{\bar{X}} = 40.03$ mm and $\bar{R} = 0.045$ mm. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and the centering factor $k$. State whether the process is capable. (Use $d_2 = 2.326$ for $n = 5$.)
**(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$USL = 40.08$ mm, $LSL = 39.92$ mm, $T = 40.00$ mm, $\bar{\bar{X}} = 40.03$ mm, $\bar{R} = 0.045$ mm, $n = 5$, $d_2 = 2.326$.

**Step 1 — Estimate $\hat{\sigma}$:**

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.045}{2.326} = 0.01935 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{40.08 - 39.92}{6 \times 0.01935} = \frac{0.16}{0.1161} = 1.378$$

**Step 3 — Compute Centering Factor $k$:**

$$k = \frac{\vert T - \bar{\bar{X}} \vert}{(USL - LSL)/2} = \frac{\vert 40.00 - 40.03 \vert}{0.08} = \frac{0.03}{0.08} = 0.375$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{pk}$:**

$$C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k) = 1.378 \times (1 - 0.375) = 1.378 \times 0.625 = 0.861$$

> *Cross-check:*
> $C_{pu} = \frac{40.08 - 40.03}{3 \times 0.01935} = \frac{0.05}{0.05805} = 0.861$
> $C_{pl} = \frac{40.03 - 39.92}{0.05805} = \frac{0.11}{0.05805} = 1.895$
> $C_{pk} = \min(0.861, 1.895) = 0.861$ ✓

**Step 5 — Verdict:**
Since $C_{pk} = 0.861 < 1.00$, the process is **NOT CAPABLE** of meeting specifications consistently, even though the spread index $C_p = 1.378$ is acceptable. The process requires **re-centering** to shift the mean from 40.03 mm back to 40.00 mm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often report only $C_p = 1.378$ and conclude the process is "capable" — this is **wrong**, because $C_p$ ignores centering. Always report and discuss $C_{pk}$. Loss of up to **2 marks** if $C_{pk}$ analysis is missing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the difference between $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $C_{pm}$, and the Six-Sigma level $Z$. State the relationship between $Z$ and $C_{pk}$.
**(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The four indices measure different aspects of process performance:

**1. $C_p$ — Process Capability (Spread only):**
$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$
Assumes the process is **centered at the midpoint**. Considers only the width of the spec vs. width of the process distribution.

**2. $C_{pk}$ — Process Capability Index (Spread + Centering):**
$$C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$$
Accounts for **off-centering** of the process mean. Always $C_{pk} \leq C_p$.

**3. $C_{pm}$ — Taguchi Capability (Target-Oriented):**
$$C_{pm} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}$$
Penalizes any deviation of $\mu$ from the target $T$. Used when **target accuracy** matters (e.g., assembly fit).

**4. $Z$ — Six-Sigma Level:**
$$Z_{min} = 3 C_{pk}$$
$Z \geq 6$ corresponds to $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$ — World-Class / Six-Sigma quality with $\leq 3.4$ defects per million opportunities (DPMO).

**Relationship:**
$$\boxed{Z = 3 C_{pk} \quad \text{or} \quad C_{pk} = \frac{Z}{3}}$$

**Sketches (describe):**
- For $C_p$: symmetric bell curve inside a wide bucket → high $C_p$.
- For $C_{pk}$: bell curve shifted right inside the bucket → $C_{pk} < C_p$.
- For $C_{pm}$: penalizes any shift; tighter effective denominator.
- For $Z$: distance from process mean to nearest spec, in $\sigma$ units.

**(b)** A process has USL = 50.10 mm, LSL = 49.90 mm, Target = 50.00 mm. From 30 subgroups of size 4, the following were obtained: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50.02$ mm, $\bar{R} = 0.06$ mm. Compute the indices and the expected defect rate in PPM.
**(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $USL = 50.10$, $LSL = 49.90$, $T = 50.00$, $n = 4$, $d_2 = 2.059$ (for $n = 4$).

**Step 1 — Estimate $\hat{\sigma}$:**

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.06}{2.059} = 0.02914 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — $C_p$:**

$$C_p = \frac{50.10 - 49.90}{6 \times 0.02914} = \frac{0.20}{0.17484} = 1.144$$

**Step 3 — Centering factor $k$:**

$$k = \frac{\vert 50.00 - 50.02 \vert}{0.10} = 0.20$$

**Step 4 — $C_{pk}$:**

$$C_{pk} = 1.144 \times (1 - 0.20) = 1.144 \times 0.80 = 0.915$$

**Step 5 — $C_{pm}$:**

$$C_{pm} = \frac{0.20}{6\sqrt{(0.02914)^2 + (0.02)^2}} = \frac{0.20}{6\sqrt{0.000849 + 0.0004}}$$

$$C_{pm} = \frac{0.20}{6\sqrt{0.001249}} = \frac{0.20}{6 \times 0.03534} = \frac{0.20}{0.2120} = 0.943$$

**Step 6 — Six-Sigma Level:**

$$Z = 3 C_{pk} = 3 \times 0.915 = 2.745$$

**Step 7 — Expected Defect Rate:**

For a normal distribution, area beyond $\pm 2.745\sigma$ corresponds to:
$$P = 2 \times [1 - \Phi(2.745)]$$
From standard normal tables, $\Phi(2.745) = 0.9970$, so tail area $= 1 - 0.9970 = 0.0030$ (one side).
$$\text{Total out-of-spec fraction} = 2 \times 0.0030 = 0.0060$$
$$\text{Defect rate} = 0.0060 \times 10^6 = 6{,}000 \text{ PPM}$$

**Verdict:** The process is **incapable** ($C_{pk} < 1.00$) and produces approximately **6,000 defective parts per million**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Forgetting to convert to **PPM (parts per million)** when the question explicitly asks for defect rate. Also, students often confuse $d_2$ for $n = 4$ ($2.059$) with $n = 5$ ($2.326$) — this single error cascades into wrong $\sigma$, wrong $C_p$, and wrong $C_{pk}$. Loss of up to **3 marks** for using incorrect $d_2$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **Process Capability** = inherent ability of a stable process; measured via $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.
- ✅ **$C_p$** considers only the **spread**; assumes process is **centered** at midpoint.
- ✅ **$C_{pk}$** considers both **spread and centering**; always $C_{pk} \leq C_p$.
- ✅ **$\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$** — remember $d_2$ depends on subgroup size $n$.
- ✅ **Industry Benchmark:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (capable); $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ (excellent); $C_{pk} \geq 2.00$ (Six-Sigma).
- ✅ **Centering Factor $k$:** $0 \leq k \leq 1$. Use $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$.
- ✅ **Taguchi Index $C_{pm}$** penalizes deviation from target $T$.
- ✅ **$P_p$ and $P_{pk}$** use sample standard deviation $s$ (long-term performance).
- ✅ **Z = $3 C_{pk}$** is the Six-Sigma distance; defect rate can be obtained from standard normal tables.
- ✅ **Always establish statistical control (Control Chart) before computing capability** — otherwise indices are meaningless.
- ✅ **Typical subgroup size:** $n = 4$ or $n = 5$; minimum $k = 25$ subgroups.
- ✅ **Never** compute capability from fewer than 100 individual data points (i.e., $n \cdot k \geq 100$).
- ✅ **KTU Lab Tip:** In your record, always present the **table of subgroup statistics** (Subgroup No. | $\bar{X}_i$ | $R_i$) before plotting the control chart and computing capability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiments on work study and standard time calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL507 - INDUSTRIAL INSPECTION AND QUALITY CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiments on work study and standard time calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Work Study** is a systematic, scientific technique used to determine the most efficient method of performing a task and to establish the standard time required for a qualified worker to perform that task at a defined level of performance under normal working conditions. It is composed of two interlinked branches:

- **Method Study (MS):** The systematic recording, analysis, and critical examination of existing and proposed ways of doing work to develop safer, easier, and more economical methods.
- **Time Study (TS) / Work Measurement (WM):** The application of techniques designed to establish the time required for a qualified worker, working at a defined performance level, to carry out a specified task under specified conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Time (ST)** is the total time allowed for a qualified worker to perform a task at a **normal performance rating** plus an allowance for personal needs, rest, and contingencies. KTU examiners treat ST as the *output* of every work-study experiment in this lab.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for Freshers)

Imagine you are timing your friend solving 10 sudoku puzzles to determine the "average time per puzzle." But here's the catch — your friend is racing through them carelessly, while a calm expert would take longer and make fewer mistakes. Two complications arise:

1. **Speed varies** between people (some are faster, some slower).
2. **Each puzzle needs a buffer** for the solver to blink, sip water, or stretch.

In work study, we do the same thing in a factory:
- **Stopwatch timing** measures *observed time*.
- A **performance rating** converts the observed speed to "normal speed" (the standard worker's pace).
- **Allowances** are added as a buffer for personal needs, fatigue, and unavoidable delays.

The result — **Standard Time** — is the time a *trained, motivated worker* should take under *normal, real-world shop-floor conditions*. This is the basis for **production planning, incentive wages, cost estimation, and line balancing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bench Strength of KTU Questions:** In this lab, the examiner typically gives raw stopwatch readings (in minutes or seconds) and asks for **Normal Time, Standard Time, Number of Cycles Required**, or **Production Rate per Day**. Always show each step with units.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Normal Working Speed (BWS Standard):** **100 units** on the **British Standard Work Study (BWS)** rating scale.
- **Westinghouse Normal:** Rating factor **= 1.00** when worker performs exactly at standard pace.
- **Typical Allowances:** Personal = **5–7 %**, Rest (basic fatigue) = **10–12 %**, Contingency = **5 %** → Total ≈ **15–20 %** of Normal Time.
- **Standard Working Day (KTU Reference):** **8 hours = 480 minutes** per shift.
- **Number of Cycles (statistical rule):** For ±5 % accuracy at 95 % confidence, **≈ 20–30 cycles** are sufficient for repetitive short-cycle work.

---

### 1.4 Glossary of Mandatory Terms (KTU Exam-Ready)

| Term | Definition | Mathematical Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Observed Time | Time recorded directly by stopwatch | $T_{obs}$ |
| Performance Rating | Worker's speed relative to "normal" | $R$ (in %) |
| Normal Time | Time at standard performance rating | $T_n$ |
| Foreign Element | Non-cyclic, occasional activity | $T_{fe}$ |
| Allowance | Buffer added to normal time | $A$ (in %) |
| Standard Time | Final authorized time per piece | $T_s$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of Work Study (Bulleted Logic Flow)

#### A. Method Study — The "How" Question
1. **Select** the job/process to be studied.
2. **Record** all relevant facts using charts (Process, Flow, Two-handed, String, Cycle, Man-Machine, Multiple Activity).
3. **Examine** each element critically using the **ECRS Principle** (Eliminate, Combine, Rearrange, Simplify).
4. **Develop** the improved method.
5. **Install** the new method with worker training.
6. **Maintain** the new method through periodic audits.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks: *"List the symbols used in a Two-Handed Process Chart"* (Operation, Transport, Delay, Storage, Inspection).

#### B. Time Study — The "How Long" Question
1. **Define** the job and break it into **elements** (natural breaks > 0.03 min preferred).
2. **Select** the operator (qualified, trained, cooperative).
3. **Observe** and record stopwatch readings for each element.
4. **Rate** the operator's performance against the BWS or Westinghouse scale.
5. **Compute** average observed time per element.
6. **Apply rating** to convert observed time to normal time.
7. **Add allowances** to obtain standard time.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average Cycle Time | $T_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i}{n}$ | min | $n$ = no. of cycles |
| 2 | Basic Time | $T_{bt} = \dfrac{T_{total} - T_{fe}}{n}$ | min | Removes foreign elements |
| 3 | Normal Time | $T_n = T_{avg} \times \dfrac{R}{100}$ | min | $R$ in percent |
| 4 | Standard Time | $T_s = T_n + \text{Relaxation Allowance}$ | min | Relaxation = $T_n \times A$ |
| 5 | Standard Time (alt) | $T_s = T_n \times (1 + A)$ | min | $A$ = allowance fraction |
| 6 | Production Rate | $PR = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{T_s}$ | pcs/hr | Per machine or operator |
| 7 | Performance Rating | $R = \dfrac{\text{Observed Speed}}{\text{Normal Speed}} \times 100$ | % | BWS scale: 0–120 |
| 8 | Foreign Element Time | $T_{fe} = \text{Personal} + \text{Delays}$ | min | Excluded from basic time |
| 9 | Allowances | $A = A_p + A_r + A_c$ | % | Personal + Rest + Contingency |
| 10 | Cycle Time per Piece | $CT = \sum T_{element_i}$ | min | Sum of all elements |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equation 5 is the master equation of KTU Standard Time problems.** Memorise the form $T_s = T_n \times (1 + A)$ — examiners reward students who write it cleanly with units.

---

### 2.3 The Westinghouse Rating System (Four Sub-Factors)

The classical Westinghouse system rates the worker on **Skill, Effort, Conditions, Consistency** — each multiplied to give a combined rating factor:

$$
R_{combined} = (1 + S_k) \times (1 + E) \times (1 + C_c) \times (1 + C_i)
$$

Where:
- $S_k$ = Skill adjustment (range −0.15 to +0.10)
- $E$ = Effort adjustment (range −0.10 to +0.10)
- $C_c$ = Conditions adjustment (range −0.20 to +0.05)
- $C_i$ = Consistency adjustment (range −0.10 to +0.05)

The result $R_{combined}$ is then multiplied by the observed time to obtain Normal Time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern KTU labs and textbooks** favour the **BWS (0–120 scale)** system over the older Westinghouse system because it is simpler. Always check the question's wording before selecting the rating method.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Production Planning:** Determines how many machines/operators are needed to meet daily demand.
- **Incentive Wage Schemes (Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux):** Standard Time forms the baseline.
- **Cost Estimation & Quoting:** Labour cost per piece = $T_s \times \text{Wage Rate}$.
- **Line Balancing (Assembly Lines):** Determines cycle time for takt-time matching.
- **Lean Manufacturing:** Standard Time is used in VSM (Value Stream Mapping) for capacity analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Derivation — From Stopwatch Reading to Standard Time

We begin with raw stopwatch observations and derive the final standard time. Every algebraic step is justified below.

#### Given Data (Representative Lab Scenario):
- Number of cycles observed: $n = 10$
- Total recorded time (including 1 abnormal element): $\sum T_i = 38.20$ min
- Foreign element time during study: $T_{fe} = 2.40$ min
- Performance Rating (BWS scale): $R = 110$
- Total Allowance fraction: $A = 0.20$ (i.e., 20 %)

#### Step 1 — Compute the Average Observed Cycle Time

$$
T_{avg} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i}{n}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
T_{avg} = \frac{38.20 \text{ min}}{10} = 3.82 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Logic:** The arithmetic mean assumes the operator repeats the cycle with near-constant performance. This is the *unmodified* average.

---

#### Step 2 — Compute the Basic Time (Removing Foreign Elements)

Foreign elements are occasional interruptions (e.g., the operator dropped a tool, talked to supervisor). These are non-repetitive and must be excluded from the standard.

$$
T_{bt} = \frac{T_{total} - T_{fe}}{n}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{bt} = \frac{38.20 - 2.40}{10} = \frac{35.80}{10} = 3.58 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Logic:** Foreign elements inflate the average; removing them gives the *true* average cycle time for the normal work pattern.

---

#### Step 3 — Compute the Normal Time

The operator worked at 110 % of BWS standard (faster than normal). To convert to normal pace, we scale down by the rating factor:

$$
T_n = T_{avg} \times \frac{R}{100}
$$

Substituting (using $T_{bt}$ from Step 2 — in KTU they often use $T_{avg}$ directly if no foreign elements exist):

$$
T_n = 3.58 \times \frac{110}{100}
$$

$$
T_n = 3.58 \times 1.10 = 3.938 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Logic:** A worker faster than normal must be "normalized" so the standard time applies to a *standard* worker, not a *super* worker. This makes the standard fair and sustainable.

---

#### Step 4 — Compute the Standard Time (Adding Allowances)

Allowances cover rest, personal needs, and unavoidable contingencies. The KTU-recommended form is:

$$
T_s = T_n \times (1 + A)
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_s = 3.938 \times (1 + 0.20)
$$

$$
T_s = 3.938 \times 1.20
$$

$$
T_s = 4.7256 \approx 4.73 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Logic:** $T_s$ is the time a *standard worker* takes when working at *standard pace*, including realistic shop-floor pauses. This is the value that production planning and costing departments use.

---

#### Step 5 — Daily Production Rate (Bonus Application)

Assuming an **8-hour shift** (480 min) and **no machine downtime** (theoretical maximum):

$$
PR = \frac{\text{Available Time per Shift}}{T_s}
$$

$$
PR = \frac{480}{4.7256} \approx 101.57 \text{ pcs/shift}
$$

**Logic:** Standard Time directly converts to output capacity — a key deliverable for any work-study experiment in industry.

---

### 3.2 Consolidated Analytical Matrix (Step-By-Step Summary Table)

| Step | Quantity | Equation | Numeric Result | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average Observed Time | $T_{avg} = \sum T_i / n$ | $3.82$ | min |
| 2 | Basic Time (no foreign elements) | $T_{bt} = (T_{total} - T_{fe}) / n$ | $3.58$ | min |
| 3 | Normal Time | $T_n = T_{bt} \times R/100$ | $3.938$ | min |
| 4 | Standard Time | $T_s = T_n \times (1 + A)$ | $4.7256$ | min |
| 5 | Production Rate | $PR = 480 / T_s$ | $101.57$ | pcs/shift |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Always carry **3 decimal places** during intermediate steps, then **round to 2 decimal places** in the final answer. Examiners deduct ½ mark for premature rounding.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Multiple Operators (Statistical Reliability Check)

**Given:** Three operators each perform the same job 5 times. Times (in min): **Op A** = 4.2, 4.0, 4.3, 4.1, 4.2; **Op B** = 3.9, 4.0, 4.1, 3.8, 4.0; **Op C** = 4.5, 4.6, 4.4, 4.5, 4.6.

**Step A — Per-Operator Averages:**

$$
T_{avg,A} = \frac{4.2 + 4.0 + 4.3 + 4.1 + 4.2}{5} = \frac{20.8}{5} = 4.16 \text{ min}
$$

$$
T_{avg,B} = \frac{3.9 + 4.0 + 4.1 + 3.8 + 4.0}{5} = \frac{19.8}{5} = 3.96 \text{ min}
$$

$$
T_{avg,C} = \frac{4.5 + 4.6 + 4.4 + 4.5 + 4.6}{5} = \frac{22.6}{5} = 4.52 \text{ min}
$$

**Step B — Grand Mean (Combined Average):**

$$
T_{combined} = \frac{T_{avg,A} + T_{avg,B} + T_{avg,C}}{3}
$$

$$
T_{combined} = \frac{4.16 + 3.96 + 4.52}{3} = \frac{12.64}{3} = 4.213 \text{ min}
$$

**Step C — Normal Time (assume $R = 105$):**

$$
T_n = 4.213 \times \frac{105}{100} = 4.213 \times 1.05 = 4.424 \text{ min}
$$

**Step D — Standard Time (assume $A = 0.18$):**

$$
T_s = 4.424 \times 1.18 = 5.220 \text{ min}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Operator **B** is fastest (potentially above rating); **C** is slowest. The **grand mean** ensures no single operator biases the standard. This is the **fair-day's-work principle** in work measurement.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Work Study Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Identify the Job] --> node2[Define Objectives]
    node2 --> node3[Conduct Method Study]
    node3 --> node4[Select Improved Method]
    node4 --> node5[Conduct Time Study]
    node5 --> node6[Record Stopwatch Data]
    node6 --> node7[Compute Average Time]
    node7 --> node8[Rate Operator Performance]
    node8 --> node9[Normal Time]
    node9 --> node10[Add Allowances]
    node10 --> node11[Standard Time]
    node11 --> node12[Production Planning and Costing]
```

**Interpretation:** The flowchart maps the **chronological logic** the lab examiner expects in any work-study write-up: Method Study → Time Study → Standard Time → Application.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Method Study vs. Time Study

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    workStudy[Work Study] --> ms[Method Study]
    workStudy --> ts[Time Study]
    ms --> msA[Select]
    ms --> msB[Record]
    ms --> msC[Examine using ECRS]
    ms --> msD[Develop]
    ms --> msE[Install]
    ms --> msF[Maintain]
    ts --> tsA[Define Elements]
    ts --> tsB[Observe and Record]
    ts --> tsC[Rate Performance]
    ts --> tsD[Compute Normal Time]
    ts --> tsE[Add Allowances]
    ts --> tsF[Establish Standard Time]
    msF --> output1[Standard Method]
    tsF --> output2[Standard Time]
    output1 --> final[Improved Productivity]
    output2 --> final
```

**Interpretation:** Method Study answers *"How should it be done?"* and Time Study answers *"How long should it take?"* Both feed into the ultimate goal: **Improved Productivity**.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Rating System Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Choose Rating System] --> q1{Workforce Familiarity?}
    q1 -- Trained Operators --> bws[BWS 0 to 120 Scale]
    q1 -- Diverse Workforce --> ws[Westinghouse Four Factor System]
    bws --> calc1[NT equals OT times R by 100]
    ws --> calc2[NT equals OT times Skill times Effort times Conditions times Consistency]
    calc1 --> apply[Apply Allowance]
    calc2 --> apply
    apply --> done[Final Standard Time]
```

**Interpretation:** KTU allows either rating method, but the BWS scale is faster and is preferred in **modern lab experiments**. The Westinghouse system is still tested in theory questions.

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Standard Time Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[Stopwatch Observations] --> block1[Sum and Average]
    input2[Foreign Element Time] --> block1
    block1 --> block2[Basic Time Block]
    input3[Performance Rating R] --> block3[Multiplier Block]
    block2 --> block3
    block3 --> block4[Normal Time]
    input4[Allowance Factor A] --> block5[Multiplier Block]
    block4 --> block5
    block5 --> output[Standard Time]
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram represents the **mathematical pipeline** every KTU calculation follows. Each block corresponds to a discrete algebraic operation, making it easy to debug or verify a step-by-step answer sheet.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Conceptual Questions

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the term "Standard Time". Briefly explain any two allowances added during its calculation.**

**Model Answer:**
Standard Time is the time allowed to a qualified worker working at normal performance to complete a specified task under specified working conditions, including provision for personal needs, rest, and contingencies.

**Two Allowances:**
1. **Personal Allowance (5–7 %):** Time for the worker to attend to natural needs (drinking water, toilet, washing hands).
2. **Rest Allowance (10–12 %):** Time to recover from fatigue caused by physical or mental exertion.

*(Alternative: Contingency Allowance — covers minor unpredictable delays like tool adjustment, material positioning, supervisor briefings.)*

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between Method Study and Time Study.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Method Study | Time Study |
|---|---|---|
| Objective | Reduce unnecessary work, improve layout | Establish standard time |
| Question Answered | *"How should work be done?"* | *"How long should work take?"* |
| Tools | Process charts, flow diagrams | Stopwatch, rating scales |
| Output | Improved method | Standard time |

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) A time study was conducted on a drilling operation for 10 cycles. The total time recorded was 32.50 minutes, which included 2.5 minutes of foreign elements. The operator was rated at 105 % on the BWS scale. If the allowance factor is 18 %, determine:**
**(i) Average observed time, (ii) Normal Time, (iii) Standard Time per piece, (iv) Production rate per 8-hour shift. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $n = 10$ cycles
- $T_{total} = 32.50$ min
- $T_{fe} = 2.50$ min
- $R = 105$ %
- $A = 0.18$
- Available time = 480 min

**Step 1 — Basic Time (after removing foreign elements):**

$$
T_{bt} = \frac{32.50 - 2.50}{10} = \frac{30.00}{10} = 3.00 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Step 2 — Normal Time:**

$$
T_n = 3.00 \times \frac{105}{100} = 3.00 \times 1.05 = 3.15 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard Time:**

$$
T_s = 3.15 \times (1 + 0.18) = 3.15 \times 1.18 = 3.717 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Step 4 — Production Rate:**

$$
PR = \frac{480}{3.717} = 129.13 \approx 129 \text{ pcs/shift}
$$

---

**(b) Explain the steps involved in conducting a Method Study. Mention the ECRS principle. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Eight Steps of Method Study:**
1. **Select** the job or process to be studied.
2. **Record** all relevant facts using process charts (Operation, Transport, Inspection, Delay, Storage symbols).
3. **Examine** each element critically.
4. **Develop** an improved method.
5. **Define** the new method with workers and management.
6. **Install** the new method with proper training.
7. **Maintain** the new method through supervision.
8. **Develop** standard practice for future reference.

**ECRS Principle (Examined during Step 3):**
- **Eliminate** unnecessary work or operations.
- **Combine** multiple operations into one.
- **Rearrange** the sequence for better flow.
- **Simplify** the remaining operations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write only the *first 5* steps (forgetting "Maintain" and "Develop standard practice"). KTU examiners allocate **1 mark per correct step** — losing 2 steps means losing 2 marks.

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a) Explain the Westinghouse rating system in detail. List its four sub-factors. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
The Westinghouse system rates a worker's performance by evaluating four qualitative characteristics using predefined numerical tables. The combined rating is a **product** of the four adjustment factors.

**Four Sub-Factors:**

| Sub-Factor | Symbol | Range | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Skill | $S_k$ | $-0.15$ to $+0.10$ | Worker's proficiency and training |
| Effort | $E$ | $-0.10$ to $+0.10$ | Willingness and energy applied |
| Conditions | $C_c$ | $-0.20$ to $+0.05$ | Lighting, temperature, noise, ventilation |
| Consistency | $C_i$ | $-0.10$ to $+0.05$ | Repeatability of cycle times |

**Combined Rating Factor:**

$$
R_{combined} = (1 + S_k)(1 + E)(1 + C_c)(1 + C_i)
$$

**Application:**

$$
T_n = T_{avg} \times R_{combined}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** Skill = +0.05, Effort = +0.05, Conditions = 0, Consistency = +0.03 → $R = 1.05 \times 1.05 \times 1.00 \times 1.03 \approx 1.135$ (i.e., 113.5 %).

---

**(b) An operator produced the following cycle times (in seconds) for an assembly task: 18, 20, 19, 21, 19, 20, 18, 19, 20, 21. The operator was rated 95 %. The allowance is 15 %. Calculate Standard Time per piece and daily output. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $n = 10$ cycles
- $R = 95$ %
- $A = 0.15$
- Total time = $18+20+19+21+19+20+18+19+20+21 = 195$ sec

**Step 1 — Average Observed Time:**

$$
T_{avg} = \frac{195}{10} = 19.5 \text{ sec/cycle}
$$

**Step 2 — Normal Time:**

$$
T_n = 19.5 \times \frac{95}{100} = 19.5 \times 0.95 = 18.525 \text{ sec/cycle}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard Time:**

$$
T_s = 18.525 \times (1 + 0.15) = 18.525 \times 1.15 = 21.30375 \text{ sec/cycle}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily Output (8-hour shift):**

$$
\text{Available Time} = 8 \times 3600 = 28800 \text{ sec}
$$

$$
PR = \frac{28800}{21.30375} = 1351.85 \approx 1352 \text{ pcs/shift}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake:** Many students forget to convert 8 hours to seconds → error of 3600×. Always check units.
> 2. **Rating Direction:** A 95 % rating means *slower* than normal → Normal Time < Average Time. Some students *increase* instead of *decrease*. Lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Rounding Errors:** Round only at the *final* step. Mid-calculation rounding leads to cumulative errors up to 5 %.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Work Study = Method Study + Time Study.**
- ✅ **Standard Time** = time at *normal performance* + *allowances*.
- ✅ Master equation: $T_s = T_n \times (1 + A)$, where $T_n = T_{avg} \times R/100$.
- ✅ Always **subtract foreign elements** before computing basic time.
- ✅ **BWS scale:** Standard = 100, range 0–120. **Westinghouse:** Combined product of four factors.
- ✅ Typical allowances: Personal (5–7 %) + Rest (10–12 %) + Contingency (5 %) ≈ **15–25 %**.
- ✅ **Production Rate** = $\dfrac{\text{Available Time per Shift}}{T_s}$.
- ✅ For ±5 % accuracy at 95 % confidence, observe **20–30 cycles** for short-cycle work.
- ✅ Always carry **3 decimal places** in intermediate steps; round only the final answer.
- ✅ Use **ECRS** (Eliminate, Combine, Rearrange, Simplify) during method-study examination.
- ✅ **Fair-day's-work principle:** Grand mean across operators gives unbiased standard time.
